text_raw:
﻿Reflection on Track Options




Minerva University
CP192: Capstone Seminar
Prof. L. Odera
January 31, 2026












________________


Reflection on Track Options
Process Documentation
To ideate what kind of track to propose, I answered the provided questions below. 
Of the tracks that would fulfill the requirements of your major(s), which are the most attractive options? Why? As a double major in CS (Data Science and Statistics) and SS (Empirical Approaches to the Social Sciences), my capstone options are already narrower than those of single majors. Out of the available tracks, I find the Machine Learning-Based Data Science track the most attractive. Not only does this satisfy both my major requirements, but it also combines machine learning (ML) and statistical methods, which I am interested in and would love to learn more about, with subject matter relevant to the SS courses I have taken. Specifically, the track offers neuroimaging and natural language discourse analysis as potential research areas for applied ML, which directly relate to my completed coursework in SS152: Cognitive Neuroscience and SS156: Comparative Politics in Practice, respectively. I am also considering taking the Computational Neuroscience tutorial in my final year to count towards both of my major credits, which further complements this track. While I am still unsure whether these are the exact domains I wish to pursue long-term, I am open to working within them, particularly since my primary interest lies in the methodology rather than the specific application domain. 
That said, I do have some reservations about the field of neuroscience. Based on my experience in SS152, much of the existing knowledge appears to be distributed across multiple levels of analysis, with limited consensus on the underlying mechanisms of cognition. This makes me somewhat uncertain about committing to neuroimaging as a long-term research focus, even though I find the methods themselves compelling.
At present, I am taking CS156: Finding Patterns in Data with Machine Learning, where I plan to work with fMRI data to understand better how ML methods perform on high-dimensional inputs. This experience may clarify whether I enjoy working with data that requires extensive preprocessing or whether I would prefer settings that place more emphasis on model development, evaluation, and interpretation. If the latter turns out to be the case, I would be interested in proposing a capstone that applies similar methodological approaches to a different type of dataset while preserving the same analytical focus. 
Are there tracks you think would be popular with other people in your major(s) but that are not a good fit for you? Why? I believe the Software Development track will be popular among CS majors, as many of them are looking to pursue software engineering positions after graduation. A deployed product would add significant value as a portfolio piece or resume point for these students, and full-stack development will allow them to develop practical skills for engineering positions. This probably also applies to double majors in SS who are more methods-oriented with a special interest in applying them to SS fields, as the Software track seems to offer a lot of flexibility for meeting major graduation requirements and exploring different topics. However, this track is not a good fit for me, as my career goals lie in pursuing ML or applied statistics research as a PhD candidate and later working in industry R&D as an ML research scientist. Thus, the skills I want to develop through my capstone are more oriented towards research than development, and having an application as the deliverable would add less signal for grad school applications than a paper or well-documented ML pipeline would. 
For double majors in CS and SS who are more domain-oriented and use computer science technologies as a means for research, I think the Social Science Data Analysis track will also be popular, as it is more of an application of data science methods to answer social science questions. For the same reason, I believe the Satellite Data Analysis may also be a viable option, although it does not explicitly list SS as an accepted major. Given the political and economic nature of environmental and urban governance, I feel like the track may be a good fit for some SS majors as well, and leveraging satellite data is undoubtedly related to CS majors' work. However, I might not be a good fit for these tracks, even if they accepted SS majors, because the questions I am interested in do not align with the inferences we can make from satellite imagery or tabular data like the World Values Survey. As I am more interested in modeling uncertainty, structure, and decision-making in complex social systems than in extracting descriptive patterns from large observational datasets, these tracks, which are primarily focused on large-scale descriptive data analysis or remote sensing applications, do not align with my research goals. 
What is a track you wish existed for your major(s)? A good track is one that would be a good fit for at least 8 students per year, most years. When you think about proposing a new track, think about what other majors it could accommodate; notice that some existing tracks can match the requirements for students in a variety of majors–perhaps all majors! I wish there were a track explicitly focused on applied machine learning systems and deployment, where the core research question is not just model performance on a fixed dataset, but how machine learning models behave when embedded in real systems. Such a track would appeal to CS majors interested in ML research and systems, as well as CS-other double majors who want to study the interaction between models and data-generating processes in specific non-CS domains. Given that many students have an interest in ML (as evidenced by the high demand for CS156), it is reasonable to assume they are interested in it as a technology. Importantly, I have noticed that many students in the course come from non-data-science CS concentrations and are there to learn about practical applications of ML that they intend to translate into deployed products or tools. 
This kind of track would fill a gap between theory-heavy ML projects and purely development-oriented software tracks. It would reflect how much contemporary ML research, both in academia and industry R&D, now focuses on deployment, monitoring, and interaction with real users and environments, rather than static benchmark performance alone. I believe this would be a strong fit for a consistent cohort of students each year, particularly those aiming for ML-focused graduate study, research-oriented industry roles, or data engineering roles. If such a track existed, I would personally be torn between this and the Machine Learning-Based Data Science track. 
Are there any tracks you think could be improved in some way? For example, you might think of a modification to an existing track that would allow students in another major to do it (e.g., “if we added X as an option to the deliverable, this NS track might be very exciting to CS majors…”). As briefly mentioned above, I believe the Satellite Data Analysis track can accommodate SS majors if the scope of the research questions is broadened enough to allow more social science interpretations of environmental change or human activity. For example, while deforestation and urban expansion have an impact on ecology, which can be analyzed through an NS lens, satellite-derived measures of land use change, night-time light intensity, or infrastructure expansion can also be used to study human behavior, governance, and political-economic dynamics, especially in regions where traditional data sources are limited. Making this connection more explicit in the track description by foregrounding human activities as a research focus alongside environmental change could make the track more clearly accessible to SS majors. Framing satellite data as a lens on social processes, rather than primarily as an environmental monitoring tool, would allow SS students to pursue substantively social questions while still engaging deeply with the technical and inferential challenges unique to satellite observations.
Students can submit a custom capstone project proposal that does not fit a track. Do you think you will submit a custom (off-track) proposal this year? Why? What percent of students in your major do you think will do this? Why? What percent of students overall do you think will do this? Why? As of now, I doubt I will submit a custom proposal, as my interests align well with the Machine Learning-Based Data Science track. However, if I discover issues with working with neuroimaging data through my first CS156 assignment (due in February), I may propose a project with similar methods and deliverable formats but substantially different datasets. 
For students double-majoring in CS and SS like me, I estimate that up to 50% will submit custom proposals. While there are few of us to begin with, I have noticed that most of those I know are focused on economics, which does not have a specific track. Thus, I expect a majority of students with an SS concentration in economics to submit custom proposals, unless they choose to pursue the Software Development track or a similar track with a broader scope that will allow them to apply the economics LOs. 
In my cohort specifically, I would estimate up to 60% of students would be interested, mostly from non-CS majors. I believe this simply because there are more concentrations than there are tracks in each college, but the non-CS tracks have specific combinations of methods and content that may fail to capture student interest. For example, there are no tracks that might fit for AH students pursuing performing arts, non-writing, or non-digital media, or for NS students studying biology. 
Work Product: Track Proposal
As a more formalized version of the ‘track I wish existed’ outlined above, I propose the following track: 
Applied Machine Learning for Systems & Products 
Accepted Majors: CS, with allowance for double majors with another major, granted that the product aligns with it 
Description: The expected deliverable is a working ML-enabled system (e.g., a web application, software, or open-source tool), accompanied by a research-style technical report. Unlike traditional machine learning projects that primarily evaluate models offline on fixed datasets, projects in this track emphasize integration: data pipelines, model serving, monitoring, user interaction, and system design choices driven by uncertainty, latency, or failure modes. Students are expected to make and justify practical tradeoffs between model complexity, reliability, interpretability, and usability.  
In addition to the deployed system, students produce a technical report documenting the system architecture, model choices, evaluation in realistic conditions, and lessons learned from deployment. This track is intended for students interested in applied ML roles in industry, product-oriented ML research, or building ML-driven tools as part of their portfolio. 
This proposed track is distinct from both the Software Development and Machine Learning–Based Data Science tracks in its treatment of machine learning as an integrated system component and in its learning objectives. Unlike the Software Development track, where the primary goal is to build functional software and the choice of backend technology is incidental, mainly, this track requires machine learning to be a core dependency of the system rather than an interchangeable implementation detail. Projects are expected to be structured around challenges that arise specifically because ML models are imperfect, probabilistic, and context-dependent. These include, for example, uncertainty representation, model calibration, data drift, feedback loops between model outputs and downstream behavior, post-deployment monitoring and evaluation, and practical constraints introduced by real-world use. As a result, the central focus is not simply whether the system works, but how it behaves under uncertainty and over time.
At the same time, this track differs from the Machine Learning-Based Data Science track, which is centered on producing a research paper and evaluating models on static datasets. Rather than prioritizing novelty in modeling or analysis, this track focuses on integrating existing or moderately novel models into end-to-end systems and studying their behavior in realistic, operational settings. 
In addition, I believe that this type of product development is more viable as a track (versus students making custom proposals) because students can benefit from more frequent meetings that deviate from the traditional capstone schedule and align better with applied ML systems development. Specifically, because such systems exhibit non-static behavior and often reveal failure modes only after deployment or interaction, students can benefit from lightweight check-ins and milestone-based reviews with their advisors, tied to system state changes such as deployments, feedback loops, and drift detection. In addition to individual advising, students could meet for periodic group diagnostics sessions focused on analyzing ML system behavior and evaluation strategies. These deviations can support collaborative, system-level learning that cannot be effectively guided through infrequent meetings, as is the case in traditional capstone advising.
HC/LO Applications for the Work Product
#gapanalysis: I identified a gap in the existing track options, specifically how they did not offer one for students who wish to develop products powered by ML models. Given the popularity of CS156, I inferred that many students are interested in ML as a technology. Still, not all of them are research-oriented or interested in the subject matter covered in the Machine Learning-Based Data Science track. While a product-oriented take on ML may loosely fit into the Software Development track, I reason that having it as a track is beneficial to students because deployed ML models have different failure modes and pathologies from non-ML software, and consequently different development timelines that warrant more frequent meetings with advisors, in addition to more opportunities for peer learning. 
#audience: I was careful to pitch a track proposal that addresses a gap students might feel in the existing track options, and to frame it in terms of learning objectives and assessable outcomes, rather than student preferences or industry trends. I also describe why my proposal would be meaningful as a track rather than custom proposals (deployed ML systems have specific milestones and common pathologies that can benefit from more frequent check-ins, which deviate from the general capstone schedule), which is administratively relevant for faculty planning and running tracks. 
#qualitydeliverables: I ensured my proposal met and exceeded the level of detail outlined in the current track option draft. Specifically, I clearly described the expected deliverables, their requirements, and how my proposal fundamentally differs from existing adjacent options. I was also careful to present a scoped, realistic track, including logistics such as planned deviations from the capstone handbook, that can be readily adopted, provided a faculty member is available to lead it. 
#navigation: I strived to give detailed answers to the provided questions, and took it as an opportunity to honestly reflect on how the new track system fits into my own capstone and (planned) coursework. Through this process, I have concluded that I may be more interested in the Machine Learning-Based Data Science track than I initially thought. Still, I would like to gain more exposure to the types of data and workflows involved before committing fully to a capstone that heavily involves neuroimaging. In particular, I want to better understand how much of the work is driven by domain-specific preprocessing and interpretation versus by methodological model development and evaluation. Overall, this reflection has helped clarify the criteria that matter most to me in a capstone experience, namely methodological depth and opportunities to build skills that translate beyond a single research context, and will inform how I approach both my remaining coursework and eventual capstone proposal. 
AI Use 
I used Grammarly.com for sentence-level editing of the final document. No other AI tools were used.

﻿LBA: Analyzing the Bangle Market of Charminar




Minerva University
SS111: Modern Economic Thought 
Prof. A. Digby
February 7, 2026












________________


LBA: Analyzing the Bangle Market of Charminar
        Located in the “Old City” of Hyderabad, India, the Charminar is known for its bustling outdoor markets. This paper focuses on the market for bangles, a popular and affordable type of bracelet, and discusses how violations of the assumptions for perfect competition make it a monopolistically competitive market.
Assumptions of a Perfectly Competitive Market
High number of buyers and firms
        The crowded streets of Charminar indicate a high number of buyers, with foot traffic exceeding 1 million annually (CEIC Data, 2024). While the actual customer base might be smaller since bangles are predominantly worn by women, the number of buyers seems easily in the hundreds, if not thousands, based on how many people were at the stalls. As for the firms, I counted at least 50 merchants selling bangles on the main roads alone, reducing individual price-setting power. 
Low barriers to entry and exit
        Under India’s street-vending law, vendors must be assigned specific vending zones with regulated fees (Centre for Public Policy Research, n.d.). While this seems not to have been fully implemented in Charminar, street merchants face other, more informal fees, such as paying nearby shopkeepers to occupy the footpath in front of them, or “mamools” (protection money) to the local mafia (Farooqui, 2022). This could pose some barrier to entry, but the barrier to exit seems low, with no notable cost of leaving the market. 
Identical goods
        A notable feature of bangles is that they are meant to be worn in stacks of identical or similar bracelets; thus, they are usually sold in sets. Therefore, in addition to variations in the material and design, the bangles sold at Charminar can also differ in the quantity per stack. Stacks often come in metal, rhinestoned plastic, or glass, and the number of bracelets ranges from 5 to 25, creating differences in perceived value for customers and violating the condition of identical goods.  
Complete information
        It is plausible that the customer base, which is predominantly local women who frequently wear bangles, could reliably discern the quality of the goods, thereby reducing information asymmetry. In addition, the vendors are organized outdoors along streets, which allows buyers and sellers to clearly see competitive prices, remaining stock, and customer foot traffic. Thus, it can be inferred that both parties have good (but still imperfect) information, making it difficult for firms to overcharge customers or undercut their competitors. 
Firms as price takers
        Most bangles were priced at 50-150 Indian rupees, suggesting that firms have some price-setting power. However, given that production costs may vary by material and quantity per set, and most vendors are aware of competitor pricings, this variation may simply reflect procurement costs. 
Surplus Analysis
Under perfect competition, we expect maximum consumer and producer surplus with no deadweight loss (Figure 1, left). Firms will be price takers under infinitely elastic demand, producing at the equilibrium quantity   where price  equals marginal cost (Figure 1, right).  
Figure 1
Market Equilibrium and Welfare Under Perfect Competition (Left: market supply and demand; Right: firm-level equilibrium with P=MC=ATC)
  

However, the Charminar bangle market is monopolistically competitive. Thus, in the short run, the firms’ equilibrium mirrors that of a monopolistic market, where they produce at the quantity  where their marginal revenue is equal to marginal cost, leading to the price  (Figure 2, right). Taking the average cost of production at  as , this results in an economic profit of  , shaded in grey. Since , this results in deadweight loss (Figure 2, left). 
Figure 2
Short-run Welfare Loss Under Monopolistic Competition (Left: market supply and demand; Right: firm-level equilibrium with MC=MR)
  

The economic profit, combined with the low barrier to entry, attracts more firms, driving down demand until average revenue equals average cost and there is no longer economic profit. This leads to the new equilibrium price  (Figure 3, left). However, since   while average revenue sloped downward, deadweight loss persists, although it is smaller due to increased competition and lower markups (Figure 3, right). [1][2]




Figure 3
Short-run Welfare Loss Under Monopolistic Competition (Left: market supply and demand; Right: firm-level equilibrium with MC=MR and AR=AC)
  

        In conclusion, due to product differentiation and imperfect information, the Charminar bangle market functions as a monopolistically competitive market with deadweight losses. This results in reduced consumer welfare relative to perfect competition, as firms restrict output below the efficient level in order to sustain prices above marginal cost.[3] 


Word count: 697 words
AI Statement: No generative AI was used in this assignment. I used Grammarly.com for sentence-level editing.

[1]  #dataviz: The visualizations distinguish market-level welfare outcomes from firm-level pricing behavior across perfect competition and monopolistic competition. Market graphs are used to compare consumer and producer surplus, while firm graphs illustrate how price-setting, entry, and product differentiation lead to persistent deadweight loss despite zero economic profit in the long run. 
[2] #ss111-economicprinciples: Economic principles are applied to analyze the Charminar bangle market by comparing it to a perfectly competitive benchmark. Concepts such as price-taking, marginal cost, average cost, and entry/exit are used to explain firm behavior and inefficiencies, demonstrating how product differentiation and market structure shape prices, output, and consumer and producer welfare.
[3]  #ss111-welfare: The consumer and producer surplus are compared and visualized across perfect conpetition, the short-run equilibrium of monopolistic competition, and the long-run equilibrium of monopolistic competition. It is demonstrated that under monopolistic competition, the deadweight loss decreases as more firms enter the market, but never entirely disappears since the market enters an equilibrium when AR=ATC and there is no economic profit, but the demand curve still slopes downwards due to product differentiation.

name: Elevation Reflection & Engagement [final]
text_raw:
﻿Elevation Reflection & Engagement 
GL96
Suisei Nakagawa


One way I plan to engage meaningfully with Hyderabad is through its local climbing community. I began bouldering in Spring 2025 and have since climbed regularly in gyms in San Francisco and Buenos Aires. While those cities differ substantially in pace and social norms, I noticed a consistent pattern where climbing gyms tend to function as social spaces where people exchange advice, climb together informally, and build trust through shared physical problem-solving (and mate, in BA). That experience has made climbing one of the few activities where I feel comfortable entering a new city without relying solely on institutional or academic settings.


From the Hyderabad Cultural Guide and other Minervans, I learned that the city has an active climbing scene as well as a strong culture of outdoor recreation in green spaces. I plan to visit local gyms early in the rotation, not just to climb but to observe how people interact, train, and organize themselves socially, and hopefully make friends.


More broadly, I want to use climbing as an entry point rather than an endpoint. Conversations that start around training routines or routes often expand into discussions about work schedules, family expectations, and how people navigate city life. Engaging in this way will ground my understanding of Hyderabad in repeated, informal interactions and force me to pay attention to differences rather than projecting my prior experiences onto a new context.

﻿Ethnographic Assignment




Minerva University
GL96: Global Learning
January 9, 2025












________________


Ethnographic Assignment
Introduction
For this assignment, I spoke to Arjun Rao, a regular at my climbing gym here in Hyderabad. We first met when he gave me advice on a dynamic climb, and got to know each other as we kept seeing each other at the gym. Outside the gym, Arjun works as a consultant and is preparing to launch his own fintech startup. 
Climbing as a Shared Language
Being a long-term member at our gym, Crag Studio, Arjun describes climbing as a “shared ‘language’ that enables community across Hyderabad’s diversity.” Indeed, many of the members come from different states and use diverse languages, and while most speak English, allowing us to complain about the same “betas” or tell me how I really need to learn how to “smear” for better stability, it pales in comparison to the shared experience of bouldering in the same community. Climbing, according to Arjun, has a context-specificity that enables less verbal forms of communication, such as demonstrating a climb or gesturing to where a hold is, which can aid collective problem-solving in ways that English or Telugu alone could never. 
Arjun says this allows for an environment where background matters much less, since “no matter what state you’re from, which language you use, or what your job might be, the wall will treat you the same: you will, at some point, fall off.” In a diverse city like Hyderabad, the shared experience of trying the same problem over and over might just be what brings people together.  
Community Norms in Crag Studio, our Climbing Gym in Hyderabad
Like many other climbing gyms across the world (Arjun has been to European ones when he was there for work), Crag Studio has a very strong sense of community. He notes that weekly lessons create consistency, where people come to recognize one another, and non-students also come to climb fairly regularly to the point that newcomers are noticed immediately. He says this is why he talked to me in the first place: he saw some new faces and wanted to befriend me and the other Minervans. 
Notably, the trainers and route-setters are almost always there, even when they are not actively teaching or working, and they join the community in cheering people on and giving advice. I’ve been helped immeasurably by the tips the community gave me, and feel like openness, mutual support, and low hierarchy are core values of the gym, whereas in other spaces, expertise seems more gatekept, especially when people might pay for it. This also made me realize that the value of lessons here is quite different from what I have seen in gyms in other rotation cities: knowledge is freely imparted anyway, so by hiring a trainer, you are paying more for the individualized support than the advice itself.  
Conclusion
From the chat with Arjun and my own experience at Crag Studio, I came to realize that sense of community is not only sustained, but actively produced through social engagement and routine. Unlike other gyms I have been to, such as one in SF, where a large part of the crowd was just there to try out climbing rather than make it a part of their routine, I feel the continuity of shared knowledge and growth here in my gym in Hyderabad. 


Word Count: 550 words

Comparative Analysis of Political Systems: Furusao Nozei in Japan
Introduction
        In an effort to counter long-term regional inequality driven by population outflows toward major cities, Japan introduced the Hometown Tax Donation (HTD) program (“Furusato Nozei”) in 2008. The program allows taxpayers to donate to non-resident municipalities in exchange for income and residential tax deductions and locally sourced gifts (総務省 (Japanese Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications) [MIC], n.d.-a; MIC, n.d.-c). While framed as a mechanism to “give back” to one’s hometown, the program has become controversial: urban municipalities report tax leakage, while rural municipalities engage in escalating gift competition (OECD, 2016, p. 109; Fukasawa et al., 2020, pp. 1-2). 
Although the central government has repeatedly revised program guidelines, most notably by introducing gift value caps and marketing restrictions in 2017, regional disparities in tax revenue persist (Ogimoto, 2017; Fukasawa et al., 2020, pp. 1-2). This paper examines why the HTD program, originally designed as a redistributive, community-oriented mechanism, evolved into a competitive, donor-driven system with divergent fiscal trajectories. Specifically, it compares Japan’s pre-2008 municipal finance management with the current HTD system to explain how institutional design, ideological framing, and informal practices transformed fiscal management.[1] 
Origins and Ideological Foundations of “Furusato Nozei” 
The term furusato (“hometown”) carries a distinct emotional weight in Japan, referring not merely to a place of origin but to an imagined community defined by belonging, memory, and nostalgia (Robertson, 1988, pp. 503–504). Importantly, furusato is not a neutral cultural artifact but a politically mobilized symbol. In postwar Japan, it has been repeatedly deployed politically to construct affective ties between citizens and their territory (Robertson, 1988, pp. 504–506). Central to this is the cultural norm of “恩返し” and “義理” (“reciprocity” or “giving back”), which frames support for one’s hometown as a moral obligation rather than a discretionary act (Wierzbicka, 1997, pp. 367–368).
This reciprocity-inflected nostalgia is explicitly invoked to legitimize the HTD program, presenting tax-deductible donations as a way to support the “home of the heart,” regardless of actual residence (MIC, n.d.-a). By institutionalizing “giving back” through a fiscal mechanism, the program translates an affective moral narrative into routine policy practice. Notably, the original statutory design did not mandate return gifts; early iterations relied solely on tax deductions and attracted limited participation (Fukasawa et al., 2020, p. 2). In the absence of formal prohibitions, municipalities began offering local products as informal tokens of appreciation. As municipalities observed the effectiveness of gifts in attracting donations, this practice diffused rapidly. What began as a discretionary gesture hardened into a de facto rule, laying the foundation for gift escalation and later regulation.[2] 
The ideology of furusato also connects to regional pride and the commodification of local identity. Municipalities use high-value signature products with symbolic appeal, such as high-prestige regional beef, as gifts to increase their competitiveness within the donation marketplace (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development [OECD], 2016, p. 108; Yamamura et al., 2021, pp. 30–31; Rausch, 2021). This linkage between identity and product branding intensifies competition by tying fiscal success to reputational status.[3] 
Finally, official discourse, such as “応援したい自治体” (a municipality you want to support), “お世話になった自治体” (a municipality that supported you), “生まれ育ったふるさとに貢献できる” (contributing to the hometown that raised you), reinforces a reciprocal mental model in which donors perceive themselves as repaying a moral debt rather than engaging in a transaction, granting altruistic legitimacy to materialistic choices (MIC, n.d.-a; Yamamura et al., 2021, p. 16). Over time, reciprocity became embedded as an informal rule: gifts are normalized as appropriate tokens of appreciation rather than compensation, smoothing tensions between self-interest and prosocial identity and stabilizing competitive gift provision.
By emphasizing donor choice and the notion of “support-worthy” municipalities, official discourse reframes intergovernmental redistribution as a competitive process mediated by individual preference rather than bureaucratic allocation (MIC, n.d.-b). In this way, furusato functions as a cognitive infrastructure that normalizes inter-municipal competition while masking its market-like character.
Institutional Logic and Behavioral Structure of the Existing System
Although more than half of Japan’s tax base is collected nationally, many policies are implemented at prefectural and municipal levels (Rausch, 2017, p. 4). Prior to 2008, municipal revenues relied primarily on standard local taxation and intergovernmental transfers, producing relatively stable and predictable fiscal outcomes. The HTD system disrupted this equilibrium. By allowing taxpayers to reallocate part of their residential tax burden through voluntary donations, offset by tax credits and incentivized by gifts, municipal budgets became partially dependent on donor behavior (Rausch, 2017, p. 6).
This shift increased the importance of formal rules governing gift eligibility, marketing practices, and deduction caps, which now directly shape donor incentives and municipal revenue flows. More fundamentally, the HTD program represents a transition from hierarchical redistribution to a donor-mediated competitive allocation mechanism, structurally positioning municipalities as rivals seeking selection under centrally defined constraints. This institutional configuration can be systematically analyzed using the ADICO framework (Ostrom, 2005, pp. 139–152).
Table 1
ADICO analysis of the most recent communication issued to prefectural governors on the appropriate use of the HTD program (2025) 
Subject of regulation
	Applying ‘points’ to donations
	Total municipal budget for HTD program
	Procurement costs for gifts
	Content of gifts
	Attribute
	Municipalities
	Deontic
	Must not
	Must not
	Must not
	Must
	Aim
	Use platforms that award points to donors
	Spend more than 50% of collected donations 
	Spend more than 30% of collected donations 
	Ensure that products or services are locally sourced
	Condition
	When collecting donations
	When participating in the HTD program
	When procuring each gift type
	When providing gifts
	Or Else
	HTD recipient registration may be revoked
	Note. The summary communication to prefectural governors (MIC, 2025a) is used instead of the original statutory text to reflect the operational considerations municipalities face when participating in the HTD program. The full translated text is provided in Appendix C. “Points” refer to a standard Japanese reward system, comparable to cash back on credit cards or airline miles. 
        As shown in Table 1, the MIC’s communication (2025a) functions primarily as a choice rule by imposing hard caps on gift values and program expenditures. These constraints aimed to curb escalating gift competition that had imposed fiscal strain on municipalities. However, rather than eliminating competition, the rules redirected it toward marketing strategies, digital platform optimization, and non-gift forms of differentiation (Rausch, 2017, p. 15; 一般社団法人自治体DX推進協議会 (Local Government Digital Promotion Association) [GDX], 2025). 
Table 2
ADICO analysis of the guidelines for tax deduction for HTD donors (2025) 
Attribute
	Donors
	Deontic
	May
	Aim
	Claim deductions from their income and residential taxes up to the legal limit as follows, based on the tax rate specified for their income bracket:
1. ¥2000 less than the amount donated is considered deductible
2. From income tax, donors may deduct income-deduction × your income-tax rate (0–45%) up to 40% of gross income
3. From basic resident tax, donors may deduct 10% of the deductible
4. As a special deduction from resident tax, donors may deduct × (100% − 10% − income-tax rate) up to 20% of their resident-tax income fraction
	Condition
	When participating in the HTD program
	Or Else
	Deductions will not be applied
	Note. The publicly available guideline (MIC, n.d.-a) was used in place of the original regulatory laws to convey better what donors consider when participating in the HTD program. The full translated text of the page is provided in Appendix C. 
Table 2 highlights how donor-facing choice rules further intensify these dynamics. Because the ¥2000 reduction constitutes a fixed participation cost, higher-income donors, with higher marginal tax rates and larger deduction ceilings, face disproportionately strong incentives to maximize material benefits with minimal out-of-pocket expense (MIC, n.d.-a). This asymmetric incentive structure sustains demand for competitive gifts.[4] 
In response, municipalities engage in ongoing benchmarking and informal escalation, monitoring rivals’ offerings and adjusting strategies accordingly (Yamamura et al., 2021, pp. 41–42; Fukasawa et al., 2020, pp. 1–2). Because gifts are tied to regional branding, fiscal competition becomes entangled with local pride and reputation, intensifying pressures to remain competitive despite formal constraints (Rausch, 2017, p. 15; Rausch, 2021).
Administrative Structure and System Architecture
Before HTD, regional finance operated within a hierarchical fiscal architecture dominated by centralized collection and redistribution. Fiscal governance was predominantly vertical, enabling predictable revenue streams and stable budget planning (Figure 1, left). The HTD program fundamentally altered this structure. What has become a ¥1.3 trillion market (MIC, 2025b) introduced new actors, including donors, platforms, and vendors, transforming fiscal governance into a brokered, networked configuration mediated by donor choice and platform visibility (Figure 1, right).[5]
Municipal revenue flows became partially decoupled from the local tax base and intergovernmental transfers, increasing volatility and complicating budget planning (Rausch, 2017, p. 4). Although municipalities retain formal autonomy, their fiscal outcomes are increasingly shaped by private platforms that structure donor attention and choice.[6] 
________________


Figure 1
Network diagram of regional tax revenue pre- vs. post-implementation of the HTD program
  

Note. The MIC is a central ministry, thus it is represented in the National government node.
Rural municipalities, framed as deserving support under the program’s revitalization narrative, are incentivized to attract donations through gift competitiveness (Kato & Yanagihara, 2021, pp. 176–185). Urban municipalities, by contrast, function primarily as net contributors, experiencing persistent revenue outflows while lacking the symbolic furusato appeal that would allow them to compete effectively (Kato & Yanagihara, 2021, pp. 182–184; Robertson, 1988). 
The central government, particularly the MIC, also operates under altered incentives. In addition to overseeing the regulatory framework, the MIC must continually adjust program guidelines in response to escalating gift competition, manage increasingly strained relations with urban municipalities, and sustain the political narrative of regional revitalization despite mixed or uneven outcomes on the ground (Rausch, 2017, p. 6; Ogimoto, 2017; MIC, 2025a).[7] 
Private platforms, such as Rakuten and Yahoo, further amplify these dynamics by monetizing visibility through rankings and recommendations, thereby transforming attention into a scarce resource (Rausch, 2017, pp. 13–14; Nikkei Business Online, 2025). Vendors benefit from increased procurement volumes, reinforcing competitive escalation as higher donations translate directly into higher revenues (GDX, 2025).[8] 
At the bureaucratic level, municipalities must now manage public relations, vendor coordination, compliance monitoring, and procurement, often through newly created units or outsourcing arrangements (Rausch, 2017, p. 6; Fukasawa et al., 2020, p. 2). Variation in administrative capacity further differentiates municipal outcomes.[9] 
Causal Mechanisms Driving Divergent Outcomes
To understand the interactions between the factors analysed above, it is necessary to examine how they operate across multiple levels: the incentive structures shaping individual donor behavior, the escalation of municipal competition mediated by platforms and vendors within a state-designed competitive allocation framework, and the role of the national government, primarily the MIC, in constraining and adjusting these interactions.[10] 
As discussed above, the MIC introduced the HTD program to address regional decline by incentivizing municipal competition, which was expected to translate into regional development. The mobilization of furusato discourse successfully attracted donors; however, as municipalities operating under norms of reciprocity observed that gift provision increased donations, competitive dynamics intensified. In response, the MIC introduced regulatory constraints aimed at de-escalating what had become a negative-sum gift competition. Yet even with tighter gift restrictions, the HTD system continues to function as a marketplace in which donors “shop” for local specialties under a moralized framing of altruism.[11] 
A key component of Figure 2 is the municipal competition outlined in red: donor preferences, platform mediation, and vendor incentives interact to produce cumulative advantages for some municipalities and cumulative disadvantages for others. As certain municipalities attract more donations, they gain greater fiscal capacity to invest in branding, platform visibility, and procurement, increasing their likelihood of future selection. Conversely, municipalities that fail to attract donations face declining appeal and diminished capacity to compete.  
________________


Figure 2
Causal diagram demonstrating how the HTD program contributes to regional disparities in revenue and consequent capacity
  

Note. The red box highlights the core competitive mechanism in the HTD program.
A key standing condition underlying this dynamic is long-term rural decline. Population outflows reduced fiscal capacity in rural areas, elevating rural revitalization as a central policy objective and motivating the creation of the HTD program. The accompanying moral narrative of “supporting one’s hometown” legitimizes material-driven donor behavior, leading to competition. As municipalities “win” or “lose” within the HTD system, these outcomes reinforce demographic and fiscal divergence, as losing municipalities become less attractive to donors and more vulnerable to continued out-migration.
A second reinforcing mechanism operates through reciprocity norms. Furusato ideology enables donors to reconcile self-interest with altruism, sustaining participation and competitive escalation, which in turn perpetuates the perception that rural municipalities require exceptional support, thereby further legitimizing the system that generates inequality in the first place.
The HTD program also transformed fiscal governance from a hierarchical, centralized allocation system into a brokered, networked configuration. By redistributing allocative authority to donors, platforms, and vendors, the system amplifies platform-mediated competition and algorithmic visibility. Municipalities are therefore compelled to adapt to platform logics in order to remain competitive, thereby increasing the influence of private intermediaries over public fiscal outcomes.
In an effort to dampen these dynamics, the MIC introduced successive regulatory interventions governing gift values, procurement budgets, and marketing practices. These interventions serve as balancing loops, designed to counteract runaway competition. However, because they do not alter the underlying competitive allocation logic, they merely redirect competition into other domains, such as branding strategies and platform optimization, rather than eliminating it. As a result, the balancing loop remains maladaptive and fails to neutralize the reinforcing mechanisms.[12] 
        Together, these reinforcing and weak balancing loops generate path-dependent outcomes that trap municipalities in states of unequal attraction. Popular municipalities consolidate their advantage by reinvesting HTD revenue into visibility and competitiveness, while less successful municipalities lack the initial capacity to escape low-revenue equilibria. Urban municipalities similarly settle into persistent negative HTD balances due to tax leakage and limited furusato appeal. By contrast, under the pre-HTD fiscal regime, municipal revenues were comparatively stable and more closely aligned with demographic need through intergovernmental transfers.[13] 
Conclusion
The divergence between Japan’s pre-2008 municipal finance system and the current HTD regime reflects a fundamental shift in institutional logic. Hierarchical redistribution gave way to a donor-mediated competitive system in which fiscal capacity emerges from interaction among formal rules, informal norms, and platform dynamics. Once established, reinforcing feedback loops entrenched inequality, while regulatory interventions functioned as weak balancing mechanisms that redirected rather than dismantled competition. The HTD program thus represents a regime shift that structurally produces fiscal divergence rather than correcting it.

Appendix
Appendix A: AI Statement
I used Grammarly.com to proofread my final submission. I also used ChatGPT to receive advice on stylistic, formatting, and editing choices, in addition to translating material. See conversation here: https://chatgpt.com/share/694085f1-c3b0-8011-a153-f1ba0151af6d 
Appendix B: LO Applications 
Footnotes in the main text indicate the location of the main application of each LO. 
#constructivism
	I discuss how existing ideologies (the Japanese affective ties to furusato), cultural norms (on-gaesi/giri; reciprocity), and political narratives (“rural revitalization”) interact to form the ideological basis of the HTD program. I then discuss how this enables the HTD discursive framing that allows for mental models (donor altruistic self-perception) to sustain donor incentives for participating in the program. 
	#FormalRules
	I apply the ADICO syntax analysis to the regulatory rule that governs municipality participation in the HTD program and the tax deduction system applied to participating donors, and classify both as choice rules that modify agent behavior. For the municipalities, I discuss how the aim intended to curb the escalating gift competition via hard caps was unable to constrain it fully and instead redirected competition to other facets, such as branding. For the donors, I discuss how the aim constitutes a fixed participation cost that functions as an incentive for claiming up to the legal limit of deductions. 
	#InformalRules
	I examine how gift-giving emerged as an informal practice rooted in cultural norms of reciprocity and was subsequently adopted across municipalities. I then show how this de facto rule intensified inter-municipal competition, prompting regulatory intervention by the MIC, which in turn inadvertently institutionalized the practice.
	#RulesAndOutcomes
	After identifying the formal rules (the institutional design of the HTD) and informal rules (the original gift-giving and competition), I demonstrate how they jointly produce fiscal inequality. Specifically, I identified how the original design of the program was intended to encourage competition through regional development; however, the introduction of gifting practices shifted the focus to attracting donors via material incentives, which were subsequently regulated by MIC (see #FormalRules entry). 
	#GovernanceNetworks
	I discuss how the introduction of the HTD system introduced new actors into a previously centralized and hierarchical system, transforming it into a more brokered system mediated by donor behavior and platform dynamics. I also discuss how this brokered nature made revenue flow more sensitive to donor, platform, and vendor actions, and how it led to strategic behavior from municipalities. 
	#BureaucracyOperations
	I analyze how HTD altered the internal operating routines of municipal bureaucracies by adding administrative overhead, forcing them to divert resources from core public service delivery toward compliance, procurement, marketing, and platform coordination, thereby reinforcing the program’s negative-sum dynamics. 
	#GovtAgentsAgency
	I discuss how government entities (municipalities and the MIC) (do not) exercise agency within the HTD framework. For municipalities, I discuss how rural municipalities act strategically within the formal regulations on gift-giving to compete for resources. In contrast, urban municipalities are constrained by their lack of nostalgic furusato appeal. For the MIC, I examine how the HTD program creates tension between regulating the market and sustaining the political narrative that the HTD is beneficial to rural revitalization, thereby preventing it from fundamentally altering the program's nature. 
	#NongovtAgentsAgency
	I examine how donors, vendors, and platforms exercise newly found agency in tax allocation under the HTD system. Donors exercise agency by choosing municipalities to support based on material incentives, often under the guise of moral altruism, and vendors and platforms monetize municipal competition through an incentive created by the proportional program budget cap.  
	#emergentproperties
	I identify how regional disparities in revenue collection, and subsequently, service provision and general capacity, follow from the competitive market dynamics of the HTD program. I demonstrate how multiple factors (pre-existing inequalities, migration, ideologies and political narratives, material incentives, informal gift-giving practices, formal regulations) across different levels (individual donor level, municipal/platform/vendor level, national level) interact nonlinearly to produce regional disparity. 
	#complexcausality
	To identify the causal mechanisms underlying my emergent property (see entry above), I employ a causal diagram that distinguishes between necessary and sufficient causes, negative and positive influences, and reinforcing and balancing feedback loops. The diagram helps identify four key mechanisms: regulation (maladaptive balancing loop), evolution towards a more brokered and decentralized fiscal management network, moral discourse, and rural decline (reinforcing loops). 
	#levelsofanalysis
	I considered how the factors contributing to the emergent property (see entries above) interact across different levels. At the individual level, I examine the incentive structure for donors created by the program’s institutional design and moral framing. At the municipal/platform/vendor level, I consider the market structure and the network structure of fiscal governance. At the national level, I consider political usage of narratives to justify the HTD program. 
	#systemmapping
	I deconstructed Japan's municipal fiscal management and provided an accompanying diagram that outlined relevant agents and the flow of funds, services, and influence. This allowed me to identify how the introduction of new agents modified the function of existing actors. 
	#systemdynamics
	I discussed how the HTD system is path-dependent and produces ‘winners’ and ‘losers,’ which serve as attractor states: popular municipalities channel revenue into renewed competitive efforts, less popular municipalities struggle to gain equal footing on platforms, making it difficult to gain traction, and urban municipalities tend to remain at a net loss due to tax leakage. This reinforcing structure amplifies preexisting disparities and prevents movement in the phase space, making the states ‘sticky.’ 
	

Appendix C: Formal Rules used in ADICO Syntax Analysis
Excerpt from the most recent communication issued to prefectural governors on the appropriate use of the HTD program (MIC, 2025a) regarding regulation of budget use
2. Recruitment Appropriateness Standards
(1) Prohibition on Recruitment via Entities That Grant Points
As of June 28, Reiwa 6, notifications and Q&A were revised to prohibit recruitment through entities that grant points or similar benefits to donors in connection with donations (excluding points equivalent to those normally granted through standard commercial payment transactions). This includes cases where points are granted via third parties.
These revisions apply from designated periods starting on or after October 1, Reiwa 7. Therefore, conducting recruitment through point-granting entities on or after October 1, Reiwa 7 may result in designation cancellation.
Local governments are requested to take appropriate measures, such as regularly confirming that contracted recruitment service providers are not granting points.
(2) Total Recruitment Cost Must Not Exceed 50%
If, during a designated period, the total cost incurred to recruit Category 1 donations exceeds 50% of the total amount of Category 1 donations received, the local government may be subject to designation cancellation of its designation.
Accordingly, local governments must not leave management entirely to contractors, but must themselves regularly confirm recruitment expenditures and donation receipts throughout the designated period.
Additionally, an audit of recruitment costs and donation receipts for the Reiwa 6 designated period is planned. If excess costs are identified, designation cancellation may result.
3. Return Gift Ratio: 30% or Less
For each individual return gift, procurement costs must not exceed 30% of the amount of the corresponding Category 1 donation, as stipulated in the Local Tax Act.
This requirement applies regardless of the reason for cost increases, including price inflation. Violations constitute grounds for designation cancellation.
Local governments must therefore adjust quantities or required donation amounts in response to cost changes and ensure strict management.
Furthermore, expenditures labeled as incentives or subsidies to gift providers, or as promotional expenses, must be included in procurement costs if they effectively enable procurement of larger quantities of return gifts. 
4. Local Product Standards
Return gifts must consist of goods produced or services provided within the relevant local jurisdiction and must meet the standards established by the Minister of Internal Affairs and Communications.
Even after the start of the designated period, if a return gift is found to violate local product standards, designation cancellation may occur.
Local governments must ensure that compliance is clearly demonstrable—for example, by specifying weight or value-added ratios for qualifying products, or by detailing production processes and value added within the jurisdiction on portal sites. 
5. Designation Cancellation
If a local government is found to be non-compliant with designation standards, designation will be canceled unless there are exceptional circumstances, such as minimal donation impact or non-attributable causes.
Even if non-compliance arises from contractors, responsibility remains with the local government.
A local government whose designation is canceled may not reapply for designation for two years from the cancellation date.
