﻿Tab 1






Analyze the Effect of Rules: Nuclear Energy Management in Japan




Minerva University
SS156: Comparative Politics in Practice
Prof. L. Odera
Oct 6, 2025


Word count: 1497 words












________________


Analyze the Effect of Rules: Nuclear Energy Management in Japan
The meltdown of the Fukushima nuclear reactor in Japan following the 2011 earthquake and tsunami reignited debate on nuclear energy governance, raising questions about regulatory oversight and safety culture. Japan’s nuclear sector has been governed by a collusive network of state, corporate, and regional government interests, pejoratively dubbed “genshi-ryoku mura” (“nuclear village”) (Kingston, 2012). Here we focus on pre-disaster local nuclear governance in Futaba Town, which hosted the Fukushima Daiichi plant operated by Tokyo Electric Power Company (TEPCO). 
 Nonlinear interaction between formal and informal rules, institutional and corporate interests, and mental models such as the anzen shinwa (“safety myth”) produced systemic social patterns, most notably the erosion of plant safety culture, which contributed to the resulting poor disaster response (Kingston, 2012). Systemic issues, including regulatory capture and the dominance of the nuclear village, led to inadequate emergency protocols and poor overall preparedness, which amplified the crisis (Funabashi, 2021). This paper examines the actors, the rules governing them, and the mental models and ideologies that entrenched the nuclear village as the dominant institutional network, then applies a complexity approach to trace how these elements interacted to produce a self-reinforcing system resistant to adaptive learning. 
Action Situation
Table 1 describes the political situation above using the action situation framework proposed by Ostrom (2005, pp. 32–53), with national-level actors, such as the Ministry of Economy, Trade, and Industry (METI), included under the category of “central government.”
Table 1
Summary of Futaba Town’s Pre-2011 Nuclear Governance Action Situation
Participant (Position)
	Actions
	Potential Outcomes
	Control
	Information
	Costs and Benefits
	TEPCO (Utility company)
	Construct and operate nuclear facilities; submit safety reports; lobby for regulatory approval; negotiate compensation and compliance terms
	Safe operation or regulatory breach; reputational loss
	High internal control over operational decisions, low control over approval outcomes (dependent on METI)
	Incomplete but privileged information; detailed technical and safety data not fully disclosed; strong informational asymmetry with local governments and residents
	High profit potential; risk of safety costs or liability
	Futaba Town Government (Local Government)
	Approve siting requests; negotiate host-community agreements; administer local subsidies; communicate with residents
	Economic gains; fiscal dependency and spatial risk exposure
	Moderate control over local implementation; minimal control over plant operation
	Incomplete and imperfect information; relies on TEPCO and METI disclosures; cannot independently assess long-term risks
	Fiscal and employment gains; dependency and political risk
	Fukushima Prefectural Government (Provincial Government)
	Coordinate disaster management and safety oversight; channel central subsidies; mediate between METI and municipalities
	Regional stability or policy failure
	Partial control over oversight and coordination; limited control over central policy
	Partial information; access to some technical reports; limited oversight of plant operations; awareness shaped by trust in TEPCO and METI
	Regional economic revitalization; reputational and administrative risks
	Central Government and National Regulatory Bodies (Central Policy Actor)
	Set energy policy; approve nuclear plant construction; allocate subsidies; regulate TEPCO
	Energy stability; policy failure; loss of local trust
	High control over policy design and approval processes; low adaptive feedback from local levels
	Most complete information; influenced by bureaucratic opacity and selective framing of risk
	Policy success; bureaucratic capture risk
	Residents of Futaba Town (Local Residents)
	Participate in consultations; accept/oppose plant siting; accept compensation schemes; vote
	Economic relief; local development
	Minimal control over outcomes; control limited to participation
	Minimal and delayed information; dependence on government and media; high uncertainty about safety
	Employment and infrastructure; exposure to latent safety and environmental risks
	

The action situation described in Table 1 is based on Onitsuka (2012), Kingston (2012), and Taniguchi (2022), who analyze pre-2011 nuclear governance at both the state and regional levels, specifically in Futaba Town. The potential outcomes do not include a disaster on the scale of the 2011 accident, since this was a latent risk beyond what actors expected. In addition, the table treats METI, the Diet, the Atomic Energy Commission, and other central policymaking bodies outlined as key actors in the nuclear village in Kingston (2012) as a single collective actor representing the central government’s role in nuclear governance. The following section examines the ideologies that influence the formal and informal rules governing the actions, control, and information available to actors. 
Mental Models and Ideologies 
A key mental model that informed regulatory practices, and the lack thereof, was the “safety myth” (anzen shinwa). The dichotomous belief that civilian use of nuclear energy, unlike military applications, is inherently safe emerged as a result of post-war pacifist nation-building and the promotion of science (Saito, 2022). The 1954 Bikini Atoll hydrogen bomb incident further reinforced this division, where civilian use was sanctified as a morally acceptable application of nuclear power. This framing was later instrumentalized by the nuclear village to promote nuclear expansion and suppress public and internal skepticism, ultimately becoming a widely accepted assumption among the public, the industry, and host communities (Ferguson & Janson, 2013, p. 7). 
The safety myth is reflected in the Atomic Energy Basic Act (1955), which emphasizes “promot[ing] industry” and the “development and utilization of nuclear energy” with little mention of risk prevention (Ministry of Justice, 2021). Overconfidence in safety measures became institutionalized, while informal norms of the nuclear village discouraged criticism by denying access to funding or media platforms (Kingston, 2012; Ferguson & Janson, 2013, p. 7). At the local level, municipalities such as Futaba internalized the same logic, framing nuclear hosting as safe modernization and discouraging dissent that might threaten subsidy flows. 
Japan’s nuclear governance was also shaped by the post-war developmental state model, which prioritized economic growth through close coordination between government and industry (Johnson, 1982, pp. 17-34). Japan’s bureaucracy, especially the Ministry of International Trade and Industry (MITI), later METI, promoted the growth of key “strategic industries” including the nuclear sector through government resources and funding (Kingston, 2012). Nuclear power was framed as a tool for energy security in Japan, which lacked oil, and reinforced the mental model that active state intervention in industry was both natural and beneficial. 
This model was mirrored in local governance, where municipal leaders cooperated closely with utilities to secure state development funds and sites for the plants (Onitsuka, 2012). These incentives were formalized through the 1974 “Three Laws for Electric Power Resource Sites” (dengen sanpō), comprised of the Special Budget Law for the Development of Electric Power, Promotion of Energy Resources Development Tax Law, and the Act on the Development of Areas Adjacent to Electric Power Generating Facilities, which tied regional development directly to the expansion of nuclear facilities (電気事業連合会[Federation of Electric Power Companies], n.d.). The state-industry cooperation model is also evident in the Electricity Business Act (1964), which seeks to “achieve the sound development of Electricity Businesses” through “appropriate and reasonable management,” thereby framing regulation as coordination rather than constraint (Ministry of Justice, 2009). 
Interaction with Rules 
Formal Rules 
This section applies the ADICO framework (Ostrom, 2005, pp. 139-152) to key legislation surrounding nuclear governance, then classifies each by its Aim (Ostrom, 2005, pp. 187-210). Article 4 of the Atomic Energy Basic Act (1955), analyzed in Table 2, establishes the Atomic Energy Commission (AEC) and the Nuclear Safety Commission (NSC), with Article 5 detailing the AEC’s policy planning responsibilities and the NSC’s advisory role on safety. 
Table 2
ADICO Analysis of the Atomic Energy Basic Act Article 4 (1955)
Component
	Applicable section
	Attribute
	The Cabinet; implied by the clause "The... Commission[s] are established under the Cabinet"
	Deontic
	Must; the clause "The... Commission[s] are established under the Cabinet... [to carry out] policies" implies obligation to establish and operate the commissions
	Aim
	Establish the AEC and NSC and implement planned nuclear energy policies
	Conditions
	When implementing nuclear energy programs; implied by "the purposes of... carrying out the planned national policies on... nuclear energy and of implementing the democratic operation of nuclear energy administration"
	Or Else
	Not explicitly stated; implied legal noncompliance if the commissions are not established (i.e., institutional illegitimacy of nuclear policy execution)
	

Note. The full Article 4 is provided in Appendix B.  As Article 4 establishes the AEC and NSC, which are collections of formal positions assigned to individuals, it falls under the category of a position rule within the Aim classification (Ostrom, 2005, pp. 187-210). 


Although Article 5 formally assigns oversight and planning functions to the AEC and NSC, in practice, this arrangement facilitated regulatory capture. The NSC, which was only an advisory body, lacked the authority to ensure that NISA, the later-established regulatory body that reported to METI, implemented and enforced safety regulations stringently (Ferguson & Janson, 2013, p. 6). Consequently, the creation of these commissions, intended to promote independent oversight, reinforced the nuclear village’s dominance, contributing to limited scrutiny and dominance. 
Article 42 of the Electricity Business Act (1964) outlines the regulatory process for energy infrastructure, and its analysis is presented in Table 3. 
Table 3
ADICO Analysis of the Electricity Business Act (1964) 
Component
	Applicable section (Clause)
	Attribute
	A person who installs Electric Facilities for Business Use (Clause 1)
	Deontic
	Must; obligation implied by use of "shall" (Clauses 1 and 2)
	Aim
	Establish safety regulations for each organization in charge of the Electric Facilities for Business Use and comply with them and notify the Minister of Economl Trade, and Industry of the regulations (Clauses 1 and 2)
	Conditions
	Before starting use of the facilities (Clause 1), when having revised safety regulations (Clause 2)
	Or Else
	Not explicitly stated; implied that noncompliance may result in METI enforcement actions such as suspension of operation license
	

Note. The full text of Article 42 is provided in Appendix B. Article 42 requires utilities to establish safety regulations, assigning them the responsibility to create and maintain regulations, making it a choice rule under the Aim classification (Ostrom, 2005, pp. 187-210). It also mandates that utilities report their regulations to METI, affecting the flow of information in the system, making it an information rule.   
        While METI may order revisions, the law presumes that the regulations developed by the installers are sufficient to ensure safety, which institutionalizes the belief in “safe” civilian use of energy infrastructure and mirroring the nuclear safety myth. Additionally, by requiring notification rather than independent oversight the law enshrines the state-industry coordination, favoring a collaborative, enabling approach over constraining utilities with strict regulation. 
Table 4 describes Article 7 of the Act on the Development of Areas Adjacent to Electric Power Generating Facilities (Digital Agency, 1974), which outlines how the state can subsidize nuclear development in municipalities.
Table 4
Article 7 of Act on the Development of Areas Adjacent to Electric Power Generating Facilities (1974) 
Component
	Applicable section
	Attribute
	The State
	Deontic
	May
	Aim
	Provide grants to local governments
	Conditions
	Within the scope of the budget and pursuant to Cabinet Order, when the municipality consents to the hosting plan
	Or Else
	No grants; implied that grants are dependent on hosting in "to cover expenses... under the agreed-upon public facility development plan"
	

Note. The full text of Article 7, translated by the author, is provided in Appendix B. Article 7 permits the state to issue grants, assigning them the ability to provide monetary incentives to municipalities, making it a choice rule.   


        Article 7 permits state subsidies to communities hosting power facilities, reflecting the developmental state model and formalizing state-industry coordination. By creating economic dependence on the utility, it incentivizes local compliance with industry priorities and discourages criticism, reinforcing social alignment with pro-nuclear norms.  
Informal Rules
One of the key informal practices that characterized nuclear governance in Japan was regulatory capture (Kingston, 2012), where the nuclear industry effectively controlled its regulators. Reflecting the model of close state–industry coordination, NISA, operating within METI’s pro-nuclear framework, implemented regulations in TEPCO’s interest, relying on self-reporting and providing weak oversight, which eroded safety culture at the plant level.
This capture was reinforced by amakudari (“descent from heaven”), the revolving door allowing retired bureaucrats to take positions in the very industries they once supervised (Kingston, 2012; Ferguson & Janson, 2013, p. 2). The practice deepened conflicts of interest between the state and utilities, further weakening regulatory independence.
The resulting dependence on self-reporting and weak safety culture sustained the safety myth, normalizing data falsification and concealment of violations while reinforcing the illusion of nuclear safety (Kingston, 2012; Ferguson & Janson, 2013, p. 7). This secrecy extended to local governments reliant on nuclear subsidies under the Three Power Source Development Laws, where economic dependence discouraged open criticism and led municipalities to withhold risk information from residents (Onitsuka, 2012).
Complex Systems Analysis of Mental Models, Ideologies, and Rules
Figure 1 illustrates the interactions between the factors identified in previous sections that give rise to the emergent property of eroded safety culture within Fukushima plant management. 
Figure 1
Causal Diagram of Mental Models, Ideologies, Formal Rules, and Informal Rules shaping Japan’s pre-2011 Nuclear Governance
  

Note. Arrows indicate influence or interaction; see text for detailed explanations of each connection.
The safety myth, developmental state ideology, and state-industry coordination framed both the design and adherence of formal and informal rules. The Electricity Business Act and the Atomic Energy Basic Act assigned responsibilities for safety regulation and oversight, but these formal obligations were enacted within a culture of regulatory capture, amakudari, and silencing. NISA’s reliance on self-reporting, coupled with METI’s collaborative rather than constraining oversight, allowed plant operators to maintain weak safety practices, reinforcing the safety myth and normalizing secrecy. Informal rules further entrenched the mental models: amakudari strengthened state-industry ties, and regulatory capture, together with the culture of silencing dissent, downplayed risks proliferated the safety myth, producing tightly coupled feedback loops. 
These interactions produced a self-reinforcing system in which local and national actors were economically and institutionally incentivized to align with industrial and state objectives rather than uphold rigorous safety standards. The resulting erosion of plant safety culture was an emergent outcome of nonlinear interactions among mental models, ideologies, and formal and informal rules, illustrating the systemic fragility of the nuclear village network and its vulnerability to external shocks as evidenced by the 2011 disaster.
________________
References
Digital Agency. (1974). 発電用施設周辺地域整備法[Act on the Development of Areas Adjacent to Electric Power Generating Facilities (Act No.78, 1974))]. e-Gov 法令検索 [e-Gov Legislation Search]. Retrieved October 8, 2025, from https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/law/349AC0000000078
電気事業連合会[Federation of Electric Power Companies]. (n.d.). 電源三法交付金制度[Three Laws for Electric Power Resource Sites]. 電気事業連合会[Federation of Electric Power Companies]. https://www.fepc.or.jp/supply/hatsuden/nuclear/chiiki/nuclear/seido/
Ferguson, C. D., & Janson, M. (2013). Regulating Japanese Nuclear Power in the Wake of the Fukushima Daiichi Accident. Federation of American Scientists. http://www.jstor.org/stable/resrep18921
Funabashi, Y. (2021). Meltdown: Inside the Fukushima Nuclear Crisis. Rowman & Littlefield Publishers.
Johnson, C. (1982). MITI and the Japanese Miracle: The Growth of Industrial Policy, 1925-1975. Stanford University Press.
Kingston, J. (2012). Japan’s Nuclear Village. Asia-Pacific Journal: Japan Focus, 10(37), 3822. https://apjjf.org/2012/10/37/jeff-kingston/3822/article
Ministry of Justice. (2009, April 1). Electricity Business Act (Act No. 170, 1964). Japanese Law Translation. Retrieved October 8, 2025, from https://www.japaneselawtranslation.go.jp/en/laws/view/51
Ministry of Justice. (2021, June 30). Atomic Energy Basic Act (Act No. 186, 1955). Japanese Law Translation. Retrieved October 8, 2025, from https://www.japaneselawtranslation.go.jp/en/laws/view/3759
Onitsuka, H. (2012). Hooked on Nuclear Power: Japanese State-Local Relations and the Vicious Cycle of Nuclear Dependence. Asia-Pacific Journal: Japan Focus, 10(3), 3677. https://apjjf.org/2012/10/3/Hiroshi-Onitsuka/3677/article
Ostrom, E. (2005). Understanding Institutional Diversity. Princeton University Press. https://www.jstor.org/stable/j.ctt7s7wm
Saito, H. (2022). The imaginary and epistemology of disaster preparedness: The case of Japan's nuclear safety failure. Poetics, 93(A), 101594. 10.1016/j.poetic.2021.101594
Taniguchi, K. S. (2022). Why Fukushima? A diachronic and multilevel comparative institutional analysis of a nuclear disaster. Energy Policy, 167, 113049. 10.1016/j.enpol.2022.113049
________________
Appendix A: HC/LO Apllications
#emergentproperties
        I identified an emergent property of pre-Fukushima nuclear governance in Japan: the lack of safety culture in plant management, which is noted as a major contributor to the 2011 disaster. Throughout the paper I discuss how the non-linear interaction of elements (mental models, legislation, and informal practices) systemically erode safety culture, which in turn reinforce the entrenched mental models. 
#complexcausality
        I discuss and visualize the causal links between mental models (sate-industry coordination and the safety myth), ideologies (developmental state model), formal rules (legislation surrounding nuclear energy governance), and informal rules (regulatory capture, amakudari, and the culture of silencing/secrecy) interact to influence the actions of actors involved in nuclear governance. I also describe how these factors may reinforce eachother through feedback loops, explaining the maladaptive nature of the system. 
#formalrules
        I discuss examples of formal legislation (Atomic Energy Basic Act, Electricity Business Act, Act on the Development of Areas Adjacent to Electric Power Generating Facilities) that are shaped by and reinforce mental models about state intervention in industry and nuclear safety. I analyzed how these rules assign authority to various actors and create economic interdependency and conflicts of interest that shape the system’s behavior. 
#informalrules
        I identify informal practices (regulatory capture, amakudari, the culture of silencing/secrecy) that shape practices in the regulation of nuclear power, where regulatory capture leads to lax oversight and mismanagement of risks, amakudari further entrenches regulatory capture by strengthening state-industry ties, and the culture of silencing/secrecy normalizes poor safety regulation and by falsifying inspection records leads to the false impression of nuclear safety. 
#rulesandoutcomes
        I explain how formal and informal rules perpetuate the erosion of safety culture through their effect on practices such as the provision of subsidies that forester economic dependence of host communities and (poorly implemented) safety regulations. By analyzing how formal rules enable informal malpractices I highlight the joint role of formal and informal rules in eroding safety culture in nuclear governance. 
#constructivism
        I analyzed how ideologies (developmental state model) shape mental models (state-industry coordination), and how these together influence the design of formal rules and informal practices within Japan’s nuclear governance. I also discuss how the formal and informal rules in turn entrench the mental models and ideologies, which led to the development of a maladaptive system prone to risk. 


________________
Appendix B: AI Statement
I used ChatGPT to clarify some concepts and seek advice on overall formatting (see conversation here: https://chatgpt.com/share/68e839d4-16bc-8011-b3b7-bcc63e055a82). I also used Grammarly.com to proofread my final submission. 
________________
Appendix C: Formal Rules used in ADICO Syntax Analysis
1. Atomic Energy Basic Act, Article 4
(Transitional Measures Concerning Restrictions on Use of Name)
Article 4        With regard to any person who actually uses the name "Japan Nuclear Cycle Development Institute" at the time of the enforcement of this Act, the provisions of Article 9 of the New Act do not apply for six months after the enforcement of this Act.
Source: Ministry of Justice, 2021
2. Electricity Business Act, Article 42
(Safety Regulations)
Article 42        (1) A person who installs Electric Facilities for Business Use shall, in order to ensure safety of the construction, maintenance and operation of the Electric Facilities for Business Use, pursuant to the provision of an Ordinance of the Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry, establish safety regulations for each organization in charge of the Electric Facilities for Business Use the safety of which should be secured uniformly, and notify the Minister of Economy, Trade and Industry of the regulations before the commencement of the use of the Electric Facilities for Business Use by the organization (in the case of facilities requiring self-inspection set forth in Article 50-2, paragraph 1 or operator's inspection set forth in Article 52, paragraph 1, before the commencement of the construction of the facilities).
(2) A person who installs Electric Facilities for Business Use shall, when having revised the safety regulations, notify the Minister of Economy, Trade and Industry of the revised matters without delay.
(3) The Minister of Economy, Trade and Industry may, when he/she finds it necessary in order to ensure safety of the construction, maintenance, and operation of Electric Facilities for Business Use, order the person who installs the Electric Facilities for Business Use to revise the safety regulations.
(4) A person who installs Electric Facilities for Business Use and employees thereof shall observe the safety regulations.
Source: Ministry of Justice, 2009
3. Act on the Development of Areas Adjacent to Electric Power Generating Facilities, Article 7 [Translated by author]
(Grants)
Article 7        The state may, within the scope of the budget and pursuant to Cabinet Order, provide grants to local public bodies (including port offices as defined in Article 4, Paragraph 1 of the Ports Act [Act No. 218 of 1950]; the same shall apply in the following article) to cover expenses related to projects under the agreed-upon public facility development plan. 
Source: Digital Agency, 1974

name: Reflection on Capstone [final]

﻿Reflection on Capstone




Minerva University
CP191: Capstone Seminar
Prof. H. Singh
September 27, 2025












________________


Reflection on Capstone
Work Product: Letter to Self
Dear me,
Congratulations on surviving your Capstone Project! I know the year-long project must have been a lot to juggle amidst recruiting season, especially when you had your end-of-semester full draft due the same week as multiple rounds of interviews with prospective employers. 
Remember how you wanted to learn machine learning for your Capstone, but didn’t get the course in the lottery so decided to learn the Python packages yourself? But then you started working with R for your civic project at Universidad de Buenos Aires and realized you wanted to do a more academic take on ML? And now your deliverable isn’t just a classification model, but a whole paper (pending publication!) on applications in clinical psychology research? You might have pivoted on your idea a couple of times, but you’ve come such a long way skill- and knowledge-wise – you’re ready to dive into the pharma industry as soon as you graduate.
Not only have you learned so much over the course of the last year working on your Capstone, but you even got real experience defending your ideas in front of experts who are out to dissect them in your Oral Defense. Now you’re all set for stakeholder presentations or conferences (not that those would even come close to the stage fright from presenting in front of a whole auditorium at Symposium in your first year). 
I’m so proud of you for pulling through this past year and graduating with such a cool project. I hope the hard work pays off and you settle right into your budding career.
Best,
Suisei from Fall 2025
Process Documentation
The idea mentioned above is my actual idea, but it was much more vague. So, my starting point was to think beyond “I want to do something related to ML” and identify what exactly my deliverables might be (a classification model and accompanying paper), what topics it might be related to (clinical psychology research), and how exactly I ended up there (I’m actually doing a Civic Project in BA). I then backtracked and identified skill gaps between my present self and the kind of roles I am seeking post-graduation, and thought about how I can acquire these skills through the process of completing a Capstone Project (self-learning and deliberately choosing a relevant Civic Project). 
AI Use
I used AI to do a quick sanity check about my Capstone idea. I actually will be using R in my Civic Project and was hoping to use it as an opportunity to pick up a new skill, but I was a little unsure if proficiency in R, such as that evidenced by using it in Capstone, makes you particularly employable for data science roles. I asked ChatGPT about different use cases of R vs. Python, the more common ML language, and how knowing R might relate to career opportunities. It turned out that some niche industries still have legacy code bases that rely on the language, so a viable career path given my SS (psychology/neuroscience) and CS (data science and statistics) double major might be corporate pharmaceutical research as it relates to clinical psychology. See the full conversation here. 
HC/LO Applications
#audience: I tried to make the tone of the letter match my usual inner monologue and self-talk. For example, I tend to think in run-on sentences with minimal punctuation, and that is reflected in the longer sentences that might be missing a comma or two (or three). The interruptions in () are also a reflection of how I tend to interject in or poke fun at my own thoughts. I was also sure to clearly tell my future self I’m proud of her because she probably felt very anxious until she landed a job and passed her last CP course. 
#interpretivelens: I somewhat simplify a lot of the personal context (“Remember when…” and onward), since the shared experiences between me and my future self will allow her to fill in the gaps. For example, I don’t mention that the course the lottery didn’t give me is CS156: Finding Patterns in Data with Machine Learning, or that getting the Civic Project with R may have been the result of a slight overstatement on my resume and the scope of the project felt very daunting. Instead, I count on my future self to have remembered these experiences and have made the most of them to view the letter in a positive light. 
Process for Current Capstone Idea
My current idea is to develop a classification/prediction model in R based on some open psychology or neuroscience database with an accompanying write-up, which I want to read like a peer-reviewed paper. I arrived at this idea by listing what I want out of mt Capstone Project and attempting to satisfy them. To provide a richer context for the letter and process description above, here are some raw notes on my project idea that I was taking as I formulated the “bold assumptions” for this assignment:
* Must include:
* Interest/major in data
   * ML?
* Relate to psch/neuro for SS major
   * Clinical datasets
      * Open data like PhysioNet, OpenNeuro?
         * Less IRB issues
         * #ResearcherBestPractices no replicability crisis here
* Relevance for post-grad goals (few years corporate, potential academic research)
   * language/technology
      * R for academia/publishing 
         * Best for classic ML like regression, trees
            * classification/predictive model? 
      * Python/SQL for corporate 
         * can be a personal project 
         * just apply to niche industries/labs that care about R
            * pharma/health?

﻿Process Documentation




Minerva University
CP191: Capstone Seminar
Prof. H. Singh
December 18, 2025












________________


Process Documentation
Overview
This process documentation outlines the development of the deliverable I produced for my Civic Project in Buenos Aires, which was conducted at the Universidad de Buenos Aires under the guidance of Professor Franco Marsico. I have been contributing to the open-source R package mispitools, which is a Bayesian decision-making toolkit for missing person searches. I wanted to develop a new function that computes likelihood ratios for missing person identification when it is known that it was a forced disappearance, so as a preliminary step, I produced an exploratory data analysis[1] (EDA) of demographic, temporal, and regional trends in open data on real missing person cases in Argentina, which I can use to build a prior distribution. I began working on the EDA in October, following my initial meeting with my mentor, and continued to work on it throughout the semester.  
Use of Generative AI
I utilized GitHub Copilot in multiple parts of my process. I chose Copilot as my primary tool because it can read entire codebases as context and has distinct, specialized ‘modes’ that are optimized for programming and fit into my workflow. Specifically, it has an ‘ask’ mode where it can read and answer questions about the code base, an ‘agent’ mode with editing and command-running privileges that can assist in actual coding, a ‘plan’ mode that helps ideate how to implement features, and an autocomplete feature that predicts what the user is tyring to code in the editor based on existing input, such as comments and function names. 
For this submission, I am unable to provide the actual chat history, as I had not saved it prior to choosing it as my topic, and the history gets wiped when restarting VSCode. 
Understanding the package
When I was first assigned to my Civic Project, I used the ‘Ask’ mode of Copilot to read through the repository files and give me a high-level overview of the directory structure and functionality of each file. Example prompts are “Which file is [copied text] of the README talking about?” and “Explain how the LR_POP() function works.” I found this particularly helpful as I was not familiar with R, the main language used in the package. This helped me identify where the package could be improved and propose a deliverable for the semester. 
Cleaning the Data
I relied heavily on GitHub Copilot’s agent mode while writing the data-cleaning script. The dataset contained mixed data types, inconsistent values and missing entry handling, and long text fields, and many column names and entries were in Spanish, which I do not speak. Copilot helped me quickly interpret columns and draft cleaning code for tasks like parsing strings and normalizing values. My prompts were mostly in the format “Help me write a diagnostic script to see how dates are formatted in the ‘fecha_detencion’ column,” or “Based on the parsing failures, add another if clause to catch these cases.” I reviewed and adjusted all generated code, verifying the results through summaries and spot checks. 
Visualizing trends
I used Copilot’s agent mode to help translate high-level visualization requests into plotting code (e.g., specifying variables, grouping, and layout for different chart types). This mainly reduced time spent on plotting syntax and library-specific details. Decisions about which visualizations to use, how variables were aggregated or binned, and how results were interpreted were made by me, and all plots were checked against the underlying data. 
Using Git and Source Control
Since I had never worked on open-source or collaborative coding projects, I was unfamiliar with how to pull new commits, merge branches, or open pull requests. Copilot’s ask and agent modes helped me with the initial setup of my workspace and troubleshooting when my files stopped tracking due to a corrupt folder. Through conversations with it, I learned more about terminal Git commands and best practices. 
HC/LO Applications for the Work Product
#cs110-PythonProgramming: I developed column-level data cleaning functions that could parse around 70-80% of mixed data entries. Although the code may not be the most concise, as it had to account for inconsistent data formats, it runs in O(n) time, making it efficient for wrangling large databases as the missing person dataset. This was an intentional tradeoff, as the goal was not to make the functions reusable utilities, but rather to optimize them for this dataset. Based on diagnostics, most of the rows that couldn’t be extracted (around 80%) appear to be genuinely missing rather than failures of the parsing logic. 
#cs110-CodeReadability: I was careful to add detailed comments to the data cleaning code, as individual functions tended to be rather long and included multiple if statements to account for various formatting inconsistencies. Specifically, I was careful to add comments that described the type of parsing attempt being made and how it was done, so that when running diagnostics, I could easily pinpoint which clause was failing. I chose to leave these comments in the code to guide reviewers on my cleaning process. 
#dataviz: I produced compelling interactive visuals to support my data analysis. My choice of chart type was heavily influenced by industry norms, such as using a population pyramid to illustrate age-by-sex demographic distribution. I also made the conscious choice to use the Plotly package (as opposed to the more common matplotlib) to make my plots interactive with hover templates, thereby reducing any visual clutter that would have resulted from adding more information to the visualizations.  
#communicationdesign: I attempted to synthesize my findings in an easy-to-understand and interactive notebook accessible to both non-technical audiences and industry professionals. Specifically, I make all of my code cells toggleable with descriptive titles and keep them hidden to reduce visual clutter, and hide longer outputs that may not be relevant to non-technical audiences. Thus, at first glance, the notebook only includes visualization outputs and markdown cells that annotate/describe processes or figures, but also preserves the code for those who are interested. 
Considered Model Deliverables
For the EDA, I considered similar works publicly available on Kaggle. I found these works by searching for “Kaggle EDA projects” on my search engine. Some examples, along with how I used them, can be found in the Appendix. These works informed my approach to cleaning data, the design of my visualizations, and the format of the accompanying text explanations. Based on examples I found, I decided to use the interactive visualization package Plotly (as opposed to static packages) and opted for a more simplistic writing style, using bullet points rather than descriptive paragraphs.  
Timeline: GitHub Commit History
Table 1 provides an accurate timeline of my programming experience.

AI Statement: I used Grammarly.com to proofread this submission, and my AI use for the work product is outlined in the respective section. No other AI tools were used for this Process Documentation itself. 
________________
References 
Andreshg. (2021, May 29). Eda 📊 beginner to expert 📈 plotly. Kaggle. https://www.kaggle.com/code/andreshg/eda-beginner-to-expert-plotly

Marsico, F. L., & Caridi, I. (2023). Incorporating non-genetic evidence in large scale missing person searches: A general approach beyond filtering. Forensic Science International: Genetics, 66, 102891. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fsigen.2023.102891
________________
Appendix: Examples of considered model deliverables 
        The following excerpts describe how I applied insights from several works in my own EDA. 
Eda 📊 beginner to expert 📈 plotly (Andreshg, 2021)  
I found the interactive plots created with Plotly to be very good for #dataviz, as they reduced the need for more granular grids, which can clutter the image, by adding counts to hover templates, and add can add more information by configuring the hover template. I decided to use the package across my whole work as well. 
Comprehensive EDA with nice graphs (Devbilalkhan, 2024)   
Summarizing the tables at the beginning of the notebook felt like a good way to introduce the reader, who is not going to be looking at the raw data, to the dataset.








  

Many of the key points were described in bullet points, which keep insights about different variables separate. I found this approach much easier to understand than paragraph descriptions and followed it in my own EDA. 
________________
[1] Note to grader: Please keep the EDA open on another tab to follow along the main text. If the link does not render as a Jupyter notebook, please click on the ‘open in Colab’ button that appears on screen. Please note that the notebooks is set to view only and is hosted on Drive to allow a quicker view of the fully rendered version.

________________


Neuroscience in the News
A recent Guardian article claims that optimists share similar frameworks for thinking about the future, suggesting that they are “literally on the same wavelength” (Davis, 2025). This paper discusses the underlying research of the article, critiques its accuracy of representation of the research findings, proposes an academically revised version of the article, and provides a short-form video on the same topic for a general audience. 
Foundational Research 
The Guardian article is based on a 2025 study by Yanagisawa et al., which used fMRI to study the neural activity in participants during an episodic future thinking task. The study recruited married adult participants, who were asked to vividly imagine scenarios varying in emotional valence (positive, neutral, or negative) and referential target (themselves or their partner) during fMRI scans. Participants then completed the Revised Life Orientation Test (LOT-R) to assess their level of optimism. 
These experimental conditions were chosen for the expected structured variability in participants' psychological experiences, which allowed for analysis with intersubject representational similarity analysis (IS-RSA) (Chen et al., 2020). This design was critical because IS-RSA is a technique that specifically exploits variability, exploring how intersubject differences in brain dynamics are linked to individual differences in behavioral disposition, such as optimism. The complex nature of the scenarios requiring integration of external information with unique endogenous factors like past experiences and future goals was necessary to elicit the strong, idiosyncratic affective experiences that IS-RSA can effectively analyze, unlike conventional methods that assume shared responses. 
The researchers found that the intersubject neural representational similarity in the medial prefrontal cortex (MPFC) is associated with optimism scores, where pairs of two optimistic individuals tended to have more similar representations of episodic future thinking, while less optimistic pairs had more variability. They also found that optimistic individuals tended to place greater emphasis on the valence of the imagined situation, suggesting that they imagined positive events as more distinct from negative ones. 
Critical Analysis
Comparing the Guardian article (Davis, 2025) to the original research (Yanagisawa et al., 2025), it appears that the title and body of the journalistic description fairly accurately captures the both the central findings, namely the neural convergence between optimistic individuals and their clearer differentiation between positive and negative future events, and key components of the experimental design in terms of methods and measures, without lending to sensationalism. 
However, the article necessarily simplifies technical concepts, such as “convergent neural representations” and “shared neural processing,” being flattened into “brain patterns” for accessibility to a broader audience. The most notable use of simplifying language is the phrase “[optimists’] brains are literally on the same wavelength,” although this is a quote directly attributed to the first author, Kuniaki Yanagisawa, and thus can be taken as a simplification on the researchers’ front. Similarly, the article also avoids technical jargon such as intersubject representational similarity analysis (IS-RSA), multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA), individual difference multidimensional scaling (INDSCAL), and default mode network (DMN) that are used to describe the analytical methods in Yanagisawa et al. (2025), but this does not distort the reporting of the findings. 
Based on this critique, an academically revised version of the article might read as shown in the following section. 
Revision
Title: Neural Correlates of Optimism During Episodic Future Thinking: A Review and Critique of Yanagisawa et al. (2025)
Recent research has explored how dispositional optimism, or the general tendency to expect positive future outcomes, shapes neural representation of imagined events. Yanagisawa et al. (2025) investigated whether optimistic individuals share similar patterns of brain activity when engaging in episodic future thinking. The authors proposed that optimism may influence how people cognitively organize and emotionally evaluate possible future scenarios. 
In their studies, 87 married adult participants underwent functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), during which they were instructed to imagine a series of future life scenarios with varying emotional valences (positive, neutral, negative, and death-related) that prompted them to imagine their (or their partner’s) future. Study 1 included death-related scenarios to observe responses to extremely negative events, while these were excluded in Study 2 to address the resulting imbalanced distribution of negative emotional conditions. 
Intersubject representational similarity analysis (IS-RSA) revealed that individuals scoring higher in optimism exhibited greater intersubject similarity in activation patterns within the medial prefrontal cortex (MPFC), a region associated with self-referential and future-oriented cognition. By contrast, subjects with lower optimism scores displayed more idiosyncratic MPFC patterns, suggesting increased variability in episodic future thinking. The studies also found from individual difference multidimensional scaling (INDSCAL) that more optimistic subjects place more emphasis on the valence differentiation (whether the event is positive, neutral, or negative), suggesting that they maintain a more distinct categorical boundary between desirable and undesirable outcomes. These findings suggest that shared MPFC activation among optimistic individuals may reflect a common cognitive framework for constructing future-oriented thought, potentially supporting resilience and social coherence. 
Public Communication
The TikTok-style informational video can be found here. 


Word count: 812 words
________________


References
Chen, P.-H. A., Jolly, E., Cheong, J. H., & Chang, L. J. (2020). Intersubject representational similarity analysis reveals individual variations in affective experience when watching erotic movies. NeuroImage, 216, 116851. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1053811920303372
        This article was used to gain context on the IS-RSA technique, which is used in the paper referenced by the Guardian article. 
Davis, N. (2025, July 21). Optimists share similar brain patterns when thinking about the future, scans show. The Guardian. Retrieved October 10, 2025, from https://www.theguardian.com/science/2025/jul/21/optimists-share-similar-brain-patterns-when-thinking-about-the-future-scans-show 
Yanagisawa, K., Nakai, R., Asao, K., Kashima, E. S., Sugiura, H., & Abe, N. (2025). Optimistic people are all alike: Shared neural representations supporting episodic future thinking among optimistic individuals. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (PNAS), 122(30), e2511101122. https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2511101122 
________________
Appendix A: HC/LO Applications
#NeuralSubstrate
        I discuss how activity in the DMN, particularly the MPFC, is related to self-referential and future-oriented cognition. I also discuss how fMRI results show similar patterns in MPFC activation between more optimistic individuals, while less optimistic individuals show more variability, suggesting that the behavioral disposition to optimism is a result of a common cognitive framework. 
#NeuroscienceApproaches
        I explain the reasoning behind the use of IS-RSA and INDSCAL, and how the focus on capturing intersubject and individual-difference structure connects to the research question in Yanagisawa et al. (2025), which was to study how optimism shapes neural representation. 
#CritiqueExpDesign
        I  discuss how IS-RSA, a technique that specifically exploits variability, unlike other methods that assume similar responses across subjects, has high synergy with the experimental design that seeks to elucidate individually varied responses from subjects.  
#critique
        I  critique the Guardian article on the basis of the accuracy of the representation of the findings in Yanagisawa et al. (2025). I identify how key findings and methodologies are fairly well-represented in the journalistic pace, whilst also noting how technical jargon may have been omitted or simplified to appeal to a broader audience.  
#audience
        I  consider the audience of Gen Z when making the short-form video by keeping it under 3 minutes (as they dislike spending too much time on a single video), using sensational, attention-grabbing language, and using trending caption formats. I also use the “stitched” style, where a knowledgeable user adds to another user’s video to answer their questions. 
#evidencebased
        In the video, I make sure to back up my claims by citing the source in on-screen captions, and strive to keep the narrative aligned with the source without exaggeration or oversimplification while balancing this with tailoring my language to the intended audience. 


________________
Appendix B: AI Statement and Acknowledgments
I used Grammarly.com to proofread my final submission. No other AI tools were used. I would also like to acknowledge my friend Lotte, who kindly participated in my video.

name: Research Proposal [final]
text_raw:
﻿Tab 1
b




Research Proposal: Building a Classifier for Early-Onset Dementia




Minerva University
SS152: Cognitive Neuroscience
Prof. T. Stan
December 18, 2025














________________


Research Proposal: Building a Classifier for Early-Onset Alzheimer’s Disease
In 2019, dementia was the seventh leading cause of death worldwide, responsible for an estimated 1.6 million deaths that year (World Health Organization [WHO], 2022, p. 1). AD is the most common form of dementia, contributing to roughly 50–60% of all dementia cases (Alzheimer's Disease International, n.d.). While the condition cannot be fully reversed with modern neuroscience, it is known that early discovery and intervention can delay the onset of late-stage symptoms, which largely reduce patients’ capacity for independent and healthy living (WHO, 2022, pp. 1-2). 
One major roadblock to effective early intervention is the costly and time-intensive nature of definitive diagnostic procedures, such as comprehensive neuropsychological batteries and neuroimaging, which limits broad screening (WHO, 2022, pp. 29-30). To address this, this paper explores the question: Can behavioral, cognitive, and demographic features alone reliably distinguish early mild cognitive impairment (MCI), the precursor of AD, from healthy aging? Specifically, this proposal outlines the methodology for a preliminary study that could serve as a pilot for a larger scale study to build a lightweight classification model based on the aforementioned factors, and as proof of concept provides an exploratory secondary analysis to determine whether the most informative features exhibit sufficient separability to support lightweight screening in principle based on a publicly available dataset of Altzheimer’s patients. 
Literature Review 
Mild Cognitive Impairment
MCI is recognized as a transitional state between healthy aging and AD, characterized by episodic memory decline and subtle impairments in inhibitory control, task shifting, and cognitive flexibility (Grundman, 2004, p. 59). While patients at this stage may remain functionally independent and show no to minimal impairment in basic activities of daily living (ADLs), they begin to exhibit measurable cognitive inefficiencies, such as reduced perceptual speed and category fluency, that fall significantly below those of healthy controls (Grundman, 2004, p. 64). 
The neurological basis of MCI is hippocampal atrophy, characterized by a reduced hippocampal volume that is typically 1.0 SD below normal, falling between that of healthy controls and AD patients (Grundman, 2004, p. 62). Structural decline leads to disruptions in default mode network connectivity, specifically altered deactivation in the anterior frontal, precuneus, and posterior cingulate cortices (Rombouts et al., 2005, p. 234). These changes represent a decrease in resting-state metabolism and a slower adaptation of the network to processing demands (Rombouts et al., 2005, p. 238). At the same time, widespread changes in medial temporal lobe structures lead to further declines in memory performance (Rombouts et al., 2005, p. 232). While there may be compensatory recruitment of other regions, such as the right parahippocampal gyrus, this is ultimately insufficient to halt the progression of cognitive pathology.[1] 
MCI is further associated with specific demographic, behavioral, and biological markers. For example, AD is a highly genetic illness with over 70 identified genetic loci, and MCI patients are significantly more likely than healthy controls to carry the apolipoprotein E ε4 allele, suggesting the condition may be hereditary (WHO, 2022, pp. 21-22; Grundman, 2004, p. 61). Furthermore, cardiometabolic stress, including hypertension, previous cardiovascular disease, and chronic hyperglycemia (indicated by higher HbA1c levels), independently predicts a greater likelihood of cognitive decline (Gorska-Ciebiada et al., 2014, p. 3). 
In addition, depression, physical inactivity, and sleep disruption are known to correlate with faster progression (Gao et al., 2022, pp. 5-6; Jia et al., 2021, p. 11; Gorska-Ciebiada et al., 2014, p. 3). Finally, cognitive screening scores, such as the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score (which often averages 27.3 in MCI), and daily function scales decline early and predict conversion risk (Grundman, 2004, p. 62). Amnestic MCI patients progress to clinically probable AD at a rate of approximately 10% to 15% per year (Grundman, 2004, p. 59).
The Difficulty of Early Detection and Potential of Computational Approaches
While MCI does have numerous, albeit weak, indicators, early detection remains notoriously difficult (Chehrehnegar et al., 2020, p. 2; WHO, 2022, pp. 28-29). While part of the problem may be attributed to a lack of structured cognitive testing in standard clinical testing, the larger underlying issue remains that the numerous indicators are seldom considered together (Chehrehnegar et al., 2020, p. 2; WHO, 2022, p. 29). This is further complicated by how many of these measures are self-reported, either by patients themselves or family, which makes them less reliable, and how many early symptoms are easily dismissed as normal aging, sleep problems, or mood disorders (Gao et al., 2022, p. 7; Chehrehnegar et al., 2020, p. 13; Gorska-Ciebiada et al., 2014, p. 5).[2] 
Given how risk factors are spread across different biological, behavioral, and cognitive domains, machine learning provides a well-suited approach to combining these weak predictors into a cohesive diagnostic framework. Tabular models, such as linear regression and random forests, are known to consistently perform well with clinical risk profiles; specifically, random forest models have demonstrated superior accuracy in predicting clinical deterioration compared to standard linear techniques (Churpek et al., 2016, p. 6). Furthermore, these approaches don’t require access to costly imaging procedures such as MRI, which often involves a prohibitive expense and a lack of standardized equipment in primary care, making them significantly more scalable to routine clinical care and low-resource settings (Schmidt et al., 2018, p. 2501).[3] 
The proposed method, outlined below, which builds a dataset from MCI patients and healthy controls, aims to utilize machine learning to understand which behavioral, cognitive, and demographic features meaningfully differentiate early MCI from healthy aging, and to quantify the extent to which each domain contributes to early decline. This approach overcomes the inherent difficulty in existing early-detection methods that rely on expensive neuroimaging or extensive neuropsychological batteries, and aligns with public health needs for low-cost, rapid screening. 
Hypothesis
Based on the literature review above, we hypothesize that: 
Individuals with early cognitive decline can be distinguished from cognitively healthy older adults using a combination of clinical risk factors. 
We aim to test this hypothesis with the experiment outlined below. 
Proposed Experimental Design
Sampling and Procedure
We will recruit 60 older adults aged 65 or above, including 30 with MCI and 30 age-matched healthy controls. The age threshold was chosen because it represents the demographic where MCI prevalence is estimated to reach 10% to 20%, and older age constitutes the strongest independent risk factor for pathological cognitive decline (Chehrehnegar et al., 2019, p. 2; WHO, 2022, p. 1). 
The proposed sample size falls within the typical range used in mechanistic neurocognitive research. For example, a recent systematic review of studies comparing MCI and control groups reported average sample sizes of around 30 per group, supporting the feasibility and interpretability of group comparisons in this design. Recruitment will be conducted through memory clinics and community aging cohorts (Paitel et al., 2025, pp. 3-4). 
The procedure will consist of three steps: first, we will collect basic demographics and health background information through a survey. Basic demographics and health background will include age, gender, education level, and self-report of medical history, including vascular risk factors, depressive symptoms, and lifestyle information (e.g., physical activity, sleep quality). These variables are associated with an increased risk of cognitive decline in older adults (Jia et al., 2021, p. 9). As part of the survey, we will also collect information such as family history of AD, age, gender, and education.
Next, we will ask participants to participate in one session of behavioral and cognitive testing. Here, participants will complete standardized cognitive assessments: the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE), which evaluates orientation, recall, attention, and language over a 30-point scale; and the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA), a more sensitive tool for MCI that probes executive function and visuospatial abilities in addition to memory and attention (Jia et al., 2021, p. 8). Additional domain-specific tasks, such as word list delayed recall, working memory (e.g., n-back), and processing speed measures, will be administered to capture deficits typical in early cognitive decline (Chehrehnegar et al., 2019, p. 5).
Finally, we will administer a structural MRI scan for mechanistic interpretation. Importantly, this will not be used to train the model, but rather a means of validating the comparison groups. 
Data Processing, Analysis, and Modeling
Structural T1-weighted MRI scans will be preprocessed using FreeSurfer, an automated cortical reconstruction and volumetric segmentation pipeline that has been widely validated for estimating hippocampal and medial temporal lobe volumes in aging, MCI, and AD cohorts (Schmidt et al., 2018, pp. 2500, 2510-2511). From these outputs, we will extract hippocampal volume and cortical thickness measures in medial temporal regions, which are among the earliest affected by AD-related pathology (Schmidt et al., 2018, p. 2510; Grundman, 2004, p. 60). 
Group differences between the MCI and healthy control groups will first be assessed using independent-samples t-tests for approximately normally distributed variables, with nonparametric alternatives (Mann–Whitney U tests) applied when distributional assumptions are violated. In addition to statistical significance testing, effect sizes (Cohen’s d) will be reported for all cognitive, functional, and neural measures to quantify the magnitude of group differences, which is particularly important in modest sample sizes typical of mechanistic neurocognitive research.
To examine relationships across domains, correlation and regression analyses will be conducted to test associations between neural markers (e.g., hippocampal volume, medial temporal lobe thickness) and behavioral or functional measures, including memory task performance, MMSE/MoCA scores, ADL measures, and subjective memory complaints. These analyses will assess whether neural degeneration is systematically related to functional impairment in early cognitive decline. 
Finally, exploratory modeling will be performed using logistic regression and random forest approaches (Churpek et al., 2016, p. 6). These models are not intended to serve as clinical diagnostic tools, but rather to identify which behavioral, clinical, and demographic features contribute most strongly to distinguishing MCI from healthy aging within the sample. Model outputs such as coefficients (logistic regression) and feature importance rankings (random forest) will be used to compare the relative contributions of cognitive scores, health variables, and demographics.
In supplementary analyses, hierarchical regression models will be used to evaluate whether neural measures explain variance in cognitive performance beyond basic demographic factors such as age, sex, and education, allowing for isolation of neurobiological contributions above known risk factors (Cohen et al., 2003).[4]


Word count: 1686 words

BMC Psychiatry, 21(1). https://doi.org/10.1186/s12888-021-03495-6
Paitel, E. R., Otteman, C. B. D., Polking, M. C., Licht, H. J., & Nielson, K. A. (2025). Functional and effective EEG connectivity patterns in Alzheimer’s disease and mild cognitive impairment: a systematic review. Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience, 17. https://doi.org/10.3389/fnagi.2025.1496235

Schmidt, M. F., Storrs, J. M., Freeman, K. B., Jack, C. R., Turner, S. T., Griswold, M. E., & Mosley, T. H. (2018). A comparison of manual tracing and FreeSurfer for estimating hippocampal volume over the adult lifespan. Human Brain Mapping, 39(6), 2500–2513. https://doi.org/10.1002/hbm.24017

________________


HC/LO Appendix
The locations of applications are tagged throughout the paper. 
#NeuralSubstrate
        I discuss the neurobiological mechanisms underlying mild cognitive impairment and its role as a precursor to Alzheimer’s disease. Specifically, I identify hippocampal atrophy as the primary driver, also mediated by changes in medial temporal lobe structures. I connect these structural changes to measurable cognitive deficits, such as episodic memory decline and subtle executive dysfunction, which justifies why I include both behavioral tasks and structural MRI in my study. The MRI is not used for prediction, but rather to validate that the behavioral differences observed in MCI reflect underlying neural pathology.  
#ethicalconsiderations
I explain how AD greatly reduces patients’ independence and ability to maintain a fulfilling life, and how early intervention can slow progression and prolong autonomy. This motivates the design of a study aimed at detecting MCI using minimal, non-invasive measures (behavioral, cognitive, and demographic features) that are scalable for routine clinical care. At the same time, I acknowledge the ethical responsibility to avoid overdiagnosis, false positives, or misinterpretation, which is why the ML models are exploratory and intended only to demonstrate feature separability rather than provide clinical diagnoses.
#CognitiveDevelopmentAndDecline
I outline how MCI patients exhibit symptoms that overlap with normal aging, and emphasize why detecting subtle differences is both challenging and important. I also discuss how lifestyle and health factors, such as sleep quality, physical activity, vascular risk, and mood, can influence or predict MCI, showing that cognitive decline results from both biological vulnerability and environmental factors. This reasoning directly informs my choice of behavioral and health-related measures to include in the dataset and ML model.
#CritiqueExpDesign
I propose a pilot study to evaluate the validity of my proposed machine learning approach for detecting early MCI. I justify the sample size (30 per group, consistent with prior mechanistic studies) and selected measures (behavioral, cognitive, demographic, and neural) to ensure they align with neuroscience best practices. I explicitly use ML as a secondary, exploratory analysis to determine which features best separate MCI from healthy aging, demonstrating that the choices in study design are purposeful, interpretable, and feasible within the scope of a pilot project.

﻿Elevation Reflection & Engagement 
GL95
Suisei Nakagawa


After reviewing the myMinerva city pages for your upcoming rotation city, and engaging with the Cultural Specific pre-readings for Elevation shared by the City Teams information answer the following two questions:


1. What was one thing you learned or were surprised by in reviewing the cultural specific pre-readings? (100 words)

I was surprised by the way the distinction between business and private seems to be vague, such as how dinner is a common setting for professional discussion, or the emphasis on socializing and staying in touch outside of work. I was also not expecting the very physically close nature of interactions, like a smaller personal space, kissing as a greeting, and the preference for in-person interactions, especially having been in SF for 8 months, where people tended to keep their distance and virtual meetings were the norm. 


2. How do you plan to engage in the city in a way that helps you make sense of the city and pushes you past surface level understanding? (150 words)


I hope to participate in a Civic Project to get a better understanding of professional culture in Argentina and expand my network beyond the Minerva community. I also hope that building relationships with locals will give me the opportunity to practice Spanish and graduate from using Google Translate for everything. I am considering joining a local bouldering gym, ideally with small-group classes, to meet new people from Buenos Aires and make friends over shared interests. 


In addition, having been in San Francisco where tech, especially AI, was the dominant industry and companies ranged from niche pre-seed startup to multi-billion-dollar corporations, I’m also excited to discover more of what industries and business models thrive in Argentina. I’m curious to see how local economics and culture shape the professional landscape.

name: Ethnographic Assignment [final]
text_raw:
﻿Ethnographic Assignment




Minerva University
GL95: Global Learning
October 30, 2025












________________


Ethnographic Assignment
Introduction
For this assignment, I interviewed Franco Marsico, a professor at the Universidad de Buenos Aires (UBA) and my civic partner. I have been working on his open-source tool for using statistical inference to find and identify missing persons, and I had the pleasure of meeting with him regularly in UBA. 
Language as a Tool for Professional Interaction
 Franco obtained his PhD in Computational Biology at UBA, where he now works as a professor. He works in the Master's Program in the Computational Science department, where he teaches statistical learning, machine learning, and large-scale data analysis for applications in business, industry, and research.
In our conversation, he emphasized how language, particularly English, has shaped his professional trajectory. Franco speaks Argentine Spanish as his native language and English primarily for academic and collaborative purposes. He considers this bilingual ability a practical necessity rather than a personal preference, explaining that many international conferences, journals, and research partnerships still operate predominantly in English. 
He described how his command of English has directly expanded his professional opportunities, enabling him to connect with international colleagues and participate in cross-institutional projects. Each year, he spends roughly four months in the United States, visiting universities and research centers to co-develop projects with their faculty. Franco explained that these exchanges not only help him access new technologies and methodologies but also expose him to different academic cultures. 
Professional Culture in Argentine Academics 
Franco described Argentine academic culture as “friendly but serious,” a mix that he feels distinguishes it from the more rigidly structured systems he has observed abroad. He noted that academics in Argentina tend to be approachable, often fostering informal yet respectful relationships with their students and colleagues. He believes this friendliness reflects broader national social norms, as Argentines value warmth and personal connection even in professional settings. He contrasted this with the U.S., where professional interactions, though polite and collegial, often retain more distance. He also notes that, while the friendly atmosphere does foster closeness, this does not undermine rigor or discipline. Instead, it creates a more collaborative atmosphere that supports creativity and open dialogue.
Franco also pointed out that despite UBA’s status as a leading institution in Latin America, there is a noticeable absence of elitism in everyday interactions. Professors and students alike tend to downplay hierarchy, focusing instead on shared learning and mutual respect. This cultural trait strengthens the sense of community within the department and makes mentorship more accessible. He believes this environment helps students feel comfortable seeking help and builds trust that supports long-term academic relationships. In his view, professional prestige in Argentina is not expressed through authority or distance, but through knowledge, generosity, and collegial engagement.
Conclusion
Through my conversation with Franco and my own observations at UBA, it became clear that professionalism in Argentine academia is defined as much by human connection as by expertise. Language enables access to the global stage, but it is the country’s distinctive warm, collaborative, and grounded academic culture that sustains it.


Word Count: 500 words

name: Mock Peer Review [final]
text_raw:
﻿Peer Review of Factors influencing chopstick use and an objective identification of traditional holding techniques in children
SS110
Only to the Editor
Recommendation: Accept pending major revisions
I think this paper could be a great contribution, especially given the cultural significance, if the efforts were more focused on one goal: either developing an objective evaluation method for chopstick use or investigating factors that influence chopstick proficiency in children. For the former, I am unsure if I am qualified to assess the strength of their model given my lack of background in image classification models. For the latter, I believe that there are many alternative explanations for the decline in chopstick proficiency and the collected data does not suffice to draw conclusions from. My major suggestions will thus be to hone in on one of the aims and introduce more rigor into the data collection and analysis.[1][2]
Seen by Authors of the Manuscript 
Summary of Paper
In this paper, the authors assess the hand posture of children when handling chopsticks under two objectives: developing a hand posture estimation model for objective evaluation and analyzing the cause of the decline in children’s chopstick proficiency. The model was confirmed to have high accuracy, indicating potential for use in chopstick education. They also report a significant relationship between grandparent-grandchild cohabitation and chopstick proficiency, in addition to a discrepancy between self-reported chopstick instruction by parents and actual proficiency in children. These suggest that the decline may be attributed to the lack of intergenerational transmission of chopstick techniques. 
While I appreciate the consideration of the socio-cultural influences on chopstick proficiency, I am somewhat concerned about the strength of the conclusions drawn due to the following concerns in the methodology.
Design and Data Collection
The study population is sampled from a single city and recruited at the city hall, which may introduce selection bias. Families engaging with civic services at the city hall or residing in Murakami City may not be representative of first-graders in Japan in terms of socio-economic or educational background. In addition, the administered survey may lead parents to overreport chopstick proficiency in themselves and their children due to recall and social desirability bias given the instrumental role of chopsticks in Japanese culinary culture. This could further contribute to the discrepancy between the parents’ assessment of chopstick proficiency and actual skill in both generations.[3][4] 
An ideal design would avoid these biases by supplementing the evaluation of the parents’s chopstick proficiency with an objective measure, and considering multiple sites across diverse regions and other random sampling methods for subject recruitment. 
Statistics and Data Analysis
The statistical analysis raises concerns about researcher degrees of freedom.[5] For example, the section on data analysis mentions “balanc[ing] the dataset,” but I am worried about the obscurity of the transformations and processing that went on in this procedure. I would like to see an explanation of “scaling and translations” in context and a discussion of the limitations posed by this on the model. While I am unfamiliar with the MediaPipe library, I suspect that artificially inflating the number of traditional grips leads to overfitting of the model in classifying traditional grips, or perhaps overweighing features of the grips prevalent in the augmented dataset in the decision tree model- both of which may undermine the reported performance metrics (Lugaresi et al., 2019). Furthermore, there should be more justification for the choice to process the data this way. 
In addition to the training of the model, I believe there should be more explanation of the rationale behind the chosen classifications and how they align with the “traditional” grip.[6] The classification defined in Yokubo et al. (2021), developed specifically for children, had three categories, whereas the model in this paper includes an “other.” This raises questions such as how strictly the classification was followed, and if not, how were the criteria modified and why. The paper also aggregates the palm prehension grip into the “other” category with little justification, which is especially surprising given that this amounts to a combined 31.5% of the subjects. If this is a limitation of the model, it should be more explicitly discussed as a potential direction for improvement. 
Results and Conclusions
It is identified in the results and discussion that grandparent-grandchild cohabitation is a significant predictor of chopstick grip in multinomial logistic regression. However, inferring from this the intergenerational transmission of chopstick techniques as a factor contributing to chopstick proficiency may be a stretch. There could be alternative explanations for the correlation, such as increased adult attention, or unaddressed confounds such as socioeconomic status or even foods commonly eaten in the household. As the correlation may be due to a multitude of other factors, the relationship should be framed as a correlation as opposed to implying causality. Collecting data on confounds would also strengthen the analysis. 
Additionally, the paper concludes with a call for the application of the model in a web application to support chopstick education. While the decline in chopstick proficiency is a foundational concern that largely motivates the study, the sudden introduction of this recommendation feels disconnected from the findings of the study. 
________________
Analysis and Commentary on the Peer Review Process
The experience of writing a peer review confirmed my prior knowledge and intuition that the process is highly subject to bias due to personal areas of expertise and illustrated how prone to oversight reviewers may be. This may be somewhat unavoidable given that reviewers will not necessarily be experts on the exact subject matter, and some finer details may not be as evident. This is a major limitation that may be difficult to address without sufficient incentive for reviewers to devote time and effort to learn about the context and conduct a thorough review. 
Thus, I have come to understand that the peer review process serves as a procedure not to dissect the motivations and content of the study, but rather to assess the rigor and reliability of the methodology. As the reviewer, also being a scientist from the discipline, must also be well-versed in research methods, having appropriate review guidelines that focus on robust methods over novelty can enable the peer review process to work as a safeguard against faulty research. 


Word Count: 1030 words
________________
References
Lugaresi, C., Tang, J., Nash, H., McClanahan, C., Uboweja, E., Hays, M., Zhang, F., Chang, C.-L., Yong, M. G., Lee, J., Chang, W.-T., Hua, W., Georg, M., & Grundmann, M. (2019). MediaPipe: A Framework for Building Perception Pipelines. ArXiv:1906.08172 [Cs]. https://arxiv.org/abs/1906.08172
Yokubo, Y. K., Ota, T., & Shibata, K. (2021). Relationship between chopstick manipulation and cross-sectional shape in the developmental stages from infancy to early school age. Applied Ergonomics, 97, 103507. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2021.103507
________________
Project Navigation Appendix
I approached this assignment in a similar manner to the CX and EA assignments I worked on in my first year. One notable difference is that I allocated significant time to understand the material, as the course does not cover the paper itself. The following bulleted list illustrates my process.

New things I tried:
