﻿Technical Interview and Self-Reflection




Minerva University
CS114: Probability, Statistics, and the Structure of Randomness
Prof. E. Volkan
April 25, 2025
















________________
Technical Interview and Self-Reflection
Feedback
I received a comment in Problem Set 2 on a question about the joint distribution resulting from answering a mix of easy and hard questions with different probabilities of answering correctly. The question asked to compare this joint distribution to the distribution of correct answers if all of the questions had the same probability of answering correctly. The comment was “The two distribution has the same mean but different variances. But there is more to that. Why are the two distributions different. Xe and Xh in X has different probabilities while in Y they don't. The former is Poisson Binomial the second is Binomial.” 
This was somewhat surprising, as I had thought that I answered correctly: the distributions are different based on their different variances and shapes. Based on this comment, I realized that while I was right to say the distributions are different, I had focused on mathematically deriving the differences and failed to notice that the difference in the data-generating process leads to two distinct distributions. 
In response to this feedback, I made an effort to focus on the data-generating process to guess the model that generated the data in my LBA. As I was working with the length of my sleep, this led me to carefully consider the circumstances that influence when I go to bed and wake up, to conclude that the distribution may follow the normal or a mixture of normals. 
Curiosities
I am curious how probability and statistics are applied in psychology research, as this is my other major, and I am interested in the intersection of data and social science research. Specifically, I am interested in the use of distributions in nested models, such as those used in in-participant comparisons across groups. I read a paper discussing parameter estimation for an SS110 assignment, and learned that nested models (the paper used hierarchical Bayesian modeling) are used to separate population-level trends from individual-level variation when trials are not independent. I am also curious about how researchers use statistical inference to infer the amount of randomness and individual variability to consider in order to identify patterns in behavior without overfitting noise. 
AI Use
I used AI to explain concepts from the Blitzstein textbook when I was doing PCW, as the textbook used a lot of technical jargon that was introduced earlier in the book that I had forgotten. This helped me both with reinforcing previously covered topics in my memory and situating them in the context of more advanced material. I believe that if you pre-train AI models to explain and not solve questions, they can be helpful in breaking down complicated ideas to make them easier to understand. Another possible productive use case could be formatting LaTeX and debugging code, or having AI write the less-important parts of code, such as creating visualizations. Conversely, having AI models solve the problems in PCW or Problem Sets altogether or write entire simulations would detract from learning, and they should not be used in this way.

﻿Technical Interview and Self-Reflection




Minerva University
CS113: Theory and Applications of Linear Algebra
Prof. L. Levitt
April 18, 2025
















________________
Technical Interview and Self-Reflection
Challenging Problem
One of the particularly challenging questions I worked on was the question in the Systems of Linear Equations Deep Dive on an optional question about the Leontief economic model, where I had to consider the constraints on the Leontief matrix M such that the economy is productive. The question required me to consider real-world constraints not only on the individual entries of the matrix, but also on its algebraic behavior, so I had to revisit early concepts like invertibility. I found this particularly difficult as I had come to over-rely on Sage functions to solve problems, and my understanding of such fundamental ideas was insufficient. The question thus served as an opportunity to reflect on my foundational knowledge. 
Feedback
I received a score on #cs113-LinearSystems in the Systems of Linear Equations Deep Dive on a question about the Leontief economic model with the comment “This isn't correct and is also lacking economic interpretation. [...] This means that the real estate industry consumes all that it produces and nobody else demands any. Thus, you could produce as much as you want, which is why there are infinitely many solutions.”
This highlighted the importance of situating solutions in the context of the problem and doing “sanity checks” to see if it makes sense before I convince myself that I solved the problem. This informed the development of my code in the following Vector Space Structure Deep Dive, where I developed an algorithm to compress images using change-of-basis matrices. My original function simply received a Sage matrix object as input and returned the compressed matrix. For larger matrices, it was difficult to visually check if it was compressed correctly, as I was essentially just comparing many numbers. Thus, I modified the function to take a raw image and output the compressed image in order to make pre- to post-compression comparisons easier. This allowed me to catch a crucial bug that was causing the image to be mirrored across the diagonal, and led me to revise the math and underlying logic. 
Curiosities
I am curious about practical applications of linear algebra not covered in class, especially those used in psychology research, because it directly relates to my other major and intended career path. I did some research about MANCOVA for my SS110 class, where multiple dependent variables are analyzed across different groups using matrices. I also read about text sentiment analysis used to encode open-ended questions and dialogue, which is also based on linear algebra. I am curious about the math underlying these statistical methods commonly used in data analysis, the results of which tell us so much about human behavior.

text_raw:
﻿Ethnographic Assignment




Minerva University
GL94: Global Learning
February 23, 2024












________________


Ethnographic Assignment
Introduction
For this assignment I interviewed Celeste Alaniz Campos, a student at SF State who lives in San Francisco. I met them through 826 Valencia, a local non-profit where we both volunteer as writing tutors. We had been working on the same program for the past five weeks at a local high school. 
Background and Language
Celeste grew up in the desert region of Lancaster, CA, where they lived until moving to SF for school in 2022. Their parents are from two different parts of Mexico, and while they both have a “traditional” upbringing that emphasizes gender roles, have been striving to break the cycle for Celeste and their siblings. Celeste grew up with more collectivist and family-oriented views compared to broader US society, where they notice a disconnect between individual decisions and their surroundings. While this difference makes it somewhat difficult for them to navigate social life in the States (“What do you mean you can’t go out tonight because you’re saving up to help with your family’s bills? Didn’t you move out?”), they appreciate the closeness and security this fosters, especially given all that their parents have done to make them feel supported in their academic and professional pursuits regardless of how they fit into their cultural norms. For example, Celeste’s parents do not conform to traditional gender roles where the father is the breadwinner and the mother is a homemaker, and instead share financial and family responsibilities. This has helped set a good example for Celeste, who now makes a point of not settling for anything less than a relationship dynamic where both partners are willing to put in the work. 
Celeste was raised in a Spanish-speaking household as a second-generation immigrant. They learned English at around four years old, and feel a strong division between their Spanish- and English-speaking selves: their cultural world and family life revolve around Spanish, but their professional, academic, and much of their social life centers on English. They note how their inner monologue is in Spanish, and how some small, day-to-day tasks like counting just “don’t make sense” in English. They also consider Spanish as pivotal in maintaining their cultural identity as a Chicana. 
Celeste self-identifies as Chicana, a term commonly used to describe Mexican Americans in SoCal. Coming from the reclaimed racial slur “Mechica,” the word originated as part of a movement to decolonize Mexican culture and reclaim Indigenous roots. They prefer this term over “Latina,” as they feel that the nod to Latin culture lends to white supremacy even though the Latinx community is far more diverse. They also feel that Chicana is a more organic representation of their background of being born in the US, and is a form of mourning not quite belonging to American or Mexican culture. 
Family Rituals and Values
Celeste’s family and close friends have a tradition of pooling funds as a de facto joint savings account. They would set aside some sum each pay cycle, and draw lots with a number representing when they will collect the funds from their “account.” This tradition, they reflect, arose as a form of mutual aid, doubling as an accountability measure for saving up and an emergency fund and loan alternative. The idea of mutual aid within Celeste’s community goes beyond monetary help. Their people and family are willing to extend a helping hand to those in need: for example, when Celeste’s partner did not have the funds to repair their car, Celeste’s parents covered the costs until they could get back on their feet and repay them. 
These strong community ties and values also translate into Celeste’s work as an activist. Celeste has been planning events and protests for issues that directly and disproportionately affect their community, such as mass incarceration, which their brother was also impacted by, policing, and immigration. While progress isn’t linear and each breakthrough feels like it’s followed by a setback, Celeste is hopeful that their work to platform and amplify voices will lead to system-level changes for the better. 
Conclusion
Celeste’s account has allowed me to better understand how cultural identity, especially in a diverse country like the US, serves as a foundation to inform how you approach connections on both a smaller, individual scale such as with friends, and on a broader level, such as with entire communities when jointly facing societal issues. Coming from a relatively homogenous country like Japan, this was especially insightful to help me see how I in turn can center myself on my own heritage and culture.  


Word Count: 754 words

Final Project: Decoding the Relationships Between Genes
        This paper discusses my implementation of a solution to find the longest common subsequence (LCS) from a set of strings, and a practical application of the solution: inferring the relationships between genes.
Python Implementation
I implemented my solution to find the LCS in a Python program. The code is accessible in the .zip folder attached as a secondary resource and included in the appendix.
Results: LCS Matrix
I generated a matrix len_lcs_matrix that stores the length of the LCS for each pair of strings in set_strings. The resulting LCS matrix is shown in Table 1.

Inferring the Relationship Between Strings from the LCS Matrix
As genes duplicate and undergo mutation, each character in the respective string has a chance of changing, being deleted, or having another character inserted in front of it. Thus, we can expect the ‘child’ genes to have ‘inherited’ characters from its ‘parent’ in the same sequence. Therefore, we can use the LCS length as a proxy to evaluate string similarity, where a high LCS indicates a closer relationship. 
Inferring the Genealogy Binary Tree from the LCS Matrix
We can infer that the root of the genealogy binary tree will be the most related to all other strings, or in other words, have the longest LCS lengths with all other strings. From the matrix, we can see that the two longest LCSs excluding the diagonal where each string is crossed with itself are both in row ‘a’, which also has larger numbers in general. Thus, we can conclude that the root is ‘a’. As a has the two longest LCSs with ‘d’ and ‘f’, these strings must be its children. Similarly, out of the unassigned strings, ‘d’ has the closest relationships with ‘b’ and ‘e’. Thus, the children of ‘d’ must be ‘b’ and ‘e’, and the remaining ‘g’ and ‘c’ must be the children of ‘f’.
Video Detailing Algorithmic Strategy to Infer Genealogy Tree
The video is accessible at this Google Drive link.
Results: Genealogy Tree
        I expect the local and global approaches to yield different trees. To explain this, I will use the notion of degrees of kinship, which describes how many steps there are between relatives. Immediate relatives, such as parents or children, are of the first degree, siblings and grandparents that require another step are of the second degree, and so forth. In the context of inferring a genealogy tree from a set of strings representing genes, we can expect relationships within the same degree to have a similar LCS length as they are an equal distance away from the string of interest.
Similar to the manual inference above, the local approach grows the tree depth-first, choosing the string that is the most similar to all other strings as the root. As it completes branches by choosing the two children before moving on to the next, we discount the possibility that strings in subsequent branches may have higher similarity with strings in the current branch, leading to sub-optimal placing. It also tends to cluster very similar strings, which may correctly represent immediate relatives, but leave the remaining strings to form only loosely related branches. Furthermore, the approach is limiting as it does not explore structures with a different root. 
As the global solution is guaranteed to find the optimal tree that maximizes overall similarity, I expect the results to be different between the local approach, which may arrive at a sub-optimal solution.
The results are shown in Figures 1 and 2. As expected, the tree structures differ across the approaches. The local approach aligns with the manual inference as expected given that they follow the same algorithmic strategy. Furthermore, the total similarity score computed as the sum of LCS lengths between each parent-child relationship is higher for the global approach, suggesting that the local approach produced a sub-optimal tree.
Figure 1

        Left: d
                Right: e

        Right: f
                Right: c

Figure 2

                Right: f

	Complexity Analysis of Tree Inference
Generating the LCS Matrix
The time complexity of generate_lcs_matrix() function depends on the length of the strings passed, , and the number of strings, . As seen in Figure 3, it creates an empty 2D numpy array to store the LCS length for each combination of strings, costing . The nested loops also run  times. 
The lcs_table() function uses a table to store the length of the LCS for consecutive subsequences in x and y. As the function initializes and populates all  sections, the complexity is .
Therefore, the generate_lcs_matrix() function has an overall complexity of .
Figure 3
The generate_lcs_matrix() and lcs_table() functions
def lcs_table(x, y):
   m = len(x)
   n = len(y)
   dp = [[0 for _ in range(n + 1)] for _ in range(m + 1)]
      
   for i in range(1, m + 1):
       for j in range(1, n + 1):
           if x[i - 1] == y[j - 1]: 
               dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j - 1] + 1
           elif dp[i - 1][j] >= dp[i][j - 1]:
               dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j]
           else:
               dp[i][j] = dp[i][j - 1]
   return dp

def generate_lcs_matrix(set_strings):
   len_lcs_matrix = [[0 for _ in range(len(set_strings))] for _ in range(len(set_strings))]


   for i in range(len(set_strings)):
       for j in range(len(set_strings)):
           len_lcs_matrix[i][j] = lcs_table(set_strings[i][1], set_strings[j][1])[-1][-1]
           len_lcs_matrix[j][i] = len_lcs_matrix[i][j]


   return len_lcs_matrix


The Local Approach
As seen above, the first few lines in the local approach, seen in Figure 4, that generate the matrix costs  . Finding the root requires traversing the entire matrix, costing . The other lines assign variables, contributing .
The while loop processes each string to find its two most similar children, checking  strings in the for loop per each of the  nodes. This results in an  complexity as it does  assignment operations based on conditionals.
Thus, the local approach costs  overall.
Figure 4
The local approach
def local_infer_tree(set_strings):
   len_lcs_matrix = generate_lcs_matrix(set_strings)
   tree = BinaryTree()
   root_index = max(range(len(set_strings)), key=lambda i: sum(len_lcs_matrix[i]))
   tree.root = Node(set_strings[root_index][0])
   nodes_to_process = [tree.root]
   processed_indices = {root_index}
   total_similarity_score = 0


   while nodes_to_process:
       current_node = nodes_to_process.pop(0)
       current_index = next(i for i, (name, _) in enumerate(set_strings) if name == current_node.name)
       left_index = right_index = None
       max_left_similarity = max_right_similarity = -1


       for i in range(len(set_strings)):
           if i not in processed_indices:
               similarity = len_lcs_matrix[current_index][i]
               if similarity > max_left_similarity:
                   max_right_similarity = max_left_similarity
                   right_index = left_index
                   max_left_similarity = similarity
                   left_index = i
               elif similarity > max_right_similarity:
                   max_right_similarity = similarity
                   right_index = i


       if left_index is not None:
           left_node = Node(set_strings[left_index][0], current_node.depth + 1)
           current_node.left = left_node
           nodes_to_process.append(left_node)
           processed_indices.add(left_index)
           total_similarity_score += max_left_similarity


       if right_index is not None:
           right_node = Node(set_strings[right_index][0], current_node.depth + 1)
           current_node.right = right_node
           nodes_to_process.append(right_node)
           processed_indices.add(right_index)
           total_similarity_score += max_right_similarity


   return tree, total_similarity_score




The Global Approach
The generate_permutations() Auxiliary Function.
This auxiliary function seen in Figure 5 generates all possible permutations of the input list in order to find all possible tree structures using brute-force. As the function does assignment operations in  for each of the  permutations, the auxiliary function costs .
Figure 5
The generate_permutations() function
   def generate_permutations(arr):
       if len(arr) == 0:
           return [[]]
       permutations = []
       for i in range(len(arr)):
           rest = arr[:i] + arr[i+1:]
           for perm in generate_permutations(rest):
               permutations.append([arr[i]] + perm)
       return permutations


The calculate_similarity() Auxiliary function.
For each permutation, we calculate the total similarity score between each parent-child relationship by iterating over each of the  strings and querying its similarity with its child from the matrix in  time, as seen in Figure 6. The overall cost of this auxiliary function is .
Figure 6
The calculate_similarity() function
   def calculate_similarity(perm):
       score = 0
       for i in range(n):
           if 2*i + 1 < n:
               score += len_lcs_matrix[perm[i]][perm[2*i + 1]]
           if 2*i + 2 < n:
               score += len_lcs_matrix[perm[i]][perm[2*i + 2]]
       return score




Overall Time Complexity.
As the global approach first generates the LCS matrix in  time, and generates permutations costing . It then loops through all of these, making the loop run  times, to calculate the total similarity costing  and comparing it to best_score and potentially updating it. Thus, the first for loop costs . It then constructs the tree based on the permutation that yields the highest total similarity, which costs  to traverse the permutation, creating a node for each string, and assigning them a position in the tree.
Therefore, the overall complexity of the brute-force global approach is +.

   len_lcs_matrix = generate_lcs_matrix(set_strings)
   n = len(set_strings)


   best_score = -1
   best_permutation = None
   permutations = generate_permutations(list(range(n)))
   for perm in permutations:

           best_score = score

       if 2*i + 2 < n:
           nodes[i].right = nodes[2*i + 2]


   tree = BinaryTree()
   tree.root = nodes[0]
   return tree, best_score

	Probability Estimation for Gene Mutation, Insertion, and Deletion
Let us assume that gene y is the ‘child’ of gene x. Given that the LCS length represents the number of characters two strings x and y have in common, we know that:
*  Any character present in x but not in the LCS must have either:
   * Been deleted, or
   * Mutated into another character
* Any character present in y but not in the LCS must have either:
   * Been inserted, or
   * Been mutated from another character. 
Algorithmic Approach to Estimation
Given the above relationships, the number of each type of mutation needed to obtain string y from x can be found by:
* Mutations: traversing x and y, identifying and counting positions where characters following those in the LCS are mismatches
   * E.g., for  x: “TAACAGT”, y: “TATTGTA”, with the LCS: “TAGT”, the first two characters “TA” match, but the following characters are “ACA” in x and “TT” in y.
* Deletions: len(x) - num_mutations - LCS_length
* Insertions: len(y) - num_mutations - LCS_length
        For example, let us consider:
* x: “TAACAGT”
* y: “ATATTGT”
* LCS: “TAGT”
We begin by aligning the strings based on the LCS as seen in Table 2:
Table 2
Aligned strings based on LCS

	A

	G

We first note that there is a gap in the LCS: “ACA” in x and “TT” in y. For “AC” that have characters in the corresponding position in y, we assume mutation. The first character in y, “A” does not have a character in the corresponding position in y. Thus, we assume it had been deleted. Finally, the remaining “A” in the gap of the LCS does not have a corresponding character in x, so we assume insertion. Therefore, there had been 2 mutations, 1 deletion, and 1 insertion.
Thus, to identify the number of each operation that took place when a ‘parent’ gene x duplicated into a ‘child’ gene y, we can use a three-pointer method that simultaneously traverses x, y, and the LCS to apply this algorithmic approach, seen in Figure 14. We can then find the probability of a single character undergoing each process by dividing the number of operations that occurred by the number of characters processed, i.e. double the number of characters in parent genes as each parent has two children. 
Figure 14
Function to find the number of each mutation based on parent, child, and LCS
def count_operations(parent, child, lcs):
   p, c, l = 0, 0, 0
   mutations = 0
   deletions = 0
   insertions = 0


   while p < len(parent) and c < len(child) and l < len(lcs):
       if parent[p] == lcs[l] and child[c] == lcs[l]:
           p += 1
           c += 1
           l += 1
       elif parent[p] == lcs[l]:
           c += 1
       elif child[c] == lcs[l]:
           p += 1
       else:
           p += 1
           c += 1
           mutations += 1


   deletions = len(parent) - mutations - len(lcs)
   insertions = len(child) - mutations - len(lcs)


   return mutations, deletions, insertions, len(parent)
Results: Probabilities of Gene Mutation, Insertion, and Deletion
Using the genealogy tree inferred using the global strategy, the found probabilities are shown in Figure 15. As the total is much smaller than 1, we can conclude that the probability of a given character mutating is sufficiently small for the ‘child’ gene to retain much of the characters in the ‘parent’. Thus, it can be said that these are reasonable estimates.

Part I: Reflection
My algorithmic strategy was informed by the discussion on DP in session 21 and the LCS problem from session 24. The DP approach to finding the LCS informed my decision to use tabulation and LCS reconstruction separately. Using tabulation helped me optimize my solution, which helped given the long strings in set_strings. In addition, the readings provided insight into how the backtracking to reconstruct the LCS can be modified to consider all possible LCSs. (74  words)


PartII: LO and HC Applications

(47 words)

I contrast the empirical results with the theoretical complexity by analyzing expected scaling behavior and discuss potential reasons for discrepancies. (46 words)
   * Previous feedback: “You could increase your use of evidence to demonstrate the empirical plots corroborate theoretical findings,” addressed by identifying how the algorithms behave as expected at larger n
* #cs110-ComputationalCritique: I contrasted the theoretical complexity of the plot with empirical results, discussing how my local and global strategies for tree inference differ in scaling. I also discussed how I expected the two approaches to produce different results due to the sub-optimal nature of the local approach. (45 words)
* #cs110-CodeReadability: I produced modular, readable code that follows PEP8 conventions and used appropriate, descriptive names for variables and classes. I also used docstrings to concisely describe my functions as opposed to over-commenting and subtracting from flow and readability. (37 words)

* #professionalism: I was careful to proofread my assignment and follow instructions in full. I was also careful to demonstrate full understanding of the concepts in class using supporting Figures and code snippets in a professional formatting. (35 words)
* #breakitdown: I decomposed the algorithm into smaller self-contained steps that informed my decisions on how to refactor my code and make it more modular. Specifically, I identified processes (like generating the LCS matrix, both to find all LCSs and to optimize the tree reconstruction) that I would reuse throughout the assignment and intentionally coded separate functions for them.  (57 words)
   * Previous feedback: “Consider how the HC focuses on justifying why this specific decomposition is particularly effective, but also the re-assembling,” addressed by intentionally making code modular in a way that I could reuse only the required functionality. For example, I could have hard-coded the tabulation into my function to find all LCSs, but I avoided this with the intention to use tabulation elsewhere.

Analysis
Critique of the Scheduler
The scheduler provided an overall feasible schedule. It allowed me to focus on the task at hand without thinking about what comes next, and avoid procrastination as I was working under a deadline. It also helped me get to all of the tasks within the day rather than putting them off for another day.
However, the scheduler failed to handle tasks that are technically not time-fixed but have to be completed within a reasonable timeframe, such as having dinner around 18:00 to 20:00 so that the restaurants are still open. 
Efficiency of the Schedule
The schedule has room for improvement in terms of efficiency in terms of the percentage of the total time spend actually completing tasks, as there is a gap at 14:55~16:00 that could not be filled even though there were flexible tasks with no incomplete dependencies in the priority queue for flexible tasks. The scheduler completes the tasks in 14 hours and 40 minutes, so the gap comprises  of the total time taken to complete all tasks. 
Efficiency of the Algorithm
The theoretical complexity of the scheduler algorithm depends on the relationships of dependency between tasks, the order of input, and the uniqueness of priority values. Here we will consider the complexity under the following assumption that there are no cyclical dependencies (e.g. Task 1 depends on Task 2 and Task 2 depends on Task 1). This assumption is necessary as it will cause an infinite loop when finding the priorities, but is reasonable since tasks cannot have cyclical dependencies in real life.
The following analysis focuses on individual parts of the class implementation and the run() method.  represents the number of input tasks,  the average number of dependencies, and  the height of the heaps. I used Big-O notation to represent an upper bound on the runtime’s scaling growth as a tight bound cannot be found. This is because the precise complexity of methods associated with max-heaps, which the algorithm is based on, depends on the height of the heap, which cannot be found unless we know the specific input characteristics. In addition, the nature of the dependencies also influences the asymptotic behavior, and we cannot be sure of this factor as well.
The __init__() method. 
As seen in Figure 4, the __init__() method sets attributes directly (costing  for assigning a value or initializing an empty MaxHeapq instance with two default attributes), creates a dictionary (costing ) and calls the methods find_dependents() and calculate_priorities(). The find_dependents() method looks at each dependency of each task, and appends the task’s id to its dependent’s dependent attribute, costing . This could cost up to  if  is proportionate to .
The calculate_task_priority() and calculate_priorities() methods together cost . This is because each task calculate_task_priority() will be called once for each task, and there can be up to  recursive calls in the case that the dependencies form a chain.
Thus, the __init__() method costs

       self.tasks = tasks
       self.task_dict = {task.id: task for task in tasks}
       self.start_time = start_time

       self.flexible_heap = MaxHeapq()
       self.fixed_heap = MaxHeapq()
       self.next_fixed_task_time = None
       self.find_dependents()
       self.calculate_priorities()
       
   def find_dependents(self):
       for task in self.tasks:
           task.dependents = []
       for task in self.tasks:
           for dep_id in task.dependencies:
               if dep_id in self.task_dict:
                   self.task_dict[dep_id].dependents.append(task.id)


   def calculate_priorities(self):

       if task.fixed_time is not None:

       priority = task.duration
       for dep_id in task.dependents:

               if self.flexible_heap.heap_size > 0 and self.flexible_heap.max_task().duration <= time_left:

                   task = self.fixed_heap.heappop()
                   self.update_next_fixed_task_time()
           else:

                   task = self.flexible_heap.heappop()
               else:
                   break

           current_time += task.duration

           task.status = self.COMPLETED

Overall time complexity. 
Based on the above analysis, initializing a MyScheduler object and calling the run() method has a time complexity of  , as the scaling growth is dominated by the  complexity of get_tasks_ready() in each of the  iterations of the while loop in run(). Thus, the runtime is largely dependent on the height of the heaps, which could cause the methods of the MaxHeapq class to cost up to  when the heaps form chain-like structures and  for balanced heaps. 
Thus, the worst-case complexity will be , occurring when the heaps are unbalanced and . This would be when:
* The order of insertion is in decreasing order, or
* Individual tasks have identical priorities
The average- and best-case complexity will be , occurring when the heaps are mostly balanced and . This would be when:
* The order of insertion is not in decreasing order, and
* Individual tasks have mostly unique priorities

Potential Improvements
        The scheduler was demonstrated to be inefficient in the sense that it failed to schedule tasks in a way that resulted in minimal time between tasks. This can be improved by looking further into the flexible_heap until either an executable task is found, or it is confirmed that there are no tasks that can be completed in the remaining time. However, this defeats the purpose of implementing a priority queue to query the root at , as traversing the heap requires a more costly operation of . 
        In addition, the scheduler can be made more efficient by replacing the priority queue for time-fixed tasks with a list that stores tasks in order of latest to earliest start time. As the re-heapifying process after a task is completed dominates the time complexity, maintaining one heap instead of two will help optimize the algorithm. Using such a list will still allow us to use the pop() operation to pop the next fixed task off the end of the list in  time, and update the time of the next fixed task using indexing with -1 in  time, while avoiding the worst-case of maintaining a chain-like heap as a resulting from input passed in descending order. Using a sorted list will cost  (Jain, 2024) if sorted with the built-in sort() operation at the setup, but will not incur additional costs in the main loop of run().
        Furthermore, the scheduler cannot run if the input contains cyclic dependencies as this will cause the priority calculation to fall into an infinite loop. Thus, the MyScheduler class could benefit from a method that identifies any cyclic dependencies and prompts the user to correct them. This can be achieved by implementing a depth-first search algorithm that graphs the dependencies. (299 words)

# code adapted from session 13 PCW (Nakagawa, 2024b)
# original Maxheapq class modified to store Task instances


class MaxHeapq:
   """
   A class that implements properties and methods
   that support a max priority queue data structure for Task objects.


   Attributes
   ----------
   heap : list
       A Python list where Task objects in the max heap are stored.
   heap_size: int
       An integer counter of the number of Task objects present in the max heap.
   """ 


   def __init__(self):   
       """
       Parameters
       ----------
       None
       """   
       self.heap       = []
       self.heap_size  = 0
      
   def left(self, i):
       """
       Takes the index of the parent node and returns the index of the left child node


       Parameters
       ----------
       i: int
         Index of parent node


       Returns
       ----------
       int
         Index of the left child node
       """
       return 2 * i + 1


   def right(self, i):
       """
       Takes the index of the parent node and returns the index of the right child node
      
       Parameters
       ----------
       i: int
           Index of parent node


       Returns
       ----------
       int
           Index of the right child node
       """
       return 2 * i + 2
      
   def parent(self, i):
       """
       Takes the index of the child node and returns the index of the parent node
      
       Parameters
       ----------
       i: int
           Index of child node


       Returns
       ----------
       int
           Index of the parent node
       """
       return (i - 1)//2


   def max_task(self):    
       """
       Returns the highest priority Task in the priority queue.
      
       Parameters
       ----------
       None


       Returns
       ----------
       Task or None
           The Task with the highest priority in the priority queue, or None if the queue is empty.
       """
       return self.heap[0] if self.heap_size > 0 else None        
    def heappush(self, task): 
       """
       Inserts a Task into the priority queue.
      
       Parameters
       ----------
       task: Task
           The Task object to be inserted.


       Returns
       ----------
       None
       """
       self.heap.append(Task(-1, "", -1, float('-inf')))
       self.increase_key(self.heap_size,task)
       self.heap_size+=1
      
   def increase_key(self, i, task):
       """
       Modifies the priority of a Task in the max priority queue to a higher value.
      
       Parameters
       ----------
       i: int
           The index of the Task to be modified.
       task: Task
           The new Task object with a higher priority.


       Returns
       ----------
       None


       Raises
       ----------
       ValueError
           If the new task priority is smaller than the current priority.
       """
       if task < self.heap[i]:
           raise ValueError('new task priority is smaller than the current priority')
       self.heap[i] = task
       while i > 0 and self.heap[self.parent(i)] < self.heap[i]:
           j = self.parent(i)
           self.heap[i], self.heap[j] = self.heap[j], self.heap[i]
           i = j   
     
   def heapify(self, i):
       """
       Creates a max heap from the index given
      
       Parameters
       ----------
       i: int
           The index of of the root node of the subtree to be heapify


       Returns
       ----------
       None
       """
       l = self.left(i)
       r = self.right(i)
       heap = self.heap
       if l < (self.heap_size-1) and heap[l] > heap[i]:
           largest = l
       else:
           largest = i
       if r < (self.heap_size-1) and heap[r] > heap[largest]:
           largest = r
       if largest != i:
           heap[i], heap[largest] = heap[largest], heap[i]
           self.heapify(largest)


   def heappop(self):
       """
       Returns the highest priority Task in the max priority queue and removes it from the queue.
      
       Parameters
       ----------
       None


       Returns
       ----------
       Task
           The Task with the highest priority in the heap that is extracted.


       Raises
       ----------
       ValueError
           If the heap is empty.
       """
       if self.heap_size < 1:
           raise ValueError('Heap underflow: There are no keys in the priority queue ')
       maxk = self.heap[0]
       self.heap[0] = self.heap[-1]
       self.heap.pop()
       self.heap_size-=1
       self.heapify(0)
       return maxk


class Task:
   """
   Represents a task with a unique identifier, description, duration, dependencies, dependents, status, priority, and optional fixed time.


   Attributes:
   - id (int): A unique task identifier.
   - description (str): A short description of the task.
   - duration (int): The duration of the task in minutes.
   - priority (int): The priority of the task. Default is 0.
   - dependencies (list): A list of task ids that must be completed before this task. Default is an empty list.
   - dependents (list): A list of task ids that depend on this task. Default is an empty list.
   - status (str): The current status of the task. Possible values are 'N' (not_yet_started), 'I' (in_priority_queue), or 'C' (completed). Default is 'N'.
   - fixed_time (str): An optional fixed time at which the task must be performed. Format is HHMM. Default is None.
   """


   def __init__(self, id, description, duration, priority=0,
               dependencies = [], dependents=[], status="N",
               fixed_time=None):
       self.id = id
       self.description = description
       self.duration = duration
       self.priority = priority
       self.dependencies = dependencies
       self.dependents = dependents
       self.status = status
       self.fixed_time = fixed_time




   def __lt__(self, other):
       return self.priority < other.priority   
  
   def reset_status(self):
       """
       Reset the status of the task to 'N' (not_yet_started).
       """
       self.status = "N"

# code adapted from session 13 breakouts (Nakagawa, 2024a)
# original TaskScheduler class modified to include a method to calculate task priorities and several helper methods


from models import Task, MaxHeapq


class MyScheduler:
   """
   A Daily Task Scheduler Using Priority Queues
   """
   NOT_STARTED = 'N'
   IN_PRIORITY_QUEUE = 'I'
   COMPLETED = 'C'
  
   def __init__(self, tasks, start_time, final_time=None):
       """
       Initialize the scheduler with tasks, start time, and optional final time.


       Parameters
       ----------
       tasks : list
           List of Task objects to be scheduled.
       start_time : str
           The start time in 'HHMM' format.
       final_time : str, optional
           The final time in 'HHMM' format.
       """
       self.tasks = tasks
       self.task_dict = {task.id: task for task in tasks}  # Create a dictionary for quick lookup
       self.start_time = start_time
       self.final_time = final_time
       self.flexible_heap = MaxHeapq()
       self.fixed_heap = MaxHeapq()
       self.next_fixed_task_time = None
       self.find_dependents()
       self.calculate_priorities()
      
   def convert_time_to_minutes(self, time_str):
       """
       Convert time from 'HHMM' format to minutes since midnight.


       Parameters
       ----------
       time_str : str
           Time in 'HHMM' format.


       Returns
       -------
       int
           Time in minutes since midnight.
       """
       if len(time_str) != 4 or not time_str.isdigit():
           raise ValueError("Time format should be 'HHMM'")
       hours = int(time_str[:2])
       minutes = int(time_str[2:])
       return hours * 60 + minutes
  
   def print_self(self):
       """
       Print the details of the tasks added to the scheduler.
       """
       print("Tasks added to the simple scheduler:")
       print("--------------------------------------")
       for t in self.tasks:
           print(f"➡️'{t.description}', duration = {t.duration} mins.")  
           if len(t.dependencies) > 0:
               print(f"\t ⚠️ This task depends on {[task.description for task in self.tasks if task.id in t.dependencies]}")
           if len(t.dependents) > 0:
               print(f"\t ⚠️ This task is a dependency for {[task.description for task in self.tasks if task.id in t.dependents]}")
          
   def remove_dependency(self, id):
       """
       Remove a task dependency by task ID.


       Parameters
       ----------
       id : int
           The ID of the task to remove as a dependency.
       """
       for t in self.tasks:
           if t.id != id and id in t.dependencies:
               t.dependencies.remove(id)          
          
   def get_tasks_ready(self):
       """
       Add tasks with no outstanding dependencies to the appropriate priority queue based on their status and dependencies.
       """
       for task in self.tasks:
           if task.status == self.NOT_STARTED:
               if task.fixed_time:
                   task.status = self.IN_PRIORITY_QUEUE
                   self.fixed_heap.heappush(task)
               elif not task.dependencies:
                   task.status = self.IN_PRIORITY_QUEUE
                   self.flexible_heap.heappush(task)       


   def check_unscheduled_tasks(self):
       """
       Check if there are any unscheduled tasks.


       Returns
       -------
       bool
           True if there are unscheduled tasks, False otherwise.
       """
       for task in self.tasks:
           if task.status == self.NOT_STARTED:
               return True
       return False  
  
   def format_time(self, time):
       return f"{time//60}h{time%60:02d}"
      
   def update_next_fixed_task_time(self):
       """
       Update the time for the next fixed task.
       """
       if self.fixed_heap.heap_size > 0:
           self.next_fixed_task_time = self.convert_time_to_minutes(self.fixed_heap.max_task().fixed_time)
       else:
           self.next_fixed_task_time = None
   
   def run(self, starting_time, print_output=True):
       """
       Run the scheduler to execute tasks based on their priorities and dependencies.


       Parameters
       ----------
       starting_time : str
           The starting time in 'HHMM' format.
       print_output : bool, optional
           Whether to print the output of the scheduler.


       Returns
       -------
       list
           List of task IDs that have been run.
       """       
       current_time = self.convert_time_to_minutes(starting_time)
       if print_output:
           print("Running the scheduler:\n")
       self.get_tasks_ready()
       self.update_next_fixed_task_time()


       # List to keep track of tasks that have been run
       ran_tasks = []


       # Continue running tasks until there are no more tasks to schedule
       while self.check_unscheduled_tasks() or self.fixed_heap.heap_size > 0 or self.flexible_heap.heap_size > 0:
           self.get_tasks_ready()
           # Check if there is a fixed task to run
           if self.next_fixed_task_time is not None:
               time_left = self.next_fixed_task_time - current_time
               # If there is enough time to run the next flexible task, run it
               if self.flexible_heap.heap_size > 0 and self.flexible_heap.max_task().duration <= time_left:
                   task = self.flexible_heap.heappop()
               # If there is not enough time, run the next fixed task
               else:
                   current_time = self.next_fixed_task_time
                   task = self.fixed_heap.heappop()
                   self.update_next_fixed_task_time()
           # If there is no fixed task, run the next flexible task
           else:
               if self.flexible_heap.heap_size > 0:
                   task = self.flexible_heap.heappop()
               else:
                   break  # No more tasks to schedule


           if print_output:
           # Print the task details and update the current time
               print(f"🕰t={self.format_time(current_time)}, started '{task.description}' for {task.duration} mins...")
           current_time += task.duration
           if print_output:
               print(f"\t✅ t={self.format_time(current_time)}, '{task.description}' completed with priority {task.priority} and dependents {[self.task_dict[dep_id].description for dep_id in task.dependents]}")


           # Update task status and remove it as a dependency for other tasks
           self.remove_dependency(task.id)
           task.status = self.COMPLETED


           ran_tasks.append(task.id)


       total_time = current_time - self.convert_time_to_minutes(starting_time)
       if print_output:
           print(f"\n🏁 Completed all planned tasks in {total_time//60}h{total_time%60:02d}min!")


       return ran_tasks
                 
   def find_dependents(self):
       """
       Find and set the dependents for each task.
       """
       for task in self.tasks:
           task.dependents = []


       # Iterate through the tasks and set the dependents
       for task in self.tasks:
           for dep_id in task.dependencies:
               if dep_id in self.task_dict:
                   self.task_dict[dep_id].dependents.append(task.id)


   def calculate_priorities(self):
       for task in self.tasks:
           task.priority = self.calculate_task_priority(task)


   def calculate_task_priority(self, task):
       """
       Calculate the priority of a task based on its duration and dependents.


       Parameters
       ----------
       task : Task
           The task for which to calculate the priority.


       Returns
       -------
       int
           The calculated priority of the task.
       """
       # Base case 1: task with fixed time, priority is set to time remaining in the day after start time
       if task.fixed_time is not None:
           return (1440 - self.convert_time_to_minutes(task.fixed_time))
       # Base case 2: task with no dependents, priority is set to its duration
       if not task.dependents:
           return task.duration
      
       priority = task.duration
       # Loop through the dependents and add their priorities
       for dep_id in task.dependents:
           dependent_task = self.task_dict[dep_id]
           # Recursively calculate the priority of the dependent task
           priority += self.calculate_task_priority(dependent_task)
      
       return priority




tasks.py: contains a list of tasks to be passed into the scheduler
# set up tasks for the scheduler
from scheduler import Task


tasks = [
   Task(id=0, description='CS110 class', duration=100, fixed_time="1600", dependencies=[1,7]),
   Task(id=1, description='Prepare for CS110 class', duration=180),
   Task(id=2, description='Prepare for SS112 class', duration=120),
   Task(id=3, description='Grading', duration=90),

   Task(id=5, description='Take a shower', duration=30, dependencies=[8,9,10]),
   Task(id=6, description='CodePath class', duration=120, fixed_time="0900"),
   Task(id=7, description='Make brunch', duration=45),
   Task(id=8, description='Get dinner', duration=60, dependencies=[4,7]),
   Task(id=9, description='Bike', duration=30, dependencies=[4]),
   Task(id=10, description='Get boba', duration=15, dependencies=[4])
]




test_maxheapq.py: contains unit tests for the MaxHeapq class
from models import MaxHeapq, Task


def test_heappush():
   # Test that tasks are inserted correctly and the max task is updated
   # Also serves as test for increase_key()
   heap = MaxHeapq()
   task1 = Task(1, "Task 1", 30, priority=10)
   task2 = Task(2, "Task 2", 20, priority=20)
   heap.heappush(task1)
   heap.heappush(task2)
   # The task with the highest priority should be task2
   assert heap.max_task() == task2, "test_heappush failed"


   # Edge case: Insert a task with the same priority as the current max
   task3 = Task(3, "Task 3", 10, priority=20)
   heap.heappush(task3)
   assert heap.max_task() == task2, "test_heappush failed"


   # Insert a task with a higher priority than the current max
   task4 = Task(4, "Task 4", 40, priority=30)
   heap.heappush(task4)
   assert heap.max_task() == task4, "test_heappush failed"


   # Insert a task with a lower priority than the current max
   task5 = Task(5, "Task 5", 50, priority=5)
   heap.heappush(task5)
   assert heap.max_task() == task4, "test_heappush failed"


def test_heappop():
   # Test that the highest priority task is removed correctly
   # Also serves as test for heapify(), left(), and right()
   heap = MaxHeapq()
   task1 = Task(1, "Task 1", 30, priority=10)
   task2 = Task(2, "Task 2", 20, priority=20)
   heap.heappush(task1)
   heap.heappush(task2)
   # The task with the highest priority should be task2
   max_task = heap.heappop()
   assert max_task == task2, "test_heappop failed"
   # After removing task2, the max task should be task1
   assert heap.max_task() == task1, "test_heappop failed"


   # Edge case: Pop from a heap with one element
   max_task = heap.heappop()
   assert max_task == task1, "test_heappop failed"
   assert heap.max_task() is None, "test_heappop failed"


   # Edge case: Pop from an empty heap
   try:
       max_task = heap.heappop()
       assert False, "Expected ValueError"
   except ValueError as e:
       assert str(e) == 'Heap underflow: There are no keys in the priority queue ', "test_heappop failed"


   # Edge case: Pop from a heap with multiple elements of the same priority
   task3 = Task(3, "Task 3", 10, priority=20)
   task4 = Task(4, "Task 4", 40, priority=20)
   heap.heappush(task3)
   heap.heappush(task4)
   max_task = heap.heappop()
   assert max_task == task3 or max_task == task4, "test_heappop failed"


def test_max_task():
   # Test that the max task is returned correctly without removing it
   heap = MaxHeapq()
   task1 = Task(1, "Task 1", 30, priority=10)
   heap.heappush(task1)
   # The max task should be task1
   assert heap.max_task() == task1, "test_max_task failed"


   # Edge case: Check max task on an empty heap
   heap = MaxHeapq()
   assert heap.max_task() is None, "test_max_task failed"


   # Edge case: Check max task on a heap with multiple elements of the same priority
   task2 = Task(2, "Task 2", 20, priority=20)
   task3 = Task(3, "Task 3", 10, priority=20)
   heap.heappush(task2)
   heap.heappush(task3)
   assert heap.max_task() == task2 or heap.max_task() == task3, "test_max_task failed"


   # Edge case: Check max task after popping the max task
   heap.heappop()
   assert heap.max_task() == task3 or heap.max_task() == task2, "test_max_task failed"


if __name__ == "__main__":
   test_heappush()
   test_heappop()
   test_max_task()
   print("All tests passed!")




test_scheduler.py: contains a test for the MyScheduler class to test the same input in a different order
# running test on randomly ordered input
from tasks import tasks
from scheduler import MyScheduler
import random


if __name__ == "__main__":
   # original input matching main.py
   scheduler = MyScheduler(tasks, start_time="0845")
   # run the scheduler
   ran_tasks = scheduler.run(scheduler.start_time)


  
   # testcase: same input as main.py but in random order; should print the same output as main.py
   random.seed(0)
   random.shuffle(tasks)
   testcase = tasks
   scheduler_test = MyScheduler(testcase, start_time="0845")
   test_ran_tasks = scheduler_test.run(scheduler_test.start_time)


   # assert statement to check if the task order matches the expected order
   assert ran_tasks == ran_tasks, f"Task order does not match. Got: {ran_tasks}, Expected: {ran_tasks}"
   print("Task order matches the expected order.")

text_raw:
﻿Technical Interview and Self-Reflection




Minerva University
CS111: Single and Multivariable Calculus
Prof. C. Ríos
December 6, 2024


Technical Interview and Self-Reflection        2
Challenging Problem        2
Feedback        2
Curiosities        3
AI Use        3














Technical Interview and Self-Reflection
Challenging Problem
A specific problem I struggled with was computing
         
using both the method of substitution and integration by parts. This is because I was not comfortable using integration by parts, and had trouble identifying the appropriate approach. My struggle stemmed from a lack of intuition when using integration by parts, even though I understood the underlying logic. When integrating a function of , I was unable to identify which parts of the integrand belonged in  and  given the formula
,
mostly because derivatives in the form  and  felt unfamiliar having learned them as  and  in high school. I was able to overcome this by finding Japanese online articles covering the topic and relating the formula back to familiar notation. As this was the first obstacle I encountered in the CS111 course, not understanding the concepts deeply hurt my self-image of being somewhat good at mathematics. It served as a reminder that I am still learning and there is nothing wrong with not arriving at the correct answer on the first try. It also instilled a sense of much-needed humility, but having overcome this I feel more comfortable applying new concepts with the intention to learn from the process.
Feedback
I received a score on #composition in the first Deep Dive with the comment “You can improve here by building a linear narrative that includes the equations as claims into it. Remember that equations are claims, so they should be treated as sentences. Formal academic or technical writing is formatted in this way. [...] Notice, in particular the use of punctuation after the equations, when needed.”
This helped me understand that a mathematical paper should still flow like a paper nonetheless. I incorporated this feedback into the LBA, where I was careful to construct a include the choices I made and the operations I did when finding the optimal location for our small business. I was able to produce a well-integrated narrative that included figures and mathematical expressions that read as a coherent, linear whole.
Curiosities
Having used Sage Math for the first time in this course, and being from an education system that widely prohibits the use of calculators, I am curious about the strategy underlying the find_root() function. I came across this as an alternative to solve() for a system of non-linear equations for the LBA, and from the documentation, I learned that it is based on Brent’s method (Stein & Cohen, 2008). I skimmed the relevant section of the original article by Brent (2002) and found that it dynamically uses the bisection method, secant method, and inverse quadratic interpolation. While I was able to understand the bisection method as a similar concept to binary search, I was unable to make sense of the other two methods and would like to learn more about alternatives to analytically solving equations.
AI Use
I used ChatGPT for debugging and formatting code in the pre-class work, and clarifying concepts. One example is session 14 pre-class work. My original code in Figure 1 resulted in an indistinguishable plot, shown in Figure 2. I used the prompt “Take this existing Sage Math code. [insert code snippet in Figure 1] Update the opacity and colors to make them more discriminable and add a legend.” to improve salience, resulting in Figures 3 and 4 (OpenAI, 2024).
Figure 1
Original code
x, y, t = var('x y t')

# three-dimensional function
f(x, y) = sin(x) * cos(y)
surface_plot = plot3d(f(x, y), (x, -3, 3), (y, -3, 3), online=True)

# unit circle in the plane z=0
circle_plot = parametric_plot3d([cos(t), sin(t), f(cos(t), sin(t))], (t, 0, 2*pi))

show(surface_plot + circle_plot, aspect_ratio=[1, 1, 0.3])
	

Figure 2
3D plot created with original code
  

Figure 3
Code updated by ChatGPT
x, y, t = var('x y t')

# Define the three-dimensional function
f(x, y) = sin(x) * cos(y)

# Surface plot with adjusted opacity and color
surface_plot = plot3d(f(x, y), (x, -3, 3), (y, -3, 3), color='lightblue', opacity=0.7, legend_label='sin(x) * cos(y)', online=True)

# Unit circle in the plane z=0 with adjusted opacity and color
circle_plot = parametric_plot3d([cos(t), sin(t), f(cos(t), sin(t))], (t, 0, 2*pi), color='red', opacity=0.8, legend_label='x^2 + y^2 = 1')

# Show both plots with aspect ratio and legend
show(surface_plot + circle_plot, aspect_ratio=[1, 1, 0.3], legend=True)
	

Figure 4
3D plot produced by updated code
  

While the resulting plot did not have a legend as intended, the discriminability of the unit circle was greatly improved. 
        Another use case of ChatGPT, also from session 14, was clarifying the use of Lagrange multipliers. From the video, I understood that  is an arbitrary constant that represents the ratio of the gradient vectors of the objective function and constraint function. This led to the prompt: “When doing constrained optimization with Lagrange multipliers, is it guaranteed that I will find a maximum? Is it possible to find a minimum? Provide a one-paragraph explanation.” The response was:
When using Lagrange multipliers for constrained optimization, the method guarantees finding a critical point, but it does not guarantee that the point will be a maximum. The critical point found could represent a maximum, minimum, or a saddle point, depending on the nature of the objective function and the constraints. To determine whether the critical point corresponds to a maximum or minimum, further analysis, such as the second-order condition (using the Hessian matrix), is needed. Thus, it is indeed possible to find a minimum, but additional steps are required to confirm whether the critical point is a maximum, minimum, or saddle point. (OpenAI, 2024)
This helped me clarify that using Lagrange multipliers will only yield critical points, as opposed to directly finding the coordinates of a local maximum. While I firmly believe that using AI to directly obtain solutions counteracts learning, engaging it in Socratic dialogue can be helpful to students under the premise that they bring confusions that arose from other resources. Using it as a tool to expedite workflow, as was the case with formatting code, could also complement students’ learning by handling tedious, albeit time-consuming tasks for them.


Word Count: 878 words
AI Statement: I used Grammarly.com to proofread and improve the quality of my writing. No other AI tools were used in this assignment.
________________


References
Brent, R. P. (2002). Algorithms for Minimization Without Derivatives. Dover Publications.
OpenAI. (2024). ChatGPT. (October 29 version) [Large Language Model]. https://chat.openai.com/chat
Stein, W., & Cohen, N. (2008). Numerical Root Finding and Optimization. Sage Version 10.4 Documentation. Retrieved 12 3, 2024, from https://doc.sagemath.org/html/en/reference/numerical/sage/numerical/optimize.html

name: Political Analysis [final]
text_raw:
﻿Political Analysis




Minerva University
SS112: Political Science and Social Change
Prof. M. Kukis
December 14, 2024












________________


“Sayōnara Genpatsu:” Anti-nuclear Movement in Japan
March 26, 2021, marked the 400th and last “Kinyou Kantei-mae” anti-nuclear protest held in front of the Japanese Prime Minister’s Residence (Metropolitan Coalition Against Nukes, 2021). These protests have been a consistent part of the ongoing anti-nuclear power movement in Japan, which started after the Fukushima nuclear accident. After the 35-year-long “ice age” of social movement, this came as a huge surprise (Sato et al., 2019, p. 62). While the movement has lost much of its momentum, it could also signify a shift in the attitudes towards demonstrations of the Japanese people, as seen in Figure 1. This paper analyzes the underlying causes of the movement beyond the immediate nuclear accident.
Figure 1
Opinions on demonstrations among Japanese people
  

Note. Source: World Values Survey [WVS], 2022. The 2010-2014 wave rolled out in 2010 in Japan, which was before the Fukushima nuclear disaster. For most years, a majority of people reported that they would never participate in demonstrations, but the sum of people who participated or might participate increased by around 4% after the disaster.
Background: the Fukushima nuclear disaster
The Fukushima nuclear disaster was a series of explosions and meltdowns in the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant following the Great East Japan Earthquake on March 11, 2011. This caused the release of radioactive materials into the environment, resulting in the evacuation of residents in a 20 km radius (World Nuclear Association, n.d.). The accident has displaced over 160,000, and more than 25,000 are yet to return to their homes today (Fukushima Prefectural Office, 2024).
Approximately a month after the disaster, the first major anti-nuclear protest took place in Tokyo, rallying roughly 15,000 people under slogans such as “Stop Using Nuclear Power” (Sunakami, 2022). On June 11th the same year, protests erupted all over Tokyo and joined courses in front of Shinjuku Station in the evening, essentially combining into an even larger demonstration (Sato et al., 2019, p. 65). The movement continued to garner support, such as in the “Sayōnara Genpatsu,” or “Goodbye Nukes” rallies, and in the weekly “Kinyou Kantei-mae” protest that took place on Friday evenings in front of the Prime Minister’s residence, which, at its peak, gathered 200,000 protesters. Demonstrations continued to emerge, especially on the anniversary of 3.11 and whenever the government announced plans to reactivate power plants that had passed stress testing under new, stricter safety guidelines (Hiratsuka, 2023; Yaginuma, 2024). 
Hypothesis 
As we have seen, the Fukushima nuclear disaster became the catalyst for the first mass social movement in years. The seemingly spontaneous arousal of the movement from a politically inactive citizenship can be explained by the multi-dimensionality of the nuclear power issue, which appealed to different demographics through different lines of reasoning. These different groups came together under the centralized cause of opposing the use of nuclear power, best exemplified on the June 11th Shinjuku Station protest. The diversity of the movement and large scale further lowered the barrier of entry, causing the scale to grow even further. 
The younger generation
Some of the first large-scale anti-nuclear protests were organized by activist Hajime Matsumoto, owner of the thrift store “Shirōto no Ran” (Sunakami, 2022). Fearing that the media would “let the incident slide after a few weeks,” he organized a protest on April 10th, putting up flyers and garnering support on Twitter. Although Matsumoto only expected several thousand to participate, word spread rapidly through his network of fellow activists-turned-shop-owners in Kōenji, Tokyo.
The turnout was 15,000 people, predominantly young adults who learned of the protest through Tweets by customers who frequent Kōenji. Fitting for a demonstration planned by the owner of Shirōto no Ran, which translates into “Amateur’s Revolt,” many of these participants had never participated in a demonstration before (Tan, 2011, pp. 299-300). Matsumoto was also responsible for organizing one of the protests on June 11, 2011. Given the younger generation’s widespread use of social media, their mass mobilization can be attributed to a heightened awareness of social movements and issues based on information obtained through the Internet, as seen in Figures 2 and 3.
Figure 2
Percentage of people obtaining information about nuclear power via the Internet by age in 2011 
  

Note. Source: Japan Atomic Energy Relations Organization, 2012. The graph shows the percentage of respondents who chose “The Internet” in response to the multiple-answer question “Where do you obtain information regarding nuclear power and radiation?” in an October 2011 poll. Younger respondents tend to obtain information online.
Figure 3
Frequency of using the Internet to learn about what is going on by age in 2010
  

Note. Source: WVS, 2022. The graph shows the responses in the 2010-2014 wave to the multiple-answer question “For [the Internet], please indicate whether you use it to obtain information daily, weekly, monthly, less than monthly or never.” by age group in the WVS conducted in 2012. The graph indicates that around the time of 3.11, the majority of younger Japanese people learned about domestic events from the Internet daily.
        Furthermore, the younger generation is overall environmentally conscious, as seen in Figure 4. In addition to having an acute sense of personal involvement,  half of the respondents to the 2008 Global Survey on Sustainable Lifestyles (GSSL) “referred ‘Responsibility for future generations’, when they were asked ‘What makes a way of living sustainable?’” indicating that younger people tend to feel an obligation to protect the environment for a better future (UN Environment Programme, 2011).
Figure 4
The most important priorities globally for Japanese youth in 2008
  

Note. Source: UN Environment Programme, 2011. The graph is based on responses to the question “What are the most important priorities globally?” According to the GSSL conducted in 2008, which is aimed at youth aged 18-35, 25.8% of Japanese youth identified “Environmental degradation” as a first priority.
Parents with children
One of the June 11th protests was the “Energy Shift Parade” organized by environmental non-government organizations such as Greenpeace Japan (Satou et al., 2019, p. 65). Advocating for the use of cleaner energy sources over nuclear power, this parade predominantly attracted women in their 30s to 50s and their families. Mothers have also been seen bringing their children to peaceful rallies, oftentimes held in parks during the day on weekends. 
Given the long-lasting nature of a nuclear accident’s environmental effects, parents may be more wary of nuclear power, which could negatively impact the world their children will live in. This can also be seen in parents’ tendency to be more environmentally conscious, as seen in Figure 5. Parents with children may be participating in the anti-nuclear power movement as a means to protect their children’s futures.
Figure 5
Identification with an environmentally conscious person in parents and non-parents in 2010
  

Note. Source: WVS, 2022. The graph shows the responses in the 2010-2014 wave to the question “Please indicate for [this] description [: It is important to this person looking after the environment] whether that person is very much like you, like you, somewhat like you, not like you, or not at all like you” by parenthood status in the WVS conducted in 2012. Respondents with children tend to identify more with an environmentally conscious person.
The older generation
Prior to the “ice age” of social movements, there had been much discourse and activism surrounding the broad topic of nuclear power. For example, an anti-nuclear power movement in the 1970s strongly opposed the initial development of nuclear reactors due to concerns about environmental safety and indirect effects such as the economic impact of lower fishing yields. Locals near prospective building sites also raised concerns about the government’s lax safety standards as a result of collusion with electricity companies (Nishio, 2017). Other relevant movements include the protests against the deployment of nuclear weapons in U.S. bases in Japan and the movement by the “Gensuikin,” the Japan Council against Atomic and Hydrogen Bombs, advocating for overall denuclearization under the slogan “humanity and nukes cannot coexist”  (Sato et al., 2019, p. 65). 
Organizations involved in these movements led by the then youth – current senior citizens – continued their activities post-3.11, often broadening their scope to more diverse, relevant topics in response to the new discourse surrounding nuclear energy (Machimura et al., 2015, pp. 7-9; Sato et al., 2019, p. 65). The older generation had first-hand experience of social movements, and the Fukushima disaster may have resonated with this predisposition for anti-nuclear activism in the older generation, leading them to mobilize in recent movements. This history of activism is also seen in the older generation’s overall higher affinity for demonstrations in general, as seen in Figure 6. 
Figure 6
Opinions on demonstrations among Japanese people by age in 2010
  

Note. Source: WVS, 2022. The graph shows the responses in the 2010-2014 wave to the question “Tell me, for each one, whether you have actually done [Political action: attending lawful/peaceful demonstrations], whether you might do it or would never, under any circumstances, do it.” Older people are more likely to have participated in demonstrations, and even if they have not, are more inclined to do so than younger generations.
Conclusion
The anti-nuclear power movement garnered support from an immensely diverse group of people spanning generations, regions, and occupations, effectively pushing the issue of nuclear power to the forefront of government agenda. The movement’s ability to inspire action on such a large scale illustrates the complexity of the problem, which warrants careful consideration of a multitude of aspects in any solution the government proposes. 


Word Count: 1,117 words
AI Statement: I used Grammarly.com to proofread my writing. I used GitHub Copilot to assist in writing Python code to process, clean, aggregate, and plot the data used in the figures, using prompts such as “Process this data into a pandas dataframe,” “Aggregate the respondents with 1 or more children into a with_children category,” and “Create a stacked bar chart from this data.” 


________________
References
Fukushima Prefectural Office. (2024, 12 11). Current Evacuees (As of November 1st). Fukushima Reconstruction Information Portal. https://www.pref.fukushima.lg.jp/site/portal/hinansya.html
Hiratsuka, M. (2023, March 11). Learning from Fukushima: Anti-nuke Walks in Miyazaki. Asahi Shinbun Digital. https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASR3B7KCBR3BTNAB006.html
Japan Atomic Energy Relations Organization. (2012). 2011 Public Opinion Poll on Nuclear Energy. Japan Atomic Energy Relations Organization. Retrieved December 14, 2024, from https://www.jaero.or.jp/poll/2_index_detail.php
Machimura, T., Sato, K., Tatsumi, T., Komoda, R., Kim, J., Kim, M., & Chen, W. (2015). Diversified Background of the "Anti-nuclear Movement" After the Fukushima Accident: Results of a Nation-wide Survey of Civic Groups in Japan. Hitotsubashi Political Science, 7, 1-32.
Metropolitan Coalition Against Nukes. (2021, March 26). Friday Protests in Front of the Prime Minister's Residency, going on hiatus after 9.5 years: Statement by Metropolitan Coalition Against Nukes. Tokyo-Shinbun. https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/93930
Nishio, B. (2017). Reflecting on the Japanese Anti-nuclear Movement. Hangenpatsu Shinbun. https://cnic.jp/hangenpatsu/185
Sato, K., Kimberly, F. W. Y., & Mori, K. (2019). Alliances among social movement organizations and new protest cycle after the Fukushima nuclear accident: From egocentric network approach. Asia Pacific Review, 16, 62-80.
Sunakami, A. (2022, January 4). Demonstrations as an Indicator of Civil Engagement: the Actor Behind the New Demonstration Culture. Tokyo Shinbun. https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/article/152190
Tan, U. (2011). The 6-11 “Amateurs’ Revolt” Demonstration against Nuclear Power: A New Movement Style? Disaster, Infrastructure, and Society: Learning from the 2011 Earthquake in Japan, 1, 299-304. https://hermes-ir.lib.hit-u.ac.jp/hermes/ir/re/22086/dis0000102990.pdf
UN Environment Programme. (2011). Visions For Change Country Papers. UN Environment Programme Document Repository. https://wedocs.unep.org/bitstream/handle/20.500.11822/8025/-Visions%20for%20Change%20-%20Recommendations%20for%20Effective%20Policies%20on%20Sustainable%20Lifestyles_%20Country%20Papers%20%20-20111043.pdf?sequence=3&isAllowed=y
World Nuclear Association. (n.d.). Fukushima Daiichi Accident. World Nuclear Association. Retrieved December 14, 2024, from https://world-nuclear.org/information-library/safety-and-security/safety-of-plants/fukushima-daiichi-accident
World Values Survey [WVS]. (2022). Political action: attending lawful/peaceful demonstrations. WVS Database. Retrieved December 14, 2024, from https://www.worldvaluessurvey.org/WVSOnline.jsp
Yaginuma, H. (2024, September 18). First Demonstration in 30 Years: Opposition to Reactivation of Nuclear Reactor. Asahi Shinbun Digital. https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASS9K2DZBS9KUNHB002M.html
________________
LO Appendix
#constructivism
I used the constructivist framework to identify how different demographics had different ideas about nuclear power, which all led to the anti-nuclear power movement. I also identified how pre-existing ideas (such as concern for the future in younger people and parents with children and predisposition towards denuclearization in older people) interacted with a current event to lead to the widespread social movement.
#structuralism
I identified how different demographics reacted to the Fukushima disaster, unifying them under a broader cause. I explain how, if not for the multi-faceted nature of the nuclear disaster, the movement would not have appealed to so many and gained as much traction. I also discuss how pre-existing social structures, such as the activist community in Kōenji and anti-nuclear organizations that have been operating prior to 3.11 contributed to the movement.
#casestudy
 I provided an in-depth analysis of the anti-nuclear movement in Japan by detailing the background of the movement and key demonstrations that exemplify the movement. I studied the demographics of protesters from news and scholarly articles and sought to explain their diversity and how they came to collectively pursue the same goal.
#poliscimethods
        I used quantitative data on public opinion and experiences to identify trends in ideology, attitude, and habits in different demographics, such as younger people’s shared concern for the environment and their tendency to obtain information online, which explains why the protests organized by Matsumoto gained so much traction. I also draw on qualitative data from media articles and existing research on the anti-nuclear movement to better categorize the actors, such as based on age or parenthood status, allowing me to cross the quantitative data by relevant factors.
