﻿Historical Public Housing in Taiwan




Minerva University
SS112: Political Science and Social Change
Prof. M. Kukis
November 11, 2024












________________


Historical Public Housing in Taiwan
The National Taiwan Museum regularly hosts guided tours of its exhibits. The Railway Department Park Branch is currently hosting the exhibit “Living Together: Public Housing Design and the Ideal Modern Life,” exploring the development of Taiwanese public housing through photos and dioramas, offering insight into the influence of governance on housing (National Taiwan Museum, n.d.). This report further explores how institutions and societal norms shaped public housing. 
Housing under the Kuomintang
The political ideology of Kuomintang (KMT), founded by Sun Yat-sen, is largely based on his philosophy of the “Three Principles of the People,” comprising nationalism, democracy, and livelihood. The pillar of livelihood addressed social welfare and stated that land should be fully utilized, with the benefits being shared by the whole society (La Grange et al., 2006, p. 58). This principle guided the KMT’s early Land-to-the-Tiller Program in the 1950s, which sought to redistribute farmland to address economic inequality (Koo, n.d., pp. 150-151). This also led Taiwanese society to widely adopt the tradition of small property ownership, which contributed to the emphasis on the purchase of public housing as opposed to rent. 
The Kuomintang (KMT) retreated from mainland China and placed Taiwan under authoritarian control from 1949 to 1987, initially funneling most of the island’s resources into national defense (Chang & Yuan, 2013, p. 88). The KMT was only concerned with public housing as far as it benefitted government personnel and their dependents, and the broader public’s housing needs were mostly self-fulfilled. 
However, the population grew as military and government personnel and refugees flooded in from mainland China, causing a significant lack of housing (Chang & Yuan, 2013, pp. 90-91). The government began to construct “military dependents’ villages,” settlements for civil servants and their families. The KMT was also forced to provide aid in response to the destruction caused by natural disasters, such as typhoons and earthquakes, going as far as mobilizing the military to assist with rescues and reconstruction. 
While the early KMT government did implement some public housing policies, these were passively rolled out only in response to the emerging crises of increasing population and natural disasters. These also had the purpose of consolidating the regime and pacifying civil unrest; in addition to housing policies, the government began allowing limited democracy and provincial autonomy in 1954 to mitigate civil pressures (Chang & Yuan, 2013, pp. 98-99). This led to the development of clientelism, where the government “bought” the loyalty of local factions with economic resources and turned a blind eye to corruption in local politicians.
The Public Housing Act of 1975
The Public Housing Act was passed to “stabilize the people’s livelihood, and promote social welfare” in 1975 (Laws & Regulation Database of the Republic of China (Taiwan), n.d.). Under this policy, the government began a construction project to build 100,000 units from 1976 to 1981 to support economic development and appease activists calling for better social welfare (Chang & Yuan, 2013, p. 91). While this public construction project seemed to be in the interests of the people who could not afford housing, it also served as an opportunity to provide favors to interest groups, such as real estate developers, that supported local politicians (Chang & Yuan, 2013, pp. 98-99). Housing policy thus became a campaign tool for political parties.
The project did not yield the promised results, falling short of their construction goals by over 30% due to slow progress (Chang & Yuan, 2013, p.91). Furthermore, the government failed to account for rising housing prices during the economic boom of the late 1970s (Chang & Yuan, 2013, pp. 91-92). Loans for public housing only accounted for up to 30% of the price of public housing units, making it increasingly difficult for lower- and middle-class families to purchase homes. Thus, public housing tended to be purchased by wealthier families, and the policy failed to support its target demographic. The grievances of these underserved people culminated in the “homeless campaign” of 1989, where they formed the United Association of the Homeless and slept on the streets of Taipei’s most expensive district in protest against skyrocketing prices.
Housing in Post-Martial Rule to Modern-day Taiwan
After the end of martial law in 1987, Taiwan was faced with a stagnating economy and political turmoil arising from the Democratic Progressive Party (DPP) challenging the KMT (Chang & Yuan, 2013, p. 93). The government struggled to continue the construction of public housing and shifted towards policies focusing on mortgage interest-rate subsidies and tax reform. However, these policies still did not serve their intended purpose, with loose review standards allowing people who can afford housing to obtain subsidies and diluting the use of government funds. This widened the poverty gap, allowed real estate agencies to uphold their high prices, and let banks profit from loan interests. The new housing policy, seemingly a means to support the people, only did so under the constraints of serving these interest groups, which drew on their networks accumulated from decades of clientelism to further their influence (Chang & Yuan, 2013, p. 99). 
Accessible housing for all continues to be a distant goal as the unchecked housing market continues to become increasingly unaffordable, especially in resource- and population-dense areas such as Taipei (Chang & Yuan, 2013, pp. 86-88). The demand for public housing far exceeds the supply. 
In response to this, the National Housing and Urban Regeneration Center (NHURC) was established in 2018. However, the NHURC’s activities have been met with local opposition in some cities such as Taipei, where the multilateral mode of governance allows non-governmental actors to have more sway (Chih & Robbins, 2021). Public hearings on housing projects are treated as opportunities for local communities and their elected leaders, lizhangs, to express their concerns and leverage the government’s political need for approval, upholding the trend of clientelism. 
Although the Taiwanese government seems to be attempting to tackle the deep-rooted housing problem, the conflicting interests and distributed power of non-government actors continue to pose a challenge to any substantial improvement (Chih & Robbins, 2021; Chang & Yuan, 2013, p. 99-100). 




Word Count: 999 words
________________


References
Chang, C.-O., & Yuan, S.-M. (2013). Chapter 6. In Public Housing Policy in Taiwan (pp. 85-101). Springer. http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/978-3-642-41622-4_6
Chih, C., & Robbins, S. (2021, June 23). A Tale of Two Cities: Taiwan's Social Housing Policy Practice in Taipei and Taoyuan City, 2014-2018. Taiwan Insight. Retrieved November 18, 2024, from https://taiwaninsight.org/2021/06/23/a-tale-of-two-cities-taiwans-social-housing-policy-practice-in-taipei-and-taoyuan-city-2014-2018/
Koo, A. Y.C. (n.d.). Consequences of Land Reform in Taiwan. Asian Survey, 6(3), 150-157. https://doi.org/10.2307/2642219
La Grange, A., Chang, C.-O., & Yip, N. (2006). Commodification and urban development: a case study of Taiwan. Housing Studies, 21(1), 53-76. http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/02673030500391114
Laws & Regulation Database of the Republic of China (Taiwan). (n.d.). Public Housing Act - Article Content - Laws & Regulations Database of The Republic of China (Taiwan). Retrieved November 18, 2024, from https://law.moj.gov.tw/ENG/LawClass/LawAll.aspx?pcode=D0070081
National Taiwan Museum. (n.d.). Living Together: Public Housing Design and the Ideal Modern Life. 國立臺灣博物館. https://event.culture.tw/mocweb/reg/NTM/Detail.init.ctr?actId=30127&request_locale=en&useLanguage=en
________________
LO Appendix
* #ss112-constructivism: I identified ideological factors that deterred the Taiwanese housing market from developing in the direction of renting as opposed to home ownership. The emphasis on ownership stems from Sun Yat-sen’s philosophy of the "Three Principles of the People," which emphasized land as a shared benefit, and the subsequent “Land-to-the-Tiller” program imparted norms of land ownership. I also explored how clientelism developed into standard government practice, which is also reflected in public hearings.
* #ss112-institutionalism: I identified the institutional practices (clientelism) of formal institutions (the KMT government) and informal institutions (real estate developers, banks, and local factions) as drivers of political outcomes. Public housing policies served the interests of the government (to appease civil interests and maintain legitimacy) and their clients (real estate developers maintaining high housing prices and banks issuing loans).

name: Ethnographic Assignment [final]
text_raw:
﻿Ethnographic Assignment




Minerva University
GL93: Global Learning
October 31, 2024












________________


Ethnographic Assignment
Introduction
For this assignment, I interviewed Mei-Chun, a Taiwanese environmental science student at NTU. I met her at the NTU library and we talked about her background over boba (which she got in her own water bottle). She hails from a very close family of six: her grandparents, who live very close by, her parents, her younger brother in middle school, and herself. Her family has a mix of Taiwanese and Hakka heritage, and she identifies with both. Her grandparents primarily speak Hakka.
Heritage, Family, and Language
Mei-Chun’s grandparents primarily speak Hakka and passed on many of their traditions and values to her, such as resilience, frugality, and community. Her parents emphasized the importance of education and respect for nature, which is a value shared among many Taiwanese people. These, combined with Taiwan’s state-wide focus on sustainability, led her to study environmental science. 
Mei-Chun speaks Mandarin, Hakka, and some Taiwanese Hokkien, though she is most fluent in Mandarin. Speaking Hakka with her grandparents has been a big part of maintaining her heritage, and keeps her connected to her cultural roots. In this sense, language goes beyond just a means of communication, but also serves as a precious link to family and tradition.
One important cultural artifact for Mei-Chun is the Hakka people’s iconic floral print fabric, which is also endearingly called “grandma’s floral cloth” in Taiwan. Fittingly, her grandmother owns garments and house decor made from this cloth that symbolizes their heritage and identity as Hakka people. 






Local Traditions and Celebrations
Mei-Chun’s family honors the Tomb Sweeping Festival (Qingming), which, as the name suggests, is a time for the living to visit the graves of their ancestors to pay respects, give offerings, and clean the space. This tradition that helps them connect to their roots serves as an opportunity for family remembrance, which is an important part of Hakka culture.
Another cultural event Mei-Chun enjoys is the Dragon Boat Festival, where they make pyramid-shaped rice dumplings wrapped in bamboo leaves (zongzi), and watch dragon boat races. The festival originates in the legend of how people tried to save Qu Yuan, an ancient Chinese poet and minister who drowned himself in protest against corruption.
The most important celebration for Taiwanese families is, no doubt, the Lunar New Year. Festivities to celebrate the fresh start and good fortune for the coming year span several days between January and early February. Mei-Chun’s family welcomes the new year by cleaning their home to get rid of the misfortunes from the past year, and join her grandparents for a reunion dinner on New Year’s Eve. The dishes served at dinner all have symbolic meanings, such as fish for surplus and abundance, long noodles that represent longevity, and dumplings that symbolize wealth because their shape resembles ancient Chinese ingots. They also exchange red envelopes filled with money, which are a symbol of good luck. They also watch the fireworks display at Taipei 101 from their balcony in the Da’an district, which draws massive crowds every year.
Conclusion
Mei-Chun’s story allowed me to learn so much about the rich cultural context around traditions and rituals that I have been seeing in Taiwan thus far, and I am happy to have gotten a glimpse of this depth as a non-local. I appreciated this opportunity to learn more about the cultural diversity within Taiwan and how the different subcommunities come together as one to celebrate special occasions.


Word Count: 570 words

﻿Elevation Reflection & Engagement 
GL93
Suisei Nakagawa


After reviewing the myMinerva city pages for your upcoming rotation city, and engaging with the Cultural Specific pre-readings for Elevation shared by the City Teams information answer the following two questions:


1. What was one thing you learned or were surprised by in reviewing the cultural specific pre-readings? (100 words)

My home country is Japan, where many traditional religious practices have become obsolete and most people are not religious. Given this background, I expected Taiwan, a nearby East Asian country with many cultural similarities, to also be this way. Thus, I was surprised that religious and spiritual practices are so deep-rooted in Taiwanese customs and daily life, especially compared to Japan. I was also surprised that visiting temples was a common practice for local residents, and that religious sites were more than mere tourist attractions. 


   2. How do you plan to engage in the city in a way that helps you make sense of the city and pushes you past surface level understanding? (150 words)

As a Japanese speaker, I can somewhat guess at written Mandarin, but want to learn the spoken language and gain a better understanding of the city by talking to locals and living like one. I also want to visit (and potentially participate in guided tours of) art and history museums. In addition to seeing the exhibits, I would like to ask curators about how Taiwanese culture developed within the international political landscape, how colonial rule and the accompanying foreign culture influenced and continues to influence traditional culture, and how that is reflected in the displays. I believe that this will allow me to better understand the historical background of contemporary Taiwanese culture and learn how political history shapes modern life.

text_raw:
﻿Summary Paper




Minerva University
IL199: Research Internship (Summer 2024 submission)
August 18, 2024












________________


Summary Paper
Description of Internship
I worked as a Camp Counselor at the Noe Valley, San Francisco location of Galileo Learning’s summer camp. My role entailed preparing and co-teaching STEAM curriculums for my assigned group of 16-24 rising 6-8th graders and monitoring unstructured play during recess and extended care. The curriculums included designing and building remote-controlled soccer player robots, escape rooms, and go-carts. The position recommended that applicants have a background in childcare and/or teaching, are comfortable performing skits in front of children, and have experience handling tools such as handsaws and power drills. Although the actual work indeed required these skills, I found that my prior experience tutoring smaller groups of 3-4 students was not directly transferable to managing larger classrooms. 
HC Appendix
#scienceoflearning: I applied this HC to better facilitate the campers’ development of skills such as critical thinking and design. Specifically, I encouraged them to use self-explanation by asking them why they think specific parts of their project didn’t work (e.g., “Why do you think your go-cart’s steering veers to the left?”) or the reasoning behind their design choices (e.g., “How did you decide to make the ‘legs’ of your soccer player robot in that shape?”) as a means to encourage deeper understanding of the project’s mechanics. I aimed to draw on the principle of the generation effect and deliberate practice through these questions that prompt the campers to reflect on their design and building process and make adjustments as needed. My work at Galileo made me consider applications of this HC as an instructor as opposed to a learner.
#breakitdown: When managing campers with behavioral challenges, I approached the problem by breaking it down first into internal/external factors contributing to the undesirable behavior. I further broke the internal factors into the camper’s level of interest in the task at hand and individual traits such as neurodivergence and temperaments, and external factors into their immediate environment and the difficulty of the task. This allowed me to identify how to address each aspect of the camper’s struggles and how these factors influence each other. For example, one camper struggled to stay composed while building a go-cart in the presence of potentially hazardous tools. Instead of dismissing this as “misbehaving,” I broke this issue down into his tendency to feel frustrated when things don’t go as planned, his disinterest in working on the steering (which was malfunctioning and adding to his frustration), his peers giving him unsolicited advice, and his drill being too heavy for him to operate. Addressing these problems individually by making a rule for him to go for a short walk to cool down when he feels overwhelmed, providing extra support with fixing his project, moving him to a different workstation, and exchanging the drill for a lighter manual screwdriver allowed him to work through the task calmly. 
#fallacies: A majority of comments and beliefs that sparked arguments between campers or led to problematic behavior contained informal fallacies of weak inductions (e.g., the appeal to ignorance fallacy “No way you actually have a black belt, you’re lying” or the false cause fallacy “My robot stopped working. It’s because [other camper] touched it”). Such problems could often be resolved by pointing out the holes in their reasoning and offering alternative explanations (e.g., “Maybe your friend tried really hard for a longer time than you expected to get that black belt” or “Maybe your robot’s wiring has a problem”).  
#algorithms: I decided with the camp directors to take an algorithmic approach to resolving behavioral issues as opposed to handling them case-by-case. This allowed for fairer treatment of campers facing such challenges and prevented other campers from feeling that some of their peers were being favored given that the solution to the problems seemed favorable (e.g., “Why does [other camper] get to draw instead of working on her project”). One of our pseudo algorithms was to address lack of participation:
1. Assess the situation
A. If the camper is showing disinterest but not engaging in disruptive/unsafe behavior, proceed to step 2
B. If the camper is engaging in disruptive/unsafe behavior, tell them to stop with direct language
        I. If they stop problematic behavior, proceed to step 2
        II. If they do not stop, tell them that the behavior warrants a restorative conversation with a camp director and potentially a call home
                1. If they stop problematic behavior, proceed to step 2
                2. If they do not stop, escalate to camp directors via Slack
2. Identify the cause of lack of participation
A. If the cause is illness/feeling unwell, allow the camper to rest at the side of the classroom/workstation
        I. If their condition improves, ask the camper to return to activity
        II. Otherwise, have the camper rest in the office and call home
                1. If guardians can pick the camper up, gather their belongings
                2. Otherwise, ask guardians for instructions (e.g., call an available relative to pick up the camper) and continue to monitor their condition in the office.
B. If the cause is lack of interest, show empathy and engage the camper in discussion on the importance and/or benefit of the current step (e.g., “I know that sawing can get a little boring, but imagine how much cooler your project will look with this piece”)
        I. If the camper still seems disinterested, suggest working on other part(s) of their project and coming back to the current task later
                1. If the camper still seems disinterested, offer them other non-disruptive activities (e.g., draw or make friendship bracelets)
C. If the cause is the difficulty of the task, show empathy and offer to help
D. If the cause is finishing early, ask them if they want to help their peers
        I. If not, instruct them on the next step
                1. If they do not want to proceed, offer them other non-disruptive activities
#audience: I adjusted my language and line of reasoning to my group, aged 10-13. For example, when asking a camper to avoid expressions such as “Jesus Christ,” I prompted them to put themselves in the shoes of their friends who might feel uncomfortable if they heard the name of a figure they have deep respect for used as an exclamation. I used this approach as this age group often struggles with considering the perspectives of others. I also framed using the name of God in vain as a problem because it makes their peers feel bad rather than a religious issue as the camper was not familiar with Christian conventions and referred to other campers as “friends” to encourage empathy. 
#shapingbehavior: I used tools such as incentives, disincentives, and rewards to encourage desirable behavior and discourage disruptive behavior in campers. Specifically, I used verbal reminders (e.g., “Help your friends so we can move on to the next step,” “The more talking you do the more time it’s going to take until you can work on your robots,” “Put the saw down while I’m talking or we’re going to have to chat with the camp director”) as incentives and disincentives. We also had a point system called “pie points” that we used as a reward for exceptionally good behavior such as staying back to help clean after they are released for recess. The system was intentionally designed to be like lottery tickets: their name was put into a hat for a chance to pie a staff member the same number of times as they had points. We did this to prevent campers from adopting a cost-benefit mindset and prevent the incentive from leading campers to behave well only when presented with it. 










Word Count: 1278 words

name: Self Reflection: Purpose and Leadership [final]
text_raw:
﻿Self Reflection: Purpose and Leadership




Minerva University
CX51: Complex Systems
Prof. P. Zoogman
April 11, 2024












________________


Self Reflection: Purpose and Leadership
        This paper serves as an in-depth self-reflection that assesses whether my actions over the past academic year were aligned with my purpose: to continually learn and improve my intrapersonal, academic, and professional skills and to apply these skills for the good of others with an emphasis on assisting them in their own learning.[1] 
Evaluation of #responsibility
        At the beginning of the Fall 2023 semester, I set a goal to invest time and effort into my physical health. I broke this down into subgoals, namely learning how to cook in order to budget enough to purchase membership at a gym and to eat healthier, and to exercise routinely. To hold myself accountable, I registered for the half-marathon section of the San Francisco marathon in July 2024, which cost $240 before tax (Figure 1). 
Figure 1
Registration Confirmation for the San Francisco Marathon
  

The prospect of finishing the iconic race with a course that crosses Golden Gate Bridge in a time under 2:30:00 serves as a motivator for me to train regularly, and the registration fee motivates me further due to the sunk cost fallacy. This has allowed me to run consistently over the past year, leading to me beating my personal record for 5km, which I had not been able to match since I quit track after high school (Figure 2).
Figure 2
Personal Record for 5km Run
  

        I also set a goal to gain 10 pounds in muscle mass through resistance training and a calorie-surplus diet by the end of January. I was able to do this by cultivating an understanding of what parts of my progress are under my internal locus of control, which translated into consciously prioritizing the time I spend cooking and training over other opportunities that I felt did not have as much value when I had the choice. This allowed me to meet my initial goal of gaining mass, as displayed in Figure 3, and I have set a new goal to lower my body fat percentage to 18%.
Figure 3
Data on Body Mass Starting in the Fall Semester
  

        While I was able to follow through on my commitment to overall fitness, I was not able to achieve my goal of taking two or fewer extensions on each course in the Spring semester. I had been on track with one or fewer extensions per course until Quinquatria break but was not able to start the final assignments early enough due to procrastination stemming from the planning fallacy. This led me to spend too much of the break where I was not traveling on trivial tasks such as taking extra time to cook, which may have been important in terms of rest and relaxation but did not serve my goals.
In terms of professional development, my goal was to expand my professional network and find employment for the summer. I was able to achieve this by blocking out time in February to work on my resume and internship applications and taking advantage of resources offered by CTD. I was also conscious of building resilience and framing rejections from prospective employers as opportunities to develop an understanding of how to improve my skill set and presentation.[2] 
Evaluation of #leadprinciples
        Over the past academic year, I have been a part of multiple group projects such as CCP, group assignments, and startups. In these team settings, I typically organized project timelines and set internal deadlines regardless of whether or not I had been formally appointed to such roles. Rather, I had influence over the team as my actions, which entailed assigning tasks, checking in on progress, and scheduling meetings, furthered the teams’ interests. As a specific example, I took the initiative to start the Negotiation Strategic Plan assignment during the break, as demonstrated in Figure 4. This was received well by my group as it aligned with their individual goal of finishing the assignment relatively stress-free and the team goal of performing well, which is more achievable with good planning and sufficient time to work. My reputation as an organized and proactive student has also assisted in keeping the group on track, as it lent credibility to my actions. 
Figure 4
Telegram Group Chat for Negotiation Strategic Plan Assignment
  

        I also demonstrated both instrumental and expressive leadership through online and in-person interactions with my group. For example, I express concern for the team’s well-being while giving explicit instructions in the message: “I think we can benefit from starting early, how about we bring one potential topic + why it would be a good choice per person and meet sometime next week,” as displayed in Figure 4.[3] 
Evaluation of #ethicalcourage
        Over the Spring semester, I have been consistently volunteering at 826 Valencia. 826 Valencia is an organization based in San Francisco’s Mission district that offers tutoring services to under-resourced students to help them with writing and homework (826 Valencia, n.d.). Given that I was in a position where I could help fill the learning gap that public education failed to address, I signed up as a volunteer tutor. Through the organization, I have been working with students at local high schools for three to six hours a week as a writing coach. Even though I enjoyed teaching, the time commitment posed a significant challenge for me as I had to balance it with academics, personal projects, and my overall well-being. Furthermore, many of the students were not enthusiastic about writing due to a lack of positive classroom experiences and were not very receptive to feedback. This also made consistently showing up difficult as I grappled with the psychological load of wanting to offer help that would benefit the students but would not necessarily be received well. 
        Regardless of these setbacks, I adopted a positive mindset and made it a priority to consistently show up for tutoring sessions. In practice, this translated into declining offers to go out with friends to make time to teach and continue to work with students even when they were unmotivated. This allowed me to break through to my students, including those who were unwilling to write, as I built relationships with them and demonstrated that I care about their progress.[4]
Prospective Improvement
        Overall, I was able to demonstrate strong organizational skills, such as time management, planning, and setting priorities, that allowed me to make continuous progress toward my goals and those of my team when I was working in a group. To strengthen this skill further, I should be careful to accurately, if not over-estimate, how long specific tasks would take in order to prevent the planning fallacy. I was also able to communicate effectively with teammates and my students at 826 Valencia to build relationships with them, which is a skill I can continue to improve on by being attentive to others’ behavioral cues and adapting my approach accordingly.




Word Count:  1,145 words[5]
AI Statement: I used Grammarly.com to proofread the final draft of this assignment.
________________


References
826 Valencia. (n.d.). About. 826 Valencia. Retrieved April 15, 2024, from https://826valencia.org/about/
________________


Appendix
Application of #selfawareness
I identified my strengths, specifically the organizational skills that allow me to act responsibly and perform well as a leader and communication skills that strengthened my teaching and teamwork. In doing so, I drew on evidence to ensure that my self-assessment was not biased. In addition to this, I identified how these strengths translate into outcomes and outlined how I can improve these skills further moving forward.
________________


________________
[1] #purpose: I identified my personal purpose that guided my actions and commitments over the past academic year, and developed a better understanding of my values and motivations. Specifically, i identified that I deeply care about personal growth and helping others grow. I also assessed whether my actual actions aligned with this purpose.
[2] #responsibility: I critiqued the responsibility in my actions throughout the past academic year, focusing on the strategies I employed and how they translated into outcomes. I used the sunk cost fallacy to my advantage and prioritized my goals when planning my days to meet my fitness and professional goals. I also identified factors such as the planning fallacy that led to my failure in following through with my goals in academics.
[3] #leadprinciples: I identified my leadership style, which is informal and founded on expertise in project management, based on the role I take on in team settings and specific interactions with my teammates form the Negotiation Strategic Plan assignment. I also consider how my actions align with individual and team goals.
[4] #ethicalcourage: I identified physical and psychological costs to teaching at 826 Valencia, namely time and the psychological toll of working with unmotivated studetns. I also identified strategies that helped me overcome these costs to consistently show up for my students.
[5] #professionalism: I adhered to assignment instructions, especially to back up claims about myself with evidence, and followed APA format. I was also careful to proofread the assignment for grammatical and spelling errors.

﻿LBA: Ethical Controversies and Conversations




Minerva University
CX51: Complex Systems
Prof. P. Zoogman
February 6, 2024
________________
LBA: Ethical Controversies and Conversations
As public schools struggle in the aftermath of COVID-19 in the United States, there has been much discourse regarding to what degree education should be the responsibility of the public sector (Kamenetz, 2022). The question of whether education, including university and beyond, should be publicized and made free raises the ethical concern of equity and rights in the education sector. While President Biden’s plan for making two years of higher education free and further investing in teachers has not gained nationwide support, some states, including California, are implementing similar policies (Romero, 2022). Advocates of such policies consider education as an investement in broader society, whereas critics voice their concerns about long-term financial sustainability.
        My interlocutor, Stella Gregovich, is of the opinion that “part of public schools being valuable is that [they’re] location-based with the kids around you” and values the community impact that public schools have (S. Gregovich, personal communication, February 2, 2024). On private schools, she feels like they are “a waste, [because] if that funding had been more evenly distributed amongst public education instead of it funding a very elite specific social sector” it would be better for society. While she acknowledges that students should have diverse options for pursuing higher education and private schools fulfill this need, she believes that it would be “better if schools were completely public and free. Or if those options were available beyond just with scholarships to communities, and if they existed locally in communities and wasn’t somewhere you have to commute to or be sent to.” She also believes that public education should be funded through pooling the funds from publicizing current private institutions and progressive taxation where “the richer you are the more you pay in terms of taxes that can be allocated to public education.”
        Underlying Stella’s views that education should be public and free is the belief that education is a positive right, the protection of which is the duty of society as a whole. This right stems from the Kantian principle that humanity is to be treated as an end by respecting individual autonomy. Education should be considered a right as it is foundational to this autonomy since it allows people to make informed decisions, and the state making education accessible by covering costs through progressive taxation serves to protect this right. Such taxation for the welfare of citizens is justified through its purpose to uphold human dignity and citizens’ consent to belong to the state as a form of social contract. To quote Kant, 
For reasons of state the government is therefore authorized to constrain the wealthy to provide the means of sustenance to those who are unable to provide for even their most necessary natural needs. The wealthy have acquired an obligation to the commonwealth, since they owe their existence to an act of submitting to its protection and care, which they need in order to live; on this obligation the state now bases its right to contribute what is theirs to maintaining their fellow citizens. (Kant et al., 2017, p. 110)
Thus, the public is obliged to support students in their educational endeavors as a means to develop and maintain their autonomy.[1] 
Furthermore, Stella’s vision for free community-based education makes education accessible to all people regardless of financial situation or geographic location. This draws on the principle of egalitarianism, as it ensures equality in opportunities to receive education and equality in opportunities made possible by education, such as post-completion career advancement and informed decision-making. In addition, her belief that funds currently going to private education should be reallocated to public education exemplifies Rawls’ difference principle: inequalities in the quality of education stemming from differences in financial capacity are not justified, as they do not necessarily benefit the worst off if the better-educated do not use their education to the advantage of society as a whole.[2]
        While I agree that education should be made more accessible, I am of the opinion that private institutions are equally important as public schools, since they serve as alternate options for students who wish to pursue specific academic paths that public institutions cannot offer in terms of specialization or resources. They also offer parents who wish to give their children a better education the opportunity to invest in schooling, given that their children’s academic abilities are sufficient for them to benefit from it. Completely publicizing education could rob students and their families of these choices, which I believe conflicts with their liberty to pursue whatever education they deem fit for themselves and use their resources, such as funds and academic ability, to their advantage. The coexistence of private and public institutions thus promotes students’ autonomy to freely choose their education and addresses the concern of desert by rewarding merit through scholarships and other opportunities while leaving room for options that do not require as much personal funds as private schools.[3]  
        Stella’s views on public education offered insight into the problem of making education accessible as a societal obligation as opposed to an issue of individual rights. While the difference in scope of the principles underlying our beliefs does not allow for a direct comparison of whose rights and freedoms we prioritize, I have come to agree that education in general should be made more accessible to all. I also acknowledge that private institutions can only, and often disproportionately, benefit a select group of students, specifically those who can afford to attend or qualify for financial aid from merit such as outstanding academic achievements. However, I was not persuaded that publicizing private institutions as a means to redistribute funds necessarily benefits society, as I am unchanged in my belief that students and their families should have the freedom to choose their education and how they expend their resources. I do, however, support the idea of funding public education with progressive taxation, as my view is founded on the premise that the state offers at least satisfactory education, which is often not the case with many schools suffering from a lack of resources and funding.


Word Count: 1009 words
Statement on AI use: I used Grammarly.com to proofread this assignment.


________________


References
Kamenetz, A. (2022, September 1). School is for everyone. The New York Times. https://www.nytimes.com/2022/09/01/opinion/us-school-history.html 
Kant, I., Denis, L., & Gregor, M. J. (2017). The metaphysics of morals. Cambridge University Press. 
Romero, S. (2022, March 31). What if college were free? this state is trying to find out. The New York Times. https://www.nytimes.com/2022/03/31/us/new-mexico-free-college.html  




________________
[1] #levelsofanalysis: The interaction between individual rights and the societal obligation to protect them under the Kantian principle of treating humanity as an end is identified: because individual autonomy, which is made possible through education, is to be respected, the public is obliged to protect the right to education. The interaction serves as justification for progressive taxation.
[2] #ethicalconsiderations: The underlying morals of my interlocutor’s views that public education should be made free and accessible through progressive taxation and publicizing private schools are analyzed using a Kantian rights approach and egalitarianism. These frameworks are justified as the basis of her argument is that education should be accessible to everyone and the public should make this possible.
[3] #ethicaljudgment: My position regarding public education presented under a libertarian framework valuing individual freedom. The position is compared to my interlocutor’s belief that private schools should be publicized to redistribute funds.

﻿Writing Journal Entry 5
MC51


Date: Februrary 19th
Time: 20 minutes
HC: #medium


My current favorite song is “G.I.N.A.S.F.S.” by Fallout Boy, a rock anthem about being hung up on past love. The intro leads in with a catchy guitar riff that sets a fast-tempo eight-beat that is joined by the drums, giving the song a sense of urgency. The strong uses the same riff and rhythm pattern throughout, creating a predictability that makes the song easy to follow and sing along to. While the song is in a major key which makes it sound somewhat happy, the use of dissonant chords, especially in the chorus, keeps it from being too upbeat and in line with the lyrics about the saddening topic of heartbreak. As for texture, the guitar uses a mix of clean and distorted tones to accompany the smooth vocals, further adding to the contrast between the feel of the song and its content. These elements make for the impression of a heartfelt song that is bittersweet, sung to express the anguish of being in love with the memory of someone.




Writing Journal Entry 6


Date: March 1st
Time: 20 minutes
HC: #persuasion


I impulsively went thrifting last weekend when I didn’t necessarily need any more clothes. It was at a pop-up store by a local thrift shop that was hosting an end-of-season sale, where customers could fill a bag with any clothes on display for $15. I found out about the event on a lifestyle account on Instagram that posts things to do in San Francisco. The post was particularly appealing because of the ELM-peripheral approaches it took: they used terms such as “only for this weekend” to emphasize scarcity and “the popular Double Vision Thrift Store” as social proof in the caption, and I already had an affinity for the account itself because of its record of showing me local low-budget activities. 
While I did find clothes that I like and believe to be worth the $15 investment, I can defend myself from such appeals by critically analyzing the text or audio for specific expressions that influence my perception of the message. For example, I would consider the implications of describing Double Vision Thrift Store as “popular” and how that would sway the audience to go shop there. 




Writing Journal Entry 7


Date: March 10th
Time: 20 minutes
HC: none


* #audience: We carefully shaped our message to address the specific needs and perspectives of our key audiences in developing our prototypes for the marketing of Clarion’s Rent FREE April initiative. We highlighted the event’s community and cultural benefits when pitching our initial ideas to our Civic Partner, and ensured that our deliverables were informative, engaging, creative, and visually appealing for potential audience members of Rent FREE April productions and prospective renters of Clarion’s space.
* #gapanalysis: We identified the strengths of the current marketing, such as frequent posting on Instagram and regular updates to the website, and weaknesses such as inconsistency in brand image and visual designs that do not adhere to the standards of #communicationdesign. Thus, our deliverable provides Clarion with templates and schedules for social media posts and a new design scheme for their website to improve existing marketing rather than provide entirely new marketing methods.
* #algorithms: We used JavaScript to animate the scrolling mechanism in early prototypes of the Rent FREE April landing page. We began by creating a flow chart representing the possible user interactions and desired behavior of the page, and branched out using if… elif… else statements, which were directly implemented in the code.
* #shapingbehavior: We added the links to the Rent FREE April booking page and calendar to the top of the landing page as opposed to the bottom in order to make it easier for site visitors to navigate to those pages. This encourages them to book slots for their own productions or look at the calendar to find productions to watch through modifying choice architecture




Writing Journal Entry 8


Date: April 9th
Time: 20 minutes
HC: #composition


The prospect of using augmented reality such as projected computer screens and holograms to facilitate better collaboration and communication in work and learning settings is very exciting to me, as I often feel the limitations of existing technology when communicating with people in such settings. Holographic projections could serve as a powerful visual aid in communication that can help with transmitting ideas like product designs or visions for a user interface, making team communication more effective. They could also provide learners with a more concrete idea of the subject matter they are learning about through 3D imagery. 
While this potential application is exciting, I’m concerned about how it may lead to people devaluing in-person communication as immersive experiences allowing users to share virtual spaces with others become more realistic. Although I believe that such immersive virtual communication serves the purpose of bridging gaps in time and space by allowing people to communicate from different physical locations in a way similar to real-life interactions, they cannot and should not replace actual human interaction. Because users will be more inclined to choose virtual communication for its convenience as it becomes more realistic, I am worried that it may replace in-person interaction and lead to people being deprived of real-life connections.[1]




AI statement: I did not use any AI tools on this assignment including Grammarly.com.
________________
[1] #responsibility: While I struggled with time management during the weekdays due to meetings and job interviews and had not been able to finish each entry before the next class, I budgeted time on my weekends where I had more control over my schedule to finish the prompts. In doing so, I prioritized this assignment under a internal locus of control in order to make writing a habit that I could continually improve on. I also made a goal to intentionally sit down in front of the document and write a sentence on the prompt even when I wasn’t sure how to respond to it in order to build momentum and prevent procrastination under the excuse that I don’t have any ideas.

﻿Video script
MC51
Let’s talk FASHION.
My guess is that when you heard that, you imagined something like this.
What you see here is an example of fast fashion, where consumers purchase clothing frequently at relatively low prices, often to keep up with micro-trends that go out of fashion in a matter of mere weeks - notice that everything is in neutrals this season? That’s exactly what I’m talking about.


Isn’t it sad? You buy SO MUCH clothes, just to wear them less than a dozen times. And it harms the people behind them too.


Workers in the textile industry are often heavily exploited in the form of long hours and low wages. 80% of contractors were found to be violating minimum wage and overtime laws, and some even restrict their employees’ rights to unionize.
Fast fashion retailers pay their factory employees as little as $1.58 - less than a quarter of the federal minimum wage in the US. What’s more, they work up to 19 hours a day. 


This is the reality behind fast fashion. Clothes are produced by exploited workers who work twice as long and earn half as much as someone at a typical 9-5 at minimum wage in the United States. Just for them to end up being worn, say, 6 times before they go out of fashion.


And where do all of these perfectly wearable clothes go? To landfills: 11.3 million tons of textile waste are thrown away in a year, with only 2.5 million tons being recycled. 


What’s more, the PRODUCTION of fast fashion harms the environment. A single pair of jeans takes 2,000 gallons of water to make - enough for a person to live for 7 years. The production of clothing and footware accounts for 8% of of annual greenhouse gas emissions at 1.7 billion tons - more than for international flights and maritime shipping combined.


What sustains this unethical model is the high demand for new clothing to keep up with trends, which makes sense. The more the consumers demand, the more the retailers produce.
How do we fix this? Or even better, how do YOU fix this?
It’s simple. Say no to going to the mall for the greyscale minimalist aesthetic that’s all the rage right now and yes to thrifting for clothes that scream “YOU.” 


Think about it - where’s the fun in looking like everyone else, especially when factory workers and our planet pay for it? Why shop fast fashion when you can dress like this? Or this? Or this, this, this, or this? Thrifting makes for creative, fun outfits like these - and guess what? Each style you just saw on me comes up to less than $10 each. 


Clothes that let you embrace your uniqueness don’t have to be expensive, harm our planet, or take advantage of factory workers. 
Go thrifting, because looking good shouldn’t come at a price. Not for you, our planet, or anyone else.


Resources:
"Slave-like" conditions at Zara supplier — Clean Clothes Campaign
The Exploitation of Garment Workers: Threading the Needle on Fast Fashion | U.S. Department of Labor Blog
Zara and Primark factory workers say they were fired after forming union | Zara | The Guardian.
Circular Economy and Sustainability of the Clothing and Textile Industry - PMC   
Textiles: Material-Specific Data | US EPA 
Assessing the potential of GHG emissions for the textile sector: A baseline study - PMC. 
The Most Important Fast Fashion Statistics [2024]. 
Minimum Wage | U.S. Department of Labor.

name: LBA: Multimodal Persuasion [final]
text_raw:
﻿LBA: Multimodal Persuasion




Minerva University
MC51: Multimodal Communications
Prof. M. Yates
April 4, 2024












________________


LBA: Multimodal Persuasion
Video Link
https://youtu.be/aFN8NfgGQYs
Description and Audience Research
The objective of the video is to illustrate the downsides of fast fashion and persuade the audience to thrift instead. The intended audience is consumers of fast fashion, especially those in their teens and 20s (Gen Z), who shop for clothing routinely to keep up with trends. 
I conducted audience research by consulting online sources on marketing and fashion to understand how my target audience interacts with related content and how they would encounter my video. This led to the insight that they often use Pinterest to gain inspiration on what clothing to wear or purchase (Maguire, 2023). As Pinterest suggests content based on their unique tastes, it can be inferred that the service gained traction because of the audience’s inclination to pursue specific niche trends (often termed “aesthetics”) as opposed to mainstream trends. I also found that they value authenticity and transparency when engaging with apparel brands through social media platforms, including YouTube (Ljunggren, 2023). Furthermore, I found that they have a high interest in sustainable fashion, which translates into support for brands that align with such values (Jiaxing Top Beauty Fashion Co., Ltd., 2023).
In addition to online research, I conducted interviews with members of my target audience. When asked what compels her to shop at fast fashion brands such as Zara and H&M, she mentioned factors such as accessibility and price (K. Moyer, personal communication, March 21st, 2024). 
Based on this information, I decided to cater to the audience’s need for fashion and retailers to meet their standards of individual taste and expressiveness, organizational transparency centered on sustainable values, and low costs.
HC Appendix
#audience: The content of the script, the video, and, most importantly, persuasion techniques are adapted to the audience characterized above: Gen Z consumers of fast fashion, which aligns with the purpose of the video to deter people from shopping fast fashion given that they are the largest consumer base. I conducted research on the target audience through online searches and interviews, which informed my choice to use Zara as an example of fast fashion due to its familiarity (0:08). I drew on statistics on labor rights (0:40-1:10) and environmental impact (1:34-2:09) to appeal to the audience’s interest in organizational transparency and sustainability. In doing so, I deliberately used expressions such as “enough [water] for a person to live seven years” to give the audience a better understanding of the magnitude of the large numbers (1:57). I also appealed to their ideals of fashion as a form of expression by portraying thrifting as a means to curate individualized outfits (2:48) at affordable prices (3:00).
#persuasion: I employed two lines of persuasion: the central route of the Elaboration Likelihood (ELM) method and Cognitive Dissonance Theory (CDT). For the ELM-central route, I used numerical data and statistics (0:40-2:09) to persuade the audience of the unethical background of fast fashion in order to make use of the audience’s existing interest. For CDT, I made the case that thrifting will better align with the audience’s values of transparency and sustainability while fulfilling their need for affordable, expressive clothing (3:06).
#communicationdesign: I employed graphics as a visual aid to my narration and depicted statistics on the labor rights and environmental impact of fast fashion (0:40-1:09, 1:32-2:11). The bar graphs at 1:08 adhered to the principles of discriminability (the color for textile workers was highly contrasted against the color for US workers and is red, which people are drawn to instinctively) and salience (the pay gap and difference in hours are large, which effectively convey the point that textile workers are undercompensated and overworked). The bar graph at 1:34 similarly adheres to the principles of discriminability and salience in addition to the principle of compatibility (the background is an image of a landfill). The slide at 1:58 also employs the principle of compatibility (the background shows pairs of jeans) with the principle of appropriate knowledge (2,000 gallons of water is put into a more tangible context). 
#multimedia: The video holistically employs music, narration, graphics, and footage to convey the message that fast fashion harms workers and the environment. In the intro explaining fast fashion (0:09-0:30), the music is from the vaporwave genre, which is commonly played in shopping malls, and the footage directly depicts a typical consumer shopping in bulk to illustrate the problem of overconsumption. In the part explaining the consequences of fast fashion (0:30-2:41), the music is a somber melody in a minor key in order to add dramatic emphasis to the statistics in the visuals, which are animated to draw attention. In the part suggesting thrifting as an alternative (2:41-3:25), my clothing is originally in greyscale to make a point (“say no to the greyscale aesthetic” at 2:30) but changes with match cuts to make the point that thrifting has the potential for unique styling (2:48). 
#designthinking: I began my design process with a storyboard and piloted a scene in stop motion using clothing, a style I wanted to use in my final video. This gave me a better understanding of the methods of stop motion and an idea of how much time and effort it would take, and I decided to not use it due to it being difficult given time constraints. I also developed a script, which I updated from using a narrative paradigm to using the ELM-central route with my decision to not use stop-motion as can be seen in the version history. I also piloted several different versions of slide animations and stock footage on Canva.
#professionalism: I adopted an informal mode of speech to adapt to the audience and dressed informally to better convey my message. I was also careful to follow the guidelines in the assignment instructions especially the length of my video.
#expression: I used techniques of verbal delivery and body language throughout the video. Specifically, I was conscious of my intonation and pacing for “This is the reality” at 1:10, “half” at 1:18, “combined” at 2:11, and “you” at 2:38, where I was conscious of slowing down and enunciating carefully. I was conscious of my body language at the end (2:12-3:13), especially when I use hand gestures at “no” (2:31), “yes” (2:36), and when I directly referred to the audience with hand gestures at 2:38. I also refer to my outfit with gestures throughout the section. Furthermore, I stood with a wide stance and squared shoulders to appear more confident. 


AI Statement: I used no AI tools in this assignment, including Grammarly.com. All proofreading was done manually.


________________


References
Clean Clothes Campaign. (n.d.). "Slave-like" conditions at Zara supplier — Clean Clothes Campaign. Clean Clothes Campaign. Retrieved April 10, 2024, from https://cleanclothes.org/issues/migrants-in-depth/stories/slave-like-conditions-at-zara-supplier
Jiaxing Top Beauty Fashion Co., Ltd. (2023, August 17). Fashion choices of Generation Z. Linkedin. Retrieved April 11, 2024, from https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/fashion-choices-generation-z-jiaxing-top-beauty-fashion-co-ltd/
Lindner, J. (2024). The Most Important Fast Fashion Statistics [2024]. Gitnux. Retrieved April 10, 2024, from https://gitnux.org/fast-fashion-statistics/
Ljunggren, T. (2023, July 29). The Power of Video Marketing: Reaching and Engaging Gen Z. LinkedIn. Retrieved April 11, 2024, from https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/power-video-marketing-reaching-engaging-gen-z-gentle-thug/
Maguire, L. (2023, September 4). How Pinterest became Gen Z's favourite fashion inspo. Vogue Business. Retrieved April 10, 2024, from https://www.voguebusiness.com/technology/how-pinterest-became-gen-zs-favourite-fashion-inspo
Rosalez, R. (2023, March 21). The Exploitation of Garment Workers: Threading the Needle on Fast Fashion. DOL Blog. Retrieved April 10, 2024, from https://blog.dol.gov/2023/03/21/the-exploitation-of-garment-workers-threading-the-needle-on-fast-fashion
Sainato, M. (2020, June 24). Zara and Primark factory workers say they were fired after forming union. The Guardian. https://www.theguardian.com/fashion/2020/jun/24/zara-primark-factory-workers-myanmar-fired-union
United States Department of Labor. (n.d.). Minimum Wage. U.S. Department of Labor. Retrieved April 10, 2024, from https://www.dol.gov/general/topic/wages/minimumwage

name: Interpretation with #medium + #context [final]
text_raw:
﻿Interpretation with #medium + #context




Minerva University
MC51: Multimodal Communications
Prof. M. Yates
February 27, 2024












________________


Interpretation with #medium + #context
1. Link to YouTube video
https://youtu.be/oUFBIzES94s 
2. Work analyzed
I analyzed a haiku by Fujiwara no Toshiyuki Ason:
“住の江の 岸による波 よるさへや 夢の通ひ路 人めよくらむ (Tani, 2010.)”
and its translation by Clay Maccauley:
“The waves are gathered
On the shore of Sumi Bay,
And in the gathered night,
When in dreams I go to you,
I hide from people’s eyes. (Maccauley, 1917)”
   3. Script of narration
Hello, and thank you for watching. In this video, I’ll be talking about a haiku from 800s Japan.
Haikus are a form of Japanese poetry comprised of the “kaminoku,” the first line with syllables clustered into groups of 5, 7, and 5, and the “shimonoku,” the second line with syllables clustered into groups of 7 and 7. They are typically season-themed and heavy on imagery, but can also be about love or travel. The haiku I will introduce you to here was written by Fujiwara no Toshiuki Ason early in the Heian period and is included in the “Ogura Hyakunin Isshu,” a collection of the greatest 100 haikus written before the 1300s. The original version goes like this:
(play  audio of professional reading of Japanese version)
As you just heard, the traditional oral delivery has a tonality and rhythm very different from contemporary Japanese. The widely acclaimed English translation by Clay Maccauley goes like this:
“The waves are gathered
On the shore of Sumi Bay,
And in the gathered night,
When in dreams I go to you,
I hide from people’s eyes. (Maccauley, 1917)”
Let’s go back to the Japanese version for an in-depth analysis, starting with the kaminoku. The highlighted text means “the waves approaching Sumi Bay.” This imagery evokes a sense of contemplative loneliness. Here, the author uses a technique called “kakekotoba,” the use of homophones to add layers of meaning. The highlighted word “よる” is used in two meanings: the verb “to approach” and the noun “night.” The repetition of the word “よる” gives the haiku a rhythm akin to that of waves drawing and receding, further adding to the image of waves washing up under the veil of night. The repetition is also used to convey the ambivalence of longing for and loathing a lover who is nowhere to be seen. The words “さへや” translates to “even,” making the overall meaning of the phrase “even at night.” This leads into the shimonoku.
While this phrase “夢の通ひ路” can be simply translated into “pathway in dreams,” the verb used to form it, “通ひ,” implies that the path is not only used to reach a destination but is used routinely, as if the destination is somewhere one would go often. This is a direct reference to the model of marriage and relationships at the time the haiku was written, where a man lives away from his partner and visits her at night, only to leave at sunrise (Takamure, 1963). In addition, people at the time believed that someone appearing in their dreams was a sign that the person loved them (Nakamura, 1999). Thus, a person not passing through the pathway of someone’s dreams would signify waning love. This phrase “人めよくらむ” is composed of three parts: the noun “人め,” “people’s eyes,” the yerb “よく,” to avoid, and the auxiliary verb “らむ,” which signifies that the statement is a guess. The verb has no subject, as Japanese allows for its omission if it is implied. Given that at the time men traveled to see women, the common interpretation is that the subject is a man described from the point of view of a female character that Fujiwara no Toshiyuki, a man from the aristocracy, made up. 
From this breakdown of the individual phrases, the haiku’s overall interpretation goes like this: Not that I’m talking about the “よる” as in the waves approaching the Sumi Bay, but could it be that he does not come to visit me, even at night passing through the pathway of my dreams, because he is avoiding people’s eyes? 
I also created a more poetic line-by-line translation of my own for reference:
At the Sumi Bay,
Waves gather at the shore
Even in the night,
Through pathways of my dreams he comes not,
Perhaps avoiding people’s eyes
Now, you may recall that this significantly differs from the English translation by Maccauley, as the interpretation of “avoiding people’s eyes” fails to take into account the historical context of Heian-era Japan. Furthermore, while the translation retains the meter and much of the essence of the original, it loses the rich layers of meaning originally introduced by the use of haiku-specific techniques and the Japanese language. My translation also falls short in this, although it offers a more historically accurate interpretation. While versions in different languages may make the haiku available to a broader audience, there is much, unfortunately, that is lost in translation. 
   4. Applied HCs
   * #medium: Elements of the haiku are analyzed and interpreted in tandem with the message directly conveyed through the prose. The author’s use of poetry- and haiku-specific techniques (rhythm, repetition, imagery, and “kakekotoba”) are used to evoke the mental image of the secluded Sumi Bay at night and convey the character’s loneliness and conflicting feelings about her partner. 
   * #context: The historical context of Heian-era Japan allows for an understanding of why the acclaimed Maccauley translation misinterprets the subject of the phrase “人めよくらむ” and supports the new interpretation. The phrase “夢の通ひ路”  is also interpreted in a historical context, drawing on the beliefs and customs of the time. 
   * #communicationdesign: A minimalistic style is chosen for the slides to draw attention to the text of the haiku under the principle of limited capacity. Visual cues such as highlighting are also incorporated to assist the audience in understanding the section under analysis in an unfamiliar alphabet under the principles of discriminability (the highlighted text stands out) and informative change. The style also incorporates Japanese design elements, such as the ink-like font and images of mizuhiki, another traditional Japanese art form, to align with the cultural context of the haiku.
   * #designthinking: I began the design process of the presentation by developing ideas on how to present a haiku, which would be in a language unfamiliar to the audience, without subtracting from its meaning. Ideas included creating a translation and conducting an in-depth analysis of it, using an existing translation and thoroughly critiquing it, and breaking the original down and providing relevant translation as the analysis goes. The last idea was used in the presentation, which had the issue of going into too much depth without providing the audience with sufficient context. The details were iteratively improved with feedback from non-Japanese speaking peers in order to mitigate bias in my assumption of the audience’s understanding of haiku- and Japanese-specific elements of the work.
   * #professionalism: I was careful to proofread and fact-check my presentation, especially as the audience does not necessarily understand Japanese, and had a Japanese peer review my work. I also adhered to the industry standards of translation by running my translation by native English-speaking peers (i.e. native checks). Furthermore, I was careful to cite my sources and format this document in APA style.
   * #expression: I was careful to narrate slowly and clearly, especially when enunciating Japanese words, in order to allow the audience time to understand the unfamiliar concepts introduced in the presentation. I was also conscious of tone and pauses in order to sound confident and collected.
   * #connotation: I introduced the connotation of the word “通う,” which typically translates to “go” but implies routine or habit (as in “I go to school in San Francisco”). This allowed for the phrase “夢の通ひ路” to be situated in the historical context of relationships in early Heian era Japan.
   5. References
Hashimoto, K., & Takamure, I. (1967). Nihon Koninshi ; Renairon. [The history of marriage in Japan: a theory on romance] Rironsha.
Maccauley, C. (1917). Hyakunin-Isshu (single songs of a Hundred poets) and nori-no-hatsu-ne (the dominant note of the law) literal translations into English with renderings according to the original metre. Kelly and Walsh.
Nakamura, A. (1999). Hanten suru yume・danzetsu suru yume: heian makki shikashu ni miru  no ishiki. [Dreams that invert/ dreams that cease to exist: the interpretation of dreams by late heian period private collections] Nihonbungaku, 48(7). https://doi.org/nihonbungaku.48.7_1 
Tani, T. (2015). Hyakunin Isshu (Zen): Bigināzu Kurashikkusu Nihon no Koten. [One hundred poets, one poem each: beginner’s classics classics of Japan]. Kabushiki Kaisha Kadokawa. 
YouTube. (2015, May 11). 百人一首 018 藤原敏行朝臣 住の江の 岸に寄る波 よるさへや 夢の通ひ路 人目よくらむ. [Entry number 18 in the ogura hyakunin isshu: “suminoeno kishi ni yoru nami yorusaeya yumenokayoiji hitomeyokuramu” by Fujiwara no Toshiyuki Ason]YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tLgLo0u3v58  
   6. Note on AI use
I used Grammarly.com to proofread my script for the narration. I did not use any other AI tools for this assignment.

name: Biases [final]
text_raw:
﻿Biases




Minerva University
EA51: Empirical Analyses
Prof. N. Klein
February 1, 2024












________________


Biases
Part A: A Comparison of Biases in Human and Artificial Intelligence
Human biases affect the results produced by large language models (LLMs) in the training of the model and prompting from users (Dhingra et al., 2023; University of Waterloo, 2023). In the former, models inherit societal injustices present in the training data, which is primarily comprised of unprocessed web text. In a 2023 study, Dhingra et al. found that LLMs prompted to describe people based on biographies tended to place members of the LGBTQ+ community in lower regard than their straight counterparts or a control group of people described using gender-neutral language, likely due to their negative portrayal in the training data. In addition to such inherent biases, the confirmation bias in prompts from users may also cause bias in responses by LLMs. As illustrated in Stokel-Walker (2023), the wording of the prompts given to LLMs can perpetuate confirmation bias when the user actively seeks information that aligns with their existing views. Thus, users may inadvertently be exposed to information that hinders the formulation of unbiased opinions or strengthens their confirmation bias unless they are aware of these biases in results from LLMs.[1][2] 
When prompted with “Write 100-150 words answering the question, ‘How might cognitive biases affect the results that a large language model like ChatGPT produces, and how might biases influence the way you use those results?’” ChatGPT 3.5 responded with the following:
Cognitive biases can significantly impact the outcomes of a large language model like ChatGPT. The model is trained on diverse datasets reflecting human language, making it susceptible to inheriting societal biases. Confirmation bias, for instance, might cause the model to prioritize information that aligns with pre-existing beliefs in the training data. This could result in skewed or incomplete responses. Additionally, availability bias may lead the model to emphasize commonly occurring themes, potentially neglecting minority perspectives.
When utilizing ChatGPT's results, it's crucial to be aware of these biases. Users should approach the information critically, recognizing that the model might unintentionally perpetuate or amplify societal prejudices. To mitigate biases, users should cross-verify information from diverse sources and actively engage in the ongoing effort to enhance the fairness and neutrality of language models. Balancing the outputs of ChatGPT with a discerning human perspective is essential to ensure responsible and unbiased utilization of the model. (OpenAI, 2024)
While my response to the prompt in the first paragraph includes the inheritance of societal biases, which is also present in the response from ChatGPT, I also identified the effect of individual-level human cognitive biases on the responses of LLMs, where ChatGPT was more focused on how LLMs themselves may be subject to biases due to trends in its training data. My response may have been subject to confirmation bias, as I had access to potential sources in this assignment’s instructions that align with my understanding that LLMs are susceptible to inheriting societal stereotypes. Unconscious use of the availability heuristic may have also caused attention bias, as I had read about the effects of biased training data on machine learning models for a recent FA51 session and have personal experience giving ChatGPT prompts worded slightly differently to work around OpenAI’s guidelines on the topic of self-harm to explore potential topics for the CX50 irrational behavior assignment.[3] In order to mitigate biases caused by the model’s training, users can strive to use neutral language, such as “police officer” as opposed to “policeman,” in prompts as a means of blinding the LLM to irrelevant contextual information. They should also seek alternative or opposing viewpoints in order to mitigate user confirmation bias, such as by using prompts phrased as “What are the pros and cons of this?” as opposed to “Why is this beneficial?” and cross-checking the provided information using relevant external sources including those with different, if not opposing viewpoints.[4]
Part B: Biases and Learning
In the Fall 2023 semester, I relied on summarization, distributed practice, and interleaved practice identified by Dunlosky et al. (2013) in addition to self-explanation in my class preparation and follow-up study. In doing so, I was able to apply principles of learning outlined in Kosslyn (2017) such as deep processing through summarization of pre-class readings, deliberate practice in applying learned material in classes and incorporating feedback from professors and TAs, appropriate examples and the generation effect in self-explanation using original examples, and interleaving and spaced practice in how I scheduled my study sessions. In addition, I strived to apply learned material and skills to different contexts, such as by applying #connotation, which was introduced in MC50, in my analysis of an advertisement in the logical thinking LBA for FA50.[5] 
My learning was in part negatively influenced by memory bias when I was somewhat familiar with the material from past education, especially in FA50. This caused me to falsely remember learning the material in the study guide when my understanding was insufficient for the class or I had not learned it at all, leading me to skim some important readings and have to re-read them afterward. I was also affected by emotional bias, where receiving poor grades on CX50 assignments led to reduced confidence in the course and, consequently, reduced verbal contributions in the next several classes. Top-down attentional bias, on the other hand, assisted me as selective attention allowed me to focus on relevant aspects and key terminology in dense readings when I read articles using the study guide as a reference. With what I learned from the bias unit in CX51, I will strive to mitigate the negative effects of memory bias when I am studying familiar concepts by approaching readings with the intent of cross-verifying my knowledge to ensure that my understanding is sufficient and be careful to take note of any discrepancies in order to prevent confirmation bias. In addition, I will try to reframe poor grades as an opportunity to reflect and the following classes as an environment in which I can safely make mistakes and learn from them in order to mitigate emotional bias.[6] 


Word Count (excluding text generated by ChatGPT): 850 words
Statement on AI use: I used ChatGPT 3.5 to generate the text in green in Part A of this assignment. As outlined in the instructions, the prompt was: “Write 100-150 words answering the question, ‘How might cognitive biases affect the results that a large language model like ChatGPT produces, and how might biases influence the way you use those results?’” I included the generated answer in Part A and compared it with my own answer to the same question. I used Grammarly.com to proofread the other parts of the assignment.


________________


References
Dhingra, H., Jayashakner, P., Moghe, S. & Strubell, E. (2023). Queer People are People First: Deconstructing Sexual Identity Stereotypes in Large Language Models. arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2307.00101
Dunlosky, J., Rawson, K. A., Marsh, E. J., Nathan, M. J., & Willingham, D. T. (2013). Improving students' learning with effective learning techniques: Promising directions from cognitive and educational psychology. Psychological Science in the Public Interest, 14(1), 4-58. https://course-resources.minerva.edu/uploaded_files/mu/00251893-1494/2013-dunlosky-psych-sci-public-interest-pg4-58.pdf 
Kosslyn, S. M. (2017). The Science of Learning. In S. M. Kosslyn & B. Nelson (Eds.), Building the Intentional University: Minerva and the Future of Higher Education. MIT Press. https://course-resources.minerva.edu/uploaded_files/mke/00069173-9345/kosslyn--s.--2017-.-the-science-of-learning.pdf
OpenAI. (2024). ChatGPT (Feb 2 version) [Large language model]. https://chat.openai.com/chat 
ChatGPT and generative artificial intelligence (AI): Potential for bias based on prompt. (2023, December 5). University of Waterloo.
https://subjectguides.uwaterloo.ca/chatgpt_generative_ai/chatgptbias 


________________
[1] #biasidentification: Two aspects of confirmation bias on the user end, seeking information that aligns with their views and them using the information, are identified in the interactions between users and LLMs. 
[2] #evidencebased: Two lines of evidence are employed to strengthen the argument that human biases can cause bias in LLMs. While counterarguments could not be addressed do to constraints of word count, the claims made in the paragraph are backed with relevant evidence.
[3] #biasidentification: Two biases that may have affected my response to the prompt are identified through comparison with the response by ChatGPT, which in itself is unbiased: confirmation bias and attention bias. The sources of each are identified as the consumption of information that aligned with my preconceived ideas and the interaction between the use of the availability heuristic and attention bias.
[4] #biasmitigation: Bias mitigation strategies specific to using LLMs are proposed. The strategies specifically address the biases in the response caused by confirmation bias in users and the confirmation bias itself by ensuring that the response is as neutral as possible and the user considers opposite and/or alternative options.
[5] #scienceoflearning: The underlying learning principles of specific study techniques are identified. The application of each technique is justified by elements of the Minerva active learning model.
[6] #biasmitigation: Feasible bias mitigation strategies relevant to the context of learning are proposed. A potential downside of the mitigation strategy for memory is identified and addressed, strengthening the strategy.
