﻿Elevation Reflection & Engagement
GL91


Reflecting on the Fall semester, in what ways have you been able to think, observe, or discuss inclusive or exclusive community practices within the Minerva community? In the Spring semester, how can we continue to move the Minerva community towards an increasingly anti-racist foundation. Give specific examples. Submit by January 5th. (150 words)


I engaged in multiple conversations about how Minerva community members are handling the topic of the “Israel-Palestine conflict,” which also spanned the discussion of whether it should be branded as a mere conflict. I have observed that some students are advocating for a cease-fire and restoration of the human rights of Palestinians, which in itself is not an inherently exclusive act nor should it be a “sensitive” topic to be avoided. However, I have come to believe that insufficient differentiation of the state and people of Israel is causing our Israeli community members to feel excluded. 
In the next semester and onwards, we should set community guidelines for navigating difficult topics with empathy towards individual stakeholders, respecting personal views and choices regarding activism, and foster a culture of upholding these values, potentially by reaching out to students on Community Portal or hosting information sessions. (144 words)

name: Ethnographic Assignment [final]
text_raw:
﻿Ethnographic Assignment




Minerva University
GL91: Global Learning I
October 16, 2023












________________


Ethnographic Assignment
I interviewed Walerka Oprawa. We had followed each other on social media for several months before arriving in San Francisco and met in person for the first time at a Foundation Week event. We immediately hit it off but did not see each other very often after that because we lived in different residence halls. We initially hit it off as fellow members of the LGBTQ community who share similar tastes in fashion and music. We also often share recommended travel destinations and study spots in the city with each other. She is from Kwidzyn, Poland, where she lived with her parents and younger brother. I decided to interview Walerka because I was interested in the cultural background that had informed her warm, welcoming personality, and Poland has a very different culture from my home country, Japan.
Walerka informed me that people in Poland highly value family connections. Walerka lived in the same town as her paternal aunts and uncles, many cousins whom she was close with, and grandparents, who all converged on a day-to-day basis. They kept in touch with relatives living far away as well, and they visited each other during holidays, especially Christmas, for family gatherings. This trend exists nationwide, with many families living in the same cities as their relatives and visiting each other for holidays. Families are very close-knit and hold a special meaning in Polish society. They also value hospitality, and guests are welcomed and even pampered. According to Walerka, guests should expect to be offered drinks and too many confectionaries. She also shared that she tries to recreate this sense of home in the Minerva community by being warm and welcoming to others. Furthermore, Polish people as a custom often keep family heirlooms, which in Walerka’s case is her grandmother’s cookie recipe. 
Poland celebrates Christian events, such as Easter and Christmas. However, these celebrations have gradually lost their religious association over time and developed into opportunities to spend time with family. The people spend weeks in anticipation of the events, planning menus for feasts, choosing gifts, creating invitee lists, and decorating their homes. These events have associated traditions, such as Easter-themed food and pastries blessed at church, painting eggs, egg hunts, and family feasts for Easter, and at least 12 dishes on the menu to symbolize the 12 months in a year for Christmas. They also celebrate All Saint’s Day after Halloween, when they go to cemeteries with intricately designed lanterns to visit relatives buried there.
The Polish language, although having Slavic origins, uses the Latin alphabet instead of the Cyrillic alphabet. They have similar grammatical structure and word roots to neighboring languages such as Ukrainian. The language has honorifics as a means to address elders and people in authority with respect. The associated body language does not differ significantly from other Slavic languages, but it is often said that Polish people do not smile much. I found this difficult to believe as my Polish classmates are very friendly and smile a lot.


Word Count: 500 words
I used Grammarly.com to proofread the final draft of this assignment.

name: MC50 journal assignment 1 [final]
text_raw:
﻿MC50


Date: Sep 6
Time: 25 mins 
HC: #interpretivelens


This May, I went to a house party held by a Minerva alum from M19 back home in Japan. He was hosting the party for Minervans who were stationed in the Tokyo area at the time, and about fifteen people came. The host told me and a few other incoming students we could ask him anything, so I asked how the Japanese Minervans he knew liked living in all of the rotation cities. He told me about a specific alum from M21: he tried smoking weed in California and ended up liking it. For context, the majority of addictive drugs, including Cannabis, are illegal in Japan. So, having grown up under the impression that all addictive drugs are illegal, this new information was a huge shock to me. I was horrified to learn that Minerva allowed students to smoke weed. The person from M19 told me that it was perfectly legal in California, but the idea still freaked me out.
Now I know that buying Cannabis is legal in California if you’re at least 18 years old and have it prescribed to you, or if you’re at least 21. I also know that Minerva is fine with students smoking weed as long as they follow the state laws and don’t do it in school facilities. I was sitting in the FW session about drug and alcohol policies and it hit me: I was freaking out over nothing, at least by California standards.










Date: Sep 15
Time: 25 mins
HC: #critique


The main character, Louise, always uses the future tense in the scenes about her daughter, and the past tense in the scenes about the heptapods. She only uses the present tense in the scenes where her husband asks “Do you want to make a baby?” From this, we can imply that the present is in that moment and that all of Louise’s “memories” about “you,” her daughter, are closer in nature to visions of the future. Regardless of this quality of the “memories,” the loving tone in which Louise describes her daughter gives us the impression that she has already experienced motherhood. In a sense, she already has: learning Heptapod B altered her memory, and she now experiences reality in a mode of simultaneity with no distinction between the past, present, and future. Because of this, she knows her daughter as not an individual whom she knows in the present, but whom she knows the entire story of. She has a deep understanding of her daughter’s personality, even in the present when she is yet to be conceived, and already shares a beautiful, loving relationship with her.








Date: Sep 20
Time: 10 mins
HC: #critique


#critique: I drew on patterns in language use that I identified through close reading and annotation. The author followed a strict pattern for his use of tense that illuminates the timeframe of each event in the story. He also employed language with positive connotations when the main character is describing or interacting with her daughter to shed light on Louise’s sentiments toward her. 








Date: Sep 28
Time: 15 mins
HC: #thesis


I believe that the education model in Minerva should be implemented in secondary schools. I went to a traditional Japanese middle school and high school, where classes were lecture-based and our grades were largely dependent on test scores. Although there is a lot I learned in that traditional setting, I noticed that many of my peers struggled with retaining knowledge and gaining a deep understanding of the material. Coming to Minerva, I realized that the education model here has numerous advantages. One is the expectations placed on students regarding class preparation. The classes are structured to build on prior knowledge, fill in any gaps, and exchange ideas through discussion- a model that only functions well if the participating students are adequately prepared for the session. This invokes a sense of responsibility in the students, which translates into reliability in a professional setting. Another benefit is the diverse perspectives that can be attained through exchanging ideas. Active learning through discussion facilitates the exchange of ideas between students, constructive criticism, and deeper engagement with the topic. On the other hand, in a lecture, the only sources of input are textbooks, readings, and the instructor. The final benefit I would like to mention is that the model is based on the science of learning. The principles the model is based on, such as the generative effect invoked by in-class discussion and deliberate practice through answering poll questions, are scientifically proven to be effective. The following sentence is the thesis for an essay or paper advocating this standing that I would like to get feedback on:
Active learning, which facilitates learning through discussion and engagement with the topic, should also be implemented in secondary education because it is based on the science of learning, making it more effective than lectures.














Date: Oct 4
Time: 15 mins
HC: #evidencebased


I believe that Appiah’s The Lies That Bind was the most compelling piece of evidence because it offered extensive insight into the role that identity plays in group dynamics.
I plan to write my Thesis-driven essay for the assignment around the thesis “Patriotism and nationalism may have different definitions, but the Japanese do not distinguish strictly between them; they have no sense of either and derive their sense of identity from the notion of what does not constitute a Japanese national.” If I were to cite this source in my Thesis-driven easy assignment, the footnote would look like this:
#evidencebased: This evidence was employed to strengthen my point that shared identities not only create a sense of belonging to a group but also create a divide between so-called in-groups and out-groups. I presented summaries and paraphrases to support the translation of the notion of this divide into a Japanese societal context.

name: MC50 journal assignment 2 [final]
text_raw:
﻿MC50


Date: Oct 29[1]
Time: 20 mins 
HC: #composition


        With its many murals, street performers, and venues, San Francisco is known for its vibrant arts scene. However, the arts community has been facing a dire challenge in the past few years: a severe lack of funding and attention. To combat this problem, this civic project aims to draw a diverse audience to our partner organization, Clarion Performing Arts Center, and integrate technology in marketing to make them more appealing to different grant donors. In doing so, we aspire to bring Clarion’s ideal to life: we will “bring the world to Chinatown.”






Date: Nov 19
Time: 20 mins 
HC: #composition


        Japanese national identity is founded on the illusion that Japan is racially and culturally homogenous. Contrary to this belief, Japan has several minority groups that had once been independent tribes and kingdoms, such as the Ainu from Hokkaido and the Ryūkyūans from Okinawa. The homeland of these groups had been colonized and annexed in the Meiji era when the people were forced to assimilate into mainstream Japanese culture through education and biological integration. Although much of their native culture had been lost due to continued oppression, there are movements to restore their tradition today.
        The Ainus and Okinawans have been severely marginalized since their integration into Japanese society on the basis of racial inferiority. They continue to be underrepresented and disadvantaged in education and the workspace. While the Japanese are largely insensitive to topics of race due to their misconception of homogeneity, structural racism still defines the actions of the minority groups assimilated into Japan.






















Date: Nov 19
Time: 20 mins 
HC: #interpetivelens


I received the feedback:
"Your answer hints at some concept from the reading, such as motivated perception, but it still remained too vague to see a clear connection between #interpetivelens and beginner's mind. To improve your answer, naming and elaborating on the role of, in your case motivated perception, to connect #interpretivelens to beginner's mind."


I learned that developing a strong understanding of key terminology and being able to apply the concepts in different contexts is important in demonstrating engagement with the pre-class readings. In the case of this poll, I should have provided an explanation of motivated perception, which is when our motives affect our inferences of communications and elaborated on its role in #interpretivelens. This would be explaining that acknowledging the influence of motivated perception and being aware of our motives can mitigate the biases present in our interpretations.




Date: Nov 27
Time: 10 mins 
HC: #composition


Growing up mixed race is complicated. People assume that you have parents from completely different backgrounds who fell in love despite their differences. They think these two people nurtured and cultivated this love, going on to start a family that mixes both of their cultures and languages. This is the single story most Japanese people have of the upbringing of mixed-race children—even when it doesn't hold true. When people ask me about my ethnicity and I tell them my father was American- emphasis on "was"- they assume that I can speak English because and only because of his presence. However, this is far from the truth. In reality, my parents separated before I was even born, and my mother taught me English the best she could as a non-native speaker. She made me fall in love with the language and gave me the chance to learn more in an international school. My father was never a part of my story, unlike most people assume. Ethnicities are complicated. Identities are complicated. Families are complicated. You should never assume, lest you mistake people and their unique backgrounds for what they are not.










Statement on AI use:
I did not use any AI tools in this assignment.
________________
[1] #responsibility: I made a rule to complete each journal entry before the next prompt rolled out in order to prevent procrastinating and writing all of the entries in one sitting. I was unable to follow through on this rule as assignments started piling up, but blocked out time to catch up during friendsgiving break. I also took the journal prompts as an opportunity to review class material.

name: LBA: My Utopia or Dystopia [final]
text_raw:
﻿LBA: My Utopia/Dystopia






Minerva University
MC50: Multimodal Communications
Prof. D. Grant
November 30, 2023












________________


LBA: My Utopia/Dystopia
The date is November 26th, 2187. Half a century since the ocean swallowed the Golden City. There was once a time when the city overflowed with the joy of expression: vibrant murals adorned the sides of buildings, street musicians drummed to the bustling rhythm of city life, and poetry covered walls and fences. The heart of the city, where the pulse of creativity kept time, was an outdoor gallery of sculptures, testimony to the flourishing art scene. The heart-shaped sculptures are more than mere structures; they are stories of the city and its diverse people, artfully told and cast in steel and stone.
It is a day of grieving in the city of San Francisco. Families, now with children and grandchildren who only know life after the Great Flood, row out into the submerged streets on boats laden with flowers and scented candles in paper boats. They embank at the roofs of the structures they once called home, treading carefully with offerings in hand. As the children set paper boats down to be borne away by the current, laughing as they are whisked away, their parents place flowers around shrines dedicated to loved ones lost to the sea. Looking at the waters threatening to take their rooftop shrines, they wonder how many more years they can keep alive the memories of those swept away.[1]
A single paper boat, carrying a candle smelling of lilies, floats down Mission Street.[2] It is empty save for the paper vessels and families visiting their past homes on this day of remembrance. What once was a mural-covered neighborhood alive with activity is now a graveyard for memories eroded by time; where there were once bustling markets there are now the remnants of buildings, some of their roofs barely visible above the water’s surface. The Flood rendered spaces below three stories uninhabitable, pushing many residents of the Mission into the little space far enough from the water. Families share apartments, a bedroom each. The hearts of the Mission once beat with the buzz of its marketplaces, currency coursing through its veins and breathing life into the community. The pulse is now little more than an aching throb, barely holding on by the thread of the thriving remnants of the neighborhood. The people, as much as they wish to alleviate the suffering of their neighbors, cannot offer solace as they themselves struggle to make ends meet.
As the paper boat makes its way to South of Market, larger structures replace the ruins of two- and three-story buildings. Museums and parks, pleasant sights in the past, are now home to aquatic creatures unaware of their aesthetic value. Sounds of rippling water echo in theaters and opera houses; cafes and restaurants have become feeding grounds for nearshore species. Shopping malls stand submerged, their only visitors schools of fish. Most retailers left as the waters washed away their in-store stock and profits sunk. There are people, however, who still call SoMa home; they are the inhabitants of the many apartment units who chose to rebuild. They are the teachers, doctors, tailors, builders, farmers of rooftop gardens – they are the ones who keep the community alive. SoMa’s heartbeats are no longer the strong tremor of consumerism they once were, but rather the steady, albeit much weaker, pulse of a communal life outside of commerce.
As the sun begins to set, the current takes the boat to the Financial District, where facilities that once drew people from all over the world bleed into a cityscape of corporate skyscrapers submerged at the base. Built on coastal sea-level land, the neighborhood was one of the first to go when the Great Flood struck. It was also the first to see its tenants go; the multi-million dollar startups and their employees were quick to jump ship.[3] The picture windows of long abandoned high-rise offices and studio apartments look down on waterlogged streets. Furnished apartments, unchanged from when their owners fled in the night, stand silent and desolate as the slanting afternoon sun draws patterns on their floors. Predatory fish lurk in submerged cars, awaiting their next victim. Water seeps into the cracks in algae-covered walls of empty lobbies, the gleam of their porcelain floors dulled with sediment carried in by the currents. Unmanned front desks serve as nooks for sea creatures to rest in.
 The derelict neighborhood bears no semblance to what it once was: there are no longer headlights painting lines on Highway 101 nor are there city lights blotting out the stars at night. The city that had been lit up from dusk to dawn is now devoid of light and life. The Flood caused cardiac arrest in the Financial District as the irregular rhythm of the tide flooded the neighborhood and stopped all activity. Its hearts have stopped beating; the economic arteries no longer carry the lifeblood of capitalism. All that is left is a husk of a district, silent, serene even, in the absence of human activity and reclaimed by forces of nature. 
The paper boat ends its journey as the sun dips below the horizon, shrouding the sunken city in darkness. It floats over the Ferry Plaza where vendors once convened for farmers’ markets on Saturday mornings. The clock tower on the Ferry Building, once a symbol beheld by seabound travelers leaving the Port of San Francisco, is frozen at 11:49. The scent of lilies carries across the dark waters of San Francisco Bay as the boat travels beyond what used to be the city’s coastline.
The date is November 26th, 2187. Half a century since the hearts of San Francisco stopped beating as they once did.[4]






Word Count: 942 words

This website introduces the origins of the many heart-shaped sculptures in San Francisco, which play a central role in the storytelling in this essay. The website also includes a map of the locations of the sculptures in the city. 
________________




Appendix

This paragraph is an especially strong application of this HC as it clearly provides an image of people grieving lost ones, where my intent was to illustrate how survivors of the flood and their children have different sentiments about the flooding and impart a sense of empathy in readers.
[2] #organizatoin: I used the paper boat floating through the city as a literary device to create an organizational flow describing several of the city’s districts in geographical order, which allowed for seamless transition between scenes. I also used the same structure in my account of each district: I first provide a description of the dystopian flooded neighborhood, followed by an account of how residents of the district reacted to the flooding, and finish with the current state of the district metaphorically explained through symbolism that draws on the heart-shaped sculptures in the city. The repetitive structure highlights the contrast of the state of each district.
[3] #connotation: I employ language associated with bodies of water throughout the essay to align the prose with the image of a submerged city. This is an especially strong application as the metaphor “jump ship,” meaning to desert a cause or group, originated from the act of escaping from a ship on water and serves as a pun on how tenants left the city as soon as the flooding occurred. 
[4] #professionalism: I used appropriate formatting in APA 7th edition and carefully proofread my final draft before submission. I also received feedback on both my outline and final draft from peers and revised iteratively over the course of several days, starting during Friendsgiving break.

name: Thesis-driven Essay [final]
text_raw:
﻿Thesis-driven Essay




Minerva University
MC50: Multimodal Communications
Prof. D. Grant
November 1, 2023












________________


Thesis-driven Essay
        Given its geographically isolated nature and history of limiting trade and communications with other countries, one may assume that Japan has a rich, unique identity cultivated in its isolation. Japan does, indeed, have a unique culture rooted in tradition that is widely, albeit unconsciously, appreciated by its nationals. However, the supposed uniqueness is not an inherent quality. Counterintuitive as it may seem, the Japanese national identity is, and has always been, derived from a contrast between the Japanese and a distinct “other.”[1]
The “Others” in Japanese National Identity: A Historical Overview[2]
        The “othering” of specific groups has been a crucial element in the formation of Japanese national identity. This process has recurred with different groups both within and outside of the country over the course of history, rooting the notion of a distinct “outgroup” in contemporary Japan. The earliest attempts to instill a sense of shared national identity in the Japanese population date back to 1868, when the newly founded Meiji state thrived to unify the people under imperial rule (Weiner, 2009, p. 1). This identity was founded on the idea of tanitsu-minzoku, a term denoting the culturally and ethnically homogenous people of Japan.
The “Other” Beyond the Borders
        There are three main actors that served as “others” in the context of Japanese history: the United States, China, and the Soviet Union. These states have played a crucial role in shaping Japanese national identity after the Meiji restoration.
        Japan’s perception of China as an enormous economic and military presence shattered after the First Opium War (Cai, 2020). China, whose loss was attributed to its failure to modernize, became an example not to follow. Thus, Japan embraced the modernization brought about by the Meiji Restoration, carrying out governmental and societal reforms based on Western models. Rapid Westernization also gave rise to a superiority complex against China and other East Asian nations, which now “represented the ‘oriental retrogression’ that Japan desperately hoped to develop away from” (Cai, 2020, p. 14). This is evident in the notion of datsua-nyūou, a movement for Japan to distance itself from Asian values in favor of Western ideals. As Japan’s status as a modern state became established, it began to assume a leadership role in Asia as seen in its foreign policy to create the daitoa-kyoeiken, or Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere.
        After World War II and the subsequent occupation by the United States, Japan emerged as a liberal democracy. Pro-communist publications and public figures were suppressed, creating a sense of political “otherness” in communist and imperialist countries (Bokh, 2009). This “otherness” was leveraged to highlight the contrast between pre- and post-war Japan, emphasizing the superiority of the new democratic regime through comparison with the communist Soviet Union and China.
        During the Cold War era, Japanese identity had two competing narratives put forth by the conservative and progressive parties, centered around the country’s relationship with the United States (Bokh, 2009; Cai, 2020). The progressive narrative emphasized the preservation of peace and democracy through the adoption of a neutral stance in the Cold War rivalry, advocating for the abolition of the military alliance with the United States. Although it drew on the notion of American imperialism and framed Japan as still occupied, the progressive discourse did not necessitate the adoption of communist ideals or an alliance with the Soviet Union (Bokh, 2009). Rather, it called for independence, much like that of China who had determined their policies free of foreign influence (Bokh, 2009; Cai, 2020). 
In opposition to this, the conservative discourse argued that the US-Japan alliance was vital not only in ensuring security amidst the Cold War rivalry, but also in preserving Japan’s post-war ideals, such as peace and freedom (Cai, 2020). Thus, the communist Soviet Union and China were regarded as opposing threats to Japanese democracy.
As these competing narratives sought to define Japan’s post-war identity, the country saw an emerging trend in literature: nihonjinron, or “theories on the Japanese.” Arguing the cultural uniqueness, and often superiority of Japan, Nihonjinron emerged as a counter-discourse to Western culture, drawing on comparisons with other countries to further its arguments (Sugimoto, 1999; Hambleton, 2011). It typically equates nationality, ethnicity, and culture in defining what constitutes “Japaneseness.”[3]
Towards the end of the Cold War, Japan’s economic bubble burst, throwing the country into a prolonged period of economic stagnation. This challenged Japan’s identity as an economic success and a leading figure in Asia, especially after China replaced Japan as the world’s second-largest economy (Cai, 2009). In addition, China adopted a harsh stance on bilateral disputes, leading to its perception as an intimidating force that threatens Japan’s position in the international community. In response to this threat, Japan began to view itself as superior on the basis of a moral high ground over China, whose bilateral attitudes were thought of as unreasonable and overbearing. 
The “Other” Within the Borders
        Although pre-war Japan frequently called on the idea of a “family state” to harbor solidarity, interior exclusion was present in multiple parts of society (Weiner, 2009). Internal exclusion affected the urban poor, the ill, and those at the bottom of the caste system, known as eta and hinin (Weiner, 2009). The urban poor were regarded with contempt and excluded from society on the basis of their material conditions and perceived moral deficiencies. People afflicted with leprosy were also faced with forced isolation and compulsory birth control in the form of abortion and sterilization. The eta and hinin were segregated based on the feudal caste system even after it was abolished with the Meiji Restoration, and their descendants still face discrimination today.
The “Other” in Extended Borders
        “Othering” also took place in the colonial order. For the indigenous people of Hokkaido, the Ainu, this manifested as racism and forced integration into the Japanese population (Weiner, 2009). They had maintained independence at the beginning of the rule of the Tokugawa Shogunate, with whom they established trade. The feudal lord reigning over the area then gradually began to exploit them by monopolizing trade and setting disadvantageous rates on their products. The Meiji Restoration marked their colonization by Japan through the introduction of Japanese as the lingua franca, biological assimilation through marriage, legal registration as Japanese citizens, and administration by the mainland Meiji government. The colonization was justified by framing the Ainu as a primitive, savage  “other” that would benefit from the introduction of civilization.
Similar to Hokkaido, Okinawa was also once a foreign territory. It belonged to the kingdom known as Ryūkyū Ōkoku, which lost its sovereignty when a feudal lord invaded the nation in 1609 to exploit it as a trading port (Weiner, 2009)[4]. The island chain was formally declared a Japanese prefecture by the Meiji government at the time of the Restoration, but in nature was still similar to a colonial possession: Japanese education and military were imposed, Japanese was appointed as the official language, and the government incentivized improved agricultural production through low-cost loans.
The Continued Portrayal of an “Outgroup”[5]
        Although contemporary Japan is often perceived as homogenous, the number of immigrants has shown a steady rise (Hambleton, 2011). With this, the number of media appearances made by non-Japanese residents has also increased. Television programs featuring non-Japanese panelists discussing their experiences in Japan as foreigners now constitute a genre of their own. These programs use strategies such as introducing panelists as representatives of their home countries, asking them to use English even if they are proficient in Japanese, and having them feign ignorance of Japanese culture, to further highlight the contrast between them and the Japanese. This process of “othering” acts to reaffirm the cultural uniqueness through which Japanese national identity is defined. Although the motives behind employing such elements that strengthen the national identities of Japanese viewers are unclear, the programs emphasize the uniqueness of Japanese culture and “depict foreign panelists in such a way that they become a spectacle, rather than a threat to the dominant ideology of a homogeneous Japan” (Hambleton, 2011, p. 43).
        To conclude, this essay argued that Japanese national identity does not exist inherently, but rather through the existence of an “other.” This was demonstrated through the synthesis of several high-quality sources that focused on the otherization of Japan’s neighboring countries and internal minorities.[6]
        




Word Count: 1369 words
Statement on AI use: 
I used Grammarly.com to proofread my final draft.




________________


References
Bukh, A. (2010). Japan’s National Identity and Foreign Policy. https://doi.org/10.4324/9780203883754 
This source explains the role of Russia, previously the Soviet Union, in shaping Japanese national identity as a contrasted “other.” Post-war Japan had conflicting perceptions of the Soviet Union as a threatening opposite or at least a drastically different social system, which was leveraged to highlight the superiority of Japan’s post-war democracy. Japan reassessed its image of post-Soviet Russia with its denouncement of communism after the Cold War, leading it to take a more cooperative stance and thus securing its identity as a mature capitalist democracy. 
Cai, P. (1970, January 1). Mutual othering and Sino-Japanese nationalism: Perceptions, themes and implications on bilateral relations. Recherche uO Research: Mutual Othering and Sino-Japanese Nationalism: Perceptions, Themes and Implications on Bilateral Relations. https://ruor.uottawa.ca/handle/10393/40489 
This source evaluates the role Japan and China played in the formation of each other’s national identity. China was a huge economic and military presence before the First Opium War, after which Japan viewed it as a “lesson learned” about embracing modernization. China’s later adoption of communist ideals evoked mixed opinions in Japan, where some regarded their independent foreign policy with respect in contrast with US-Japan relations, and others frowned upon the communist regime. China’s emergence as the second-largest economy in the world and its harsh stance on bilateral disputes led to Japan perceiving it as a threat.
Hambleton, A. (2011). Reinforcing identities? Non-Japanese residents, television and cultural nationalism in Japan. Contemporary Japan, 23(1), 27–47. https://doi.org/10.1515/cj.2011.003 
This source discusses the findings of a study conducted on Japanese television programs featuring non-Japanese panelists. It concludes that such programs deliberately employ strategies to highlight the “otherness” of the panelists, thus strengthening the national identities of viewers. The programs are thought to play a role in cultural nationalism, reaffirming notions of Japan’s cultural uniqueness.
Sugimoto, Y. (1999). Making sense of Nihonjinron. Thesis Eleven, 57(1), 81–96. https://doi.org/10.1177/0725513699057000007 
This paper by Sugimoto discusses Nihonjinron, a genre of Japanese literature centered around the uniqueness of the Japanese people and culture. It analyzes the claims in Nihonjinron literature, their socio-political implications, and how they contribute to the development of Japanese identity. The paper provides an in-depth examination of Japanese nationalism and cultural discourse and valuable insights into the construction of national identity in Japan.
Weiner, M. (2009). Japan’s minorities: The illusion of homogeneity. Routledge. 
Michael Weiner challenges the perception of Japan being a homogeneous society. Weiner highlights the diversity and complex issues faced by its minorities. Weiner's work provides an extensive analysis of minority groups in Japan, including the Ainu, Okinawans, and resident Koreans, examining their historical, social, and political stories. The book was a critical resource for understanding the multicultural aspects of Japanese society and the challenges of minority integration in writing this essay.[7]

[2] #professionalism: The essay is written and formatted according to standard APA guidelines, which includes the proper formatting of headings and subheadings, the indentation of the references, and the use of in-text citations (including correctly formatted multi-paper in-text citations).

﻿Complex Systems Analysis Case Study




Minerva University
CX50: Complex Systems
Prof. L. Odera
October 21, 2023












________________


Complex Systems Analysis Case Study
The current democratic regime in Japan is suffering from declining civilian interest in politics with a voter turnout rate of 55%, an alarmingly low rate for a functioning democracy (Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications [MIC], 2022). This problem is especially prevalent in voters aged 18 to 29, who display a voter turnout rate below 40%. This case study analyzes the explanatory challenge of political apathy prevalent in younger Japanese voters and its origins.
Historically speaking, democracy in Japan is relatively new. The closest Japan had come to democracy was the Taishō Democracy movement, which took place roughly after the Russo-Japan War in 1905. Although this caused a regime shift towards a democratic government, the parliament was founded on the idea of “Minpon Shugi,” a form of democracy with monarchical sovereignty, as the Meiji Constitution explicitly stated that sovereignty belonged to the emperor. This incomplete democracy dissolved into imperialistic rule in 1940, when the numerous political parties in parliament were reformed into the monolithic “Taisei Yokusankai,” also known as the Imperial Aid Association. This statist ruling party went on to adopt a nomination commission, barring non-sanctioned candidates from running in elections and furthering the totalitarian trend. The party quashed opposition, becoming the backbone for operations in World War II. 
The current regime came into being after the signing of the Potsdam Declaration, which stated that the U.S. General Headquarters (GHQ) would occupy Japan until democratic systems were “recovered” in 1945. The new Constitution of Japan, now entailing democratic ideals and the sovereignty of the people, was drafted by the GHQ at this time and came into effect in 1947. In sum, democracy was never something inherently present in Japan but rather a system imposed on the country during its brief occupation by the United States.
Although the Imperial Aid Association dissolved in 1945, allowing voters to take any political stance of their choosing, there remains minimal diversity in political standing among Japanese voters. The majority of voters, at 40%, are independents with no particular party they support (Japan Broadcasting Corporation [NHK], 2023). This group can be further decomposed into independents who are interested in politics and vote based on proposed policies rather than parties and politically apathetic independents who do not have political interests or opinions (Tanaka, 1992). It has also been found that independents tend to be younger. Around 35% support the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP), one of the current ruling parties (NHK, 2023). The remaining 25% is distributed among nine parties, none of which with more than 10% of voters supporting them.[1] Although the large number of independents should make the election results unpredictable, the LDP has been the ruling majority for most of post-WWII political history. This stability is due to independent votes being fractured among parties and the low voter turnout rates among independents (NHK, 2021; NHK, 2022). The LDP’s large base of support makes it difficult for any other party to outnumber them in parliament when the independents vote for different parties. In addition, independents are less likely to vote, making LDP supporters a majority.[2]
Although the majority of voters are independents, there are some trends among the votes actually cast. The center-right LDP and Democratic Party for the People (DPP) are especially popular among the younger generation aged 18 to 29 (NHK, 2021; NHK, 2022). The right-wing Japan Innovation Party (JIP) has its largest base of support in working-age voters between 30 and 49. The centralist New Kōmei Party (NKP) receives votes from all age groups. The center-left Constitutional Democratic Party (CDP) and far-left Japanese Communist Party (JCP) both gain support from voters aged 50 and above. 
In addition to analyzing the differences in political leanings among different age groups, an analysis of media influence on the electorate’s political opinions is crucial in exploring the explanatory challenge. Figure 1 illustrates the network relations between three levels of nodes: the major political parties, media, and electorate. The edges represent influence from the source of the edge to the destination. The multi-layered network illustrates the vital role media plays in informing the electorate’s opinion, where the entire media plane can be considered a single point of failure.[3]  














Figure 1
Network diagram of political parties, media, and electorate
  

As illustrated in Figure 1, political parties influence parts of the electorate by advocating for policies that target their respective support bases, mainly through the media. They influence television stations and newspapers through the Japan National Press Club, an association of members of the press that regularly sets up press rooms for reporters to gain insight directly from authorities (Japan National Press Club, n.d.). The strong ties between reporters and politicians created by this environment limit political coverage in the media to mainstream party opinions (Freeman, 2014). In addition, all major television stations are owned by newspapers, therefore causing the two mediums to influence each other to maintain consistency in press releases. Finally, the political views of voters are heavily influenced by different forms of media, with television being the most potent form of influence, followed by online news outlets and newspapers (Miyata et al., 2014). While most people aged 30 and above consider newspapers as their primary source of news, people under 30 tend to use online news outlets instead (MIC, 2021). All age groups reported to watch news on television.[4] 
Figure 2
Causal diagram of political apathy
  

Given that media plays a huge part in the electorate’s formulation of political beliefs, the prevalent political disinterest in Japanese voters under 30 may be in part attributed to widespread media apathy. The decline in newspaper readership of Japanese youth had previously been attributed to online sources replacing traditional news outlets, but it has been found that 28% of people aged 20 to 29 do not consume any form of news at all (Hashimoto, 2014). Since political interest, media consumption, and political knowledge form a reinforcing loop where voters who are interested in politics consume news that furthers their knowledge, and subsequently their interest, this apathy leads to political disinterest (Miyata et al., 2014). This, combined with an inherent lack of education about democracy due to the ideal being “borrowed” to begin with, makes it difficult for voters to stay informed about politics and to form opinions about it. In addition, low interest in politics causes lower voter turnout rates, incentivizing parties to favor voters who cast more votes, such as the elderly, in their policies. This leads to younger voters losing trust in the government to serve their best interests, further reinforcing their political apathy.[5]
















Figure 3
Phase space diagram of Japanese democracy
  

Figure 4
Phase space diagram of Japanese democracy with depth index 
  

Note. The incline of slopes represents difficulty in moving across the phase space.
Figure 3 illustrates a phase space for the current Japanese regime. Figure 4 is a 3-dimensional version of the same diagram representing the depth of the basins of attraction with grooves representing possible pathways to regime shifts. The horizontal axis is the level of trust voters place in the government, measured by the proportion of the population that trusts the government as measured by the OECD Trust Survey (Nguyen et al., 2022). The vertical axis is the level of electorate participation in democracy, measured by the Participatory Democracy Index (Coppedge et al., 2023). The two basins of attraction A and B represent the current Japanese regime and an ideal liberal democracy. As illustrated in Figure 4, basin A is deep and requires heightened public trust to escape. This can be achieved by political parties following through on their campaign pledges and setting forth policies that improve the economy, which will bring the system to critical point D. Higher trust in the government will lead to higher political participation via voting, which will, in turn, incentivize parties to further consider the interests of the voters, creating a reinforcing loop that pushes the system to basin B. However, this state is prone to democratic backslide, as small missteps on the government’s end, such as a scandal, are sufficient for the public to harbor a sense of distrust in the government, pushing the system past critical point C and back into basin A. This is illustrated in the shallowness of basin B in Figure 4.[6] 
        The decomposition of the current Japanese democratic system based on political parties and age groups in the electorate served to assist in the exportation of the explanatory challenge. From analyses drawing on historical context, networks, causal diagrams, and phase spaces, it can be concluded that the widespread political apathy in Japanese youth is by large due to media apathy, a lack of opportunities to learn about democratic ideals and systems, and distrust in the government that stems from feelings of being underrepresented in policies. 


Word Count: 1456 words


________________


References

Retrieved October 29, 2023, from https://www.soumu.go.jp/menu_news/s-news/01iicp01_02000111.html.

________________
[1] #systemmapping: The Japanese electorate is decomposed based on political standing. The decomposition illuminates the lack of set opinions in the majority of voters and identifies a group to analyze further to explore the explanatory challenge.
[2] #emergentproperties: The LDP’s long-standing position as a ruling party despite the presence of a large number of independents is explained by analyzing individual voter behavior in independents and the effect this has on the system as a whole.
[3] #networks: The influence parties have over opinions in the electorate is illustrated with a network diagram. The centrality of media to the formation of opinions in the electorate is explained by the single point of failure in the network.
[4]  #levelsofanalysis: Three levels of analysis, political parties, media, and the electorate, are identified. Interactions between the levels are analysed to understand the formulation of political beliefs in the electorate.
[5] complexcausality: Causes of political apathy in Japanese youth are identified and analyzed based on their interactions. Reinforcing loops that contribute to widespread political apathy are also identified. 
[6] #systemdynamics: A phase space with measurable and relevant axes is created and basins, attractors, and critical points are identified and justified. Potential pathways for regime shifts are also explained with a reinforcing loop.

name: LBA: Social Change in Practice [final]
text_raw:
﻿LBA: Social Change in Practice




Minerva University
CX50: Multimodal Communications
Prof. C. Odera
December 7, 2023












________________


LBA: Social Change in Practice
Introduction
826 Valencia is a nonprofit organization supporting under-resourced students with their writing skills (826 Valencia, n.d.-a). Founded in 2002, they have expanded from their first Writing Center in 826 Valencia, San Francisco to include two more centers in underprivileged neighborhoods in the city and serve thousands of students every year. This paper analyzes their pursuit of social change through an evaluation of their operations from a complex systems perspective.
Evaluation of Purpose and Pursued Social Change
        826 Valencia strives to address the problem of writing being severely undertaught in public schools and Black and Lantine students being less proficient in English compared to their white peers by offering support to underserved students (826 Valencia, 2023). This problem is shaped by social norms surrounding the instruction of writing, where it is ascribed lower priority in the testing-based classroom culture established with the passing of the No Child Left Behind Act (NCLB) in 2002 (Campodonico, 2022; California Department of Education, 2022; Moses & Nanna, 2007, p. 63). The NCLB mandated schools to administer standardized tests to ensure that all students are making sufficient academic progress as described in the curriculum. Although this emphasis on standardized testing was somewhat mitigated when the NCLB was replaced by the Every Student Succeeds Act (ESSA) signed into power in 2015, the testing-centered culture persisted (California Department of Education, 2023).[1]
To this end, they are “dedicated to supporting under-resourced students ages six to eighteen with their creative and expository writing skills and to helping teachers inspire their students to write” through “services [that] are structured around the understanding that great leaps in learning can happen with individualized attention and that strong writing skills are fundamental to future success (826 Valencia, n.d.-a, About Us section, para. 1).” Underlying this mission is the core belief that writing fosters critical thinking and creativity, is a vital skill in academic and professional settings, builds community, and empowers young people to express themselves (826 Valencia, 2023; Campodonico, 2022). They also acknowledge the importance of one-on-one attention from adults in learning, particularly for students learning English as a second language. 
These values directly translate into their self-proclaimed “audacious” 20-year vision to equip all under-resourced students in San Francisco with the writing skills and access to publishing platforms necessary for them to make positive changes in society (826 Valencia, 2022). Likewise, these core values inform their goals for 2026: reaching more elementary school students, increasing tutors to provide individualized attention, connecting more students to publishing and leadership programs, expanding the audience for student publication, reaching more teachers through professional development programs, and raising their budget to expand their services. 
These values are also reflected in 826 Valencia’s operations, such as hosting workshops and programs for creative and academic writing for over 6,000 students in 2022-2023 and achieving a 2.3:1 student-to-tutor ratio in their workshops and programs (826 Valencia, 2023). These operations have contributed to improved writing proficiency in students and transformed relationships to writing, with many students newly finding joy in writing as confirmed through conversations with 826 Valencia Students (Personal Communication, October 6, 2023). Furthermore, their Writing Centers have contributed to developing a sense of community in the neighborhoods they serve (Dineen, 2019).[2]
Analysis of Operations
Analysis of Direct Interactions with Students
        To undo the lasting effects of the NCLB in the devaluation of writing, 826 Valencia offers opportunities for students to freely explore writing outside of the classroom and for teachers to engage their students in creative writing (826 Valencia, n.d.-a). In doing so, they are drawing on the ideological power they have as educators by contesting standardized testing culture. They are also introducing a new value that emphasizes the importance of writing as an agency and thus allows for more opportunities for its instruction to both students and teachers.[3] 
        826 Valencia also challenges the culture of silence imposed on marginalized youth “in a world that systemically devalues the voices and stories of young people of color (826 Valencia, n.d.-a, Why Writing? Section, para. 3).” They seek to do this by elevating the writing skills of students from marginalized groups to provide them with the tools and confidence for self-expression through individualized tutoring in programs designed to encourage free expression. This contrarian approach of unteaching norms and empowering students to express themselves is made possible by the authority, expertise, and good intentions that students see in the tutors, whom they perceive as teachers who challenge them to become better writers and value their ideas (826 Valencia, 2023). This allows the tutors’ instruction to have a substantial effect on students’ learning, which in turn increases their confidence and pride to effectively contest the norm of staying silent.
        In order to better facilitate learning, tutors are instructed to encourage students to be imaginative and make them feel supported throughout the writing process. This can be achieved by sharing students’ excitement about their ideas and the pride they take in progress, as discussed in a conversation with 826 Valencia staff J. Totterdell at a volunteer session (Personal Communication, October 6, 2023). Experiencing individualized support while maintaining autonomy, in the case of 826 Valencia Students making creative and stylistic decisions, encourages deep engagement with content and leads to higher satisfaction in their learning (Klem & Connell, 2004, p. 262). Furthermore, there is evidence suggesting that students who perceive higher levels of support from their teachers outperform their peers who do not have the same good relationships with their instructors (Elias & Haynes, 2008, pp.477-478). Volunteers are also asked to pay close attention to students’ emotions and adjust their instruction accordingly, such as by engaging students in dialogue for brainstorming when they are feeling frustrated about an idea they cannot quite articulate or giving them space to work individually when they want to concentrate.[4] 
Structural Analysis of Network Relations
        As illustrated in Figure 1, 826 Valencia supports the San Francisco education scene through a wide range of services. Nodes in the network diagram represent schools and students while edges represent the flow of knowledge imparted through instruction and opportunities. 826 Valencia offers students direct instruction in their workshops, after-school programs, and summer camps (826 Valencia, n.d.-b; 826 Valencia, n.d.-f; 826 Valencia, n.d.-g). They also support local schools with in-school programs that work with small groups of students to produce publications and provide extra assistance with schoolwork, and field trips where teachers bring their classes to a Writing Center for day-long writing programs (826 Valencia, n.d.-c; 826 Valencia, n.d.-d). Furthermore, they offer older students programs that prepare and support them in higher education and their careers, such as college application writing support, college scholarships, leadership development programs, and internships (826 Valencia, n.d.-e).




















Figure 1
Network diagram of 826 Valencia, schools, and Students  
        826 Valencia acts as a single point of failure that significantly decreases the robustness of the network of the local education scene, which represents their importance to students’ learning. The network is also highly centralized, with 826 Valencia having a high out-degree centrality and multiple paths to some agents. This represents the variety of opportunities that go beyond the scope of schooling that they offer to their students, both directly and through local schools. Their high connectedness allows them to reach more students, and the short average path length allows them to regulate the flow of learning to maximize the improvement in students’ writing skills with their control over teaching methods, which they leverage to ensure a low student-to-tutor ratio for individualized attention. Overall, they act as an intermediate agent that fills in the gaps in learning, especially in writing, that school alone cannot provide. This role as a mediator and supplementary source of learning with decisional power over instruction methods and the capability to reach many students closely aligns with their goal of supporting under-resourced students in their writing and their emphasis on one-on-one attention to facilitate learning.[5]
Conclusion
        This analysis served to illuminate the pivotal role 826 Valencia plays in facilitating growth in students’ writing skills through an evaluation of their operations in relation to their goals and values and in the broader context of the San Francisco education scene. It can be concluded from the analysis that while the pursuit of social change by 826 Valencia may be ambitious, they are making steady progress thus far and are taking a promising and effective approach.






Documentation of Engagement with Organization: I volunteered as a tutor for a third-grade class field trip to 826 Valencia as a part of a staff-led experience. I also deeply engaged with their website to gain insight into their purpose and operations, and read articles about them from local news outlets such as the San Francisco Chronicle and San Francisco Standard. 
Word Count: 1296 words
Statement on AI use: I used Grammarly.com to proofread the final draft of this assignment.

The adverse outcomes of conforming action (writing is undertaught and non-white students are especially struggling) are assessed and the contrarian operations of 826 Valencia are evaluated for their practicality.
[2] #purpose: The purpose, goals, and operations of 826 Valencia are evaluated for their consistency with their core values identified through exploration of their website and an interview account with the founders. The consistency between their values and goals and their operations is demonstrated through numerical figures that illustrate how the operations produce results that directly advance them.
[3] #powerdynamics: The source of power that 826 Valencia leverages to influence the extent to which students and teachers prioritize writing is identified as the third dimension of ideological power. The analysis also explains how 826 Valencia is attempting to address the widespread devaluation of writing by establishing a new value system.
[4] #emotionaliq: The role of emotional intelligence in tutoring is assessed using two branches of the four-brach model proposed by Salovey and Grewal, perceiving (being attentive to students’ emotions and adjusting instruction accordingly) and managing emotions (being supportive and engaging to make students feel supported). The application of these skills is justified by scholarly evidence describing a causal relationship between students’ sense of being supported and learning. 
[5] #networks: The network structure of the San Francisco education scene is visualized and described in terms of topology, robustness, path length, and connectedness. The real-world implications of these properties of the network are used to analyze 826 Valencia’s social impact and their success in advancing their goals.

﻿Scientific Proposal Part 1




Minerva University
EA50: Empirical Analysis
Prof. C. Sealfon
November 11, 2023












________________


Scientific Proposal Part 1
        With rising sea levels, San Francisco faces a heightened risk of coastal flooding (SLR Coordinating Committee, 2023). This paper analyzes the demographic patterns of vulnerable populations and proposes hypotheses to explain them. 
        Fig. 1 and Table 1 are based on a 2016 assessment of the flood vulnerability of San Francisco census blocks by Wolff and Comerford. It measured four categories of flood vulnerability of census block residents: demographic and socioeconomic factors, exposure to harsh weather, health complications, and housing situations. It then aggregated these into a flood health index measuring the block’s vulnerability to flooding. 
As seen in Fig. 1 and Table 1, a strong negative correlation exists between the proportion of individuals over 25 with at least a high school degree and the proportion of residents that do not identify as white (not Hispanic or Latinx) per block. Furthermore, the mean for each variable in different flood health index quintiles indicates that blocks in the fifth quintile are especially likely to have a high proportion of non-white residents and a low proportion of adults with a high school degree.[1]  
Figure 1. Proportion of individuals over 25 with at least a high school degree vs. proportion of residents that do not identify as white (not Hispanic or Latinx) per census block with color encoding by flood health index quintile, based on San Francisco Department of Public Health, 2016. Higher flood health index indicates higher flood vulnerability. The vertical axis is truncated to enhance visibility of points. There is a strong correlation between the two variables.


  

Table 1. Mean, median, and Pearson’s correlation coefficient for proportion of individuals over 25 with at least a high school degree and proportion of residents that do not identify as white (not Hispanic or Latinx) per census block for each flood health index quintile. Blocks in the fifth quintile have an especially high proportion of non-white residents and a low proportion of adults with at least a high school degree compared to other quintiles.


        Because the flood health index aggregates multiple risk indicators, this trend suggests these communities are under heightened risk due to compounding harms beyond demographics and education. This prompts the question: Why might communities with a high proportion of non-white residents and a low proportion of adults with at least a high school degree be especially vulnerable to flooding? 
This question can shed light on the effect of structural racism on factors of flood vulnerability, such as housing and employment, established by Pager and Shepherd (2008). Furthermore, lower education levels in affected populations are associated with lower capability to cope after a natural disaster (Frankenberg et al., 2013). Addressing these problems in vulnerable populations can mitigate the effects of flooding in urban areas, including infrastructure destruction, displacement, and health complications, such as anxiety and injury (Heberger et al., 2011; Hajat et al., 2005).[2]
The pattern can be explained by two hypotheses: non-white residents without high school education have limited knowledge to cope with a flood and are less likely to speak English, making it difficult for them to seek the help they need (H1), or they struggle to obtain high-paying employment, making them likely to acquire housing less resilient to flooding (H2). 
H1 predicts a positive correlation between the proportion of non-white residents without a high school degree and the proportion of non-English-speaking residents in a community and a significant difference in the quality of life after a flood between them and other residents. Although the predicted correlation can be tested with current census data, the effects of a flood cannot be tested immediately. H2 predicts a significant difference in average income between non-white adults without a high school degree and others and a positive correlation between income and housing flood resistance. This can be tested with census data and surveys to make up for unmeasured variables. Thus, this paper proposes the readily testable H2 as a working hypothesis.[3]






Word Count:  498 words


________________


References
Frankenberg, E., Sikoki, B., Sumantri, C., Suriastini, W., & Thomas, D. (2013). Education, vulnerability, and resilience after a natural disaster. Ecology and Society, 18(2). https://doi.org/10.5751/es-05377-180216 
Hajat, S., Ebi, K. L., Kovats, R. S., Menne, B., Edwards, S., & Haines, A. (2005). The human health consequences of flooding in Europe: A Review. Extreme Weather Events and Public Health Responses, 185–196. https://doi.org/10.1007/3-540-28862-7_18 
Heberger, M., Cooley, H., Herrera, P., Gleick, P. H., & Moore, E. (2011). Potential impacts of increased coastal flooding in California due to sea-level rise. Climatic Change, 109(S1), 229–249. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-011-0308-1 
Pager, D., & Shepherd, H. (2008). The sociology of discrimination: Racial discrimination in employment, housing, credit, and Consumer Markets. Annual Review of Sociology, 34(1), 181–209. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev.soc.33.040406.131740 
Wolff, M., & Comerford, C., sf.gov (2016). Climate and Health Understanding the Risk: An Assessment of San Francisco’s Vulnerability to Flooding and Extreme Storms. San Francisco Department of Public Health. Retrieved November 11, 2023, from https://sf.gov/sites/default/files/2023-05/FloodVulnerabilityReport_v5.pdf.pdf. 
SLR Coordinating Committee, Varat, A., Hsiao, A., Wenger, M., May, K., & Askeland, K., sfplanning.org (2020). Retrieved November 11, 2023, from https://sfplanning.org/sea-level-rise-action-plan.

The scatterplot uses a colorblind-friendly palette with highly contrasted colors. The distribution and descriptive statistics are used to interpret trends in the data to identify a research question.
[2] #evidencebased: Four lines of evidence were used to justify the benefits of addressing the research question. Evidence specific to the United States and coastal flooding in California was employed. Other evidence was chosen based on the generalizability of the findings, as the effects of natural disasters on urban areas are similar across countries.
[3] #hypothesisdevelopment: Multiple hypotheses to account for the pattern identified from trends in the data were proposed. Predictions and methods of testing based on the hypotheses were also proposed to select a testable working hypothesis.

name: EA50 problem Solving Part 2 [final]
text_raw:
﻿Problem Solving Part 2




Minerva University
EA50: Empirical Analysis
Prof. C. Sealfon
October 7, 2023












________________


Problem Solving Part 2
As of now, the Tenderloin neighborhood is facing severe homelessness problems. According to the 2022 San Francisco Point-in-Time (PIT) Count, District 6, which at the time of the survey encompassed the entire Tenderloin among other neighborhoods, had 3,848 homeless residents (Applied Survey Research [ASR], 2022). In addition, District 6 had the largest homeless population per resident, at approximately 1 homeless resident per 28 housed residents in 2019 (ASR, 2022; The San Francisco Redistricting Task Force, 2022). If we were to solve this problem, an attainable goal would be the homeless-to-housed ratio of District 10, which had the second largest homeless population in San Francisco in 2019 with approximately 1 homeless resident per 46 housed residents.[1] 
Figure 1
Line graph of homeless population per district resident by San Francisco's districts  
This problem primarily concerns the neighborhood of Tenderloin, but solving it will also require the city government’s effort. However, the community's distrust toward government interventions may pose an obstacle to achieving this goal, with San Francisco residents rating the “overall job of local government in providing services” as a C on a scale of A (Excellent) to F (Failing) (City and County of San Francisco Controller’s Office, 2023).[2]
The larger homelessness problem in the Tenderloin has two sides: problems in the homeless population and those in the surrounding community. Homeless residents face difficulty receiving adequate health care, housing, and poverty. Inadequate healthcare can be attributed to health complications and insurance issues. Health complications can be categorized into physical and mental conditions.
Figure 2
Fishbone diagram of deconstruction of the problem “Homelessness in Tenderloin”
  

Addressing the mental health crisis in the homeless population is crucial in solving Tenderloin’s homeless problem, as these conditions make exiting homelessness difficult. Individuals suffering from mental health issues often exhibit behavioral and cognitive symptoms that hinder them from vital aspects of obtaining stable housing, such as earning stable incomes and getting help. For example, substance abuse is a common problem in the homeless population. Alcohol abuse increases the risk of unemployment and decreases opportunities for finding employment or being rehired (Jørgensen et al., 2019). The abuse of other substances, such as tobacco and cannabis, is also associated with job loss (Airagnes et al., 2019). Mental health issues also often disrupt people’s ability to form secure relationships with potential sources of assistance, such as caregivers, family, and friends, making it difficult for them to seek help when experiencing homelessness. For example, schizophrenia, Alzheimer’s disease, and major depressive disorder cause social dysfunction, which often manifests as social withdrawal (Porcelli et al., 2019).[3]
According to the PIT Count conducted in January 2022, many homeless residents face mental health issues, with 52% of those surveyed reporting substance abuse, 38% reporting symptoms of PTSD, and 36% reporting psychiatric or emotional conditions (ASR, 2022). In addition, the majority of the homeless population has little to no access to mental healthcare, with only 15% having access to Medi-Cal, Medicare, or Covered California, and 12% utilizing community-based health services. A goal state in addressing this issue would be to ensure that the homeless residents of Tenderloin have adequate access to mental and behavioral health services. One measure of success would be the responses to the PIT Count questions regarding the usage of government assistance and community-based services. The percentage of homeless residents currently accessing government-supported health insurance and community-based health services were both around 20% in 2017. Therefore, an appropriate initial goal would be to return these statistics to their former levels. This problem concerns the entire Tenderloin community regardless of housing circumstances. However, solving it may be difficult as 56% of the city’s homeless residents stated “Don’t want government assistance” as their reason for not receiving government assistance. 
The San Francisco Community Clinic Consortium (SFCCC) offers a promising solution to the mental health crisis in the homeless population: the Street Outreach Services (SOS) program (San Francisco Community Clinic Consortium [SFCCC], 2018). SOS is a van-based mobile clinic for homeless San Franciscans that provides urgent care and links them to partner clinics to address ongoing health issues. They also connect them to other resources such as legal support and assistance with housing and benefits applications through partnerships with other non-profits and organizations, such as shelters, food banks, and the San Francisco Department of Public Health. Their team consists of doctors, nurses, and volunteers serving over 1,000 clients annually, around 13% of the entire homeless population in the city (ASR, 2022).
The success of the SOS programs can be attributed to their approach of “meeting them [the unhoused people] on their own turf” (SFCCC, 2018) to build trust and make medical care more accessible. For example, SOS operates in front of Martin de Porres House of Hospitality, a free lunch restaurant. Making medical care available near other high-priority resources offers clients a chance to address health issues they would have otherwise left unattended.[4]
To reach the previously identified goal of having 20% of homeless residents access health care services, the current solution offered by SFCCC needs to reach more people in need. Currently, staff ask patients what conditions they need treatment for and take notes on a clipboard to add them to a queue. They then receive treatment from available staff on a first-come-first-serve basis. This system could be revised to manage the line of clients waiting to receive treatment. Many staff were also rushing back and forth from the van to retrieve supplies. From observation of their operations, it can be concluded that the SOS team is understaffed. However, increasing staff as a non-profit offering free services is not feasible, especially when they need special training to work in the medical field. Lengthening their operating hours will also be difficult with their current numbers.[5]
Figure 3
I observed the SOS operations at Martin de Porres House of Hospitality. This allowed me to better ideate solutions to the identified subproblem.
  

While increasing staff is unrealistic, SOS operations would be more efficient if staff spent less time fetching supplies. To do this, they would have to keep supplies on hand, and as they cannot carry everything, they should set up stations. This system can be further refined if staff had specific roles and were assigned patients based on their conditions. Examples of these roles would be providing patients with medication and first-aid for minor conditions, referring them to long-term care facilities, connecting them with behavioral health services, and offering them non-medical resources such as legal support.[6] Besides saving time, this approach can enhance the SOS program’s efficiency in several ways. For staff tasked with connecting patients with external services, this specialization will familiarize them with the area. It will also alleviate the strain on doctors and nurses, as they can focus on treating patients needing professional medical care.






Word Count: 1,100 words


________________


References
Airagnes, G., Lemogne, C., Meneton, P., Plessz, M., Goldberg, M., Hoertel, N., Roquelaure, Y., Limosin, F., & Zins, M. (2019). Retrieved October 9, 2023, from https://hsh.sfgov.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/2022-PIT-Count-Report-San-Francisco-Updated-8.19.22.pdf.
Jørgensen, M. B., Pedersen, J., Thygesen, L. C., Lau, C. J., Christensen, A. I., Becker, U., & Tolstrup, J. S. (2019). Alcohol consumption and labour market participation: A prospective cohort study of transitions between work, unemployment, sickness absence, and social benefits. European Journal of Epidemiology, 34(4), 397–407. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10654-018-0476-7
Porcelli, S., Van Der Wee, N., van der Werff, S., Aghajani, M., Glennon, J. C., van Heukelum, S., Mogavero, F., Lobo, A., Olivera, F. J., Lobo, E., Posadas, M., Dukart, J., Kozak, R., Arce, E., Ikram, A., Vorstman, J., Bilderbeck, A., Saris, I., Kas, M. J., & Serretti, A. (2019). Statement on AI use:
I used Grammarly.com to proofread my final draft.


Reflection:        
I originally planned to apply the problem-solving HCs in the order of #rightproblem, #breakitdown, #rightproblem, #gapanalysis, #constraints, and #heuristics. Although I structured the paper in this order, the ideation and writing process was not this linear, as I iteratively returned to my #gapanalysis as I applied #heuristics to ideate a solution that retained the strengths of the existing solution and revisited #breakitdown as I applied #rightproblem. From this process, I learned that the framework offered by the problem-solving HCs is similar to a map with potential travel destinations within a city: they are things you can go to when you don’t know what to do, you do not necessarily have to visit all of them, especially if you are short on time, nor there is a set order between them, and you can come back to parts you feel like you haven’t seen enough of. That being said, the HCs are a robust tool in ideating effective solutions to problems and were a crucial part of my problem-solving process. Understanding the problem-solving process as non-linear and using the HCs, sometimes iteratively, to guide it helped me effectively ideate solutions without being at a loss as to my next steps. 




________________
[1] #evidencebased: I employed relevant evidence to support my argument that Tenderloin has a homelessness problem by comparing the population per one homeless resident in each district of San Francisco. These statistics are better suited to strengthen my point than the number of homeless residents in the district, as it takes into account the ratio of the homeless to housed populations. 
[2] #rightproblem: I characterized the initial state, goal state, scale, and obstacles for both the broader problem of homelessness in Tenderloin and the subproblem of mental health in the homeless population of Tenderloin. In both applications, I defined the initial state with quantifiable and specific figures, identified existing trends in the problems, and defined a tractable goal state based on the initial problems. I also analyzed the scale at which solutions sould be proposed, and potential obstacles to the solutions.
[3] #breakitdown: I broke the problem down with four iterations; where in the community the problem arises, major factors that contribute to quality of life, internal and external factors in healthcare problems, and how the complication affects the individual. I stopped the iteration when it had a means of assessing the solution’s degree of success. I also provided justification for the mental health crisis being a subproblem of homelessness.
[4] #gapanalysis: I assessed the strengths and weaknesses of existing solution to the problem to conclude that improvement to it would suffice. The suggested improvement retains the identified strengths and focuses on improving the weakness of not reaching enough people.
[5] #constraints: I identified two constraints, the number of staff and operating hours. These are justified by the nature of SFCCC, which is a non-profit with limited funding. They guided my proposed solution, which satisfies both.




[6] #heuristics: I applied two heuristics to ideate a solution to make the SOS operations more efficient: observation and working backwards. I noticed through observation that staff spent a lot of time retrieving supplies from the van and proposed a solution to minimize this time by working backwards.
