﻿Problem Solving Part 1




Minerva University
EA50: Empirical Analysis
Prof. C. Sealfon
September 19, 2023












________________


Problem Solving Part 1
As of now, the neighborhood of Tenderloin is facing severe homelessness problems. According to the 2022 San Francisco Point-in-Time (PIT) Count, District 6, which at the time of the survey encompassed the entire Tenderloin among other neighborhoods, had 3,848 homeless residents (Applied Survey Research [ASR], 2022). In addition, District 6 had the largest homeless population per resident, at approximately 1 homeless resident per 28 housed residents in 2019 (ASR, 2022; The San Francisco Redistricting Task Force, 2022). If we were to solve this problem, an attainable goal would be to match the homeless-to-housed ratio of District 10, which had the second largest homeless population in San Francisco in 2019 with approximately 1 homeless resident per 46 housed residents.[1] 
Figure 1
Line graph of homeless population per district resident for each of San Francicso’s districts
  



This problem primarily concerns the neighborhood of Tenderloin, but solving it will also require the city government’s effort. However, the Tenderloin and broader community's distrust toward government interventions may pose an obstacle to achieving this goal, with San Francisco residents rating the “overall job of local government in providing services” as a C on a scale of A (Excellent) to F (Failing) (City and County of San Francisco Controller’s Office, 2023).[2]
The larger problem of homelessness in the Tenderloin has two sides: problems in the homeless population and those in the surrounding community. Homeless residents face difficulty receiving adequate health care, housing, and poverty. Inadequate healthcare can be attributed to health complications and insurance issues. Health complications can be categorized into physical and mental conditions.[3] 
Figure 2
Fishbone diagram of deconstruction of the problem “Homelessness in Tenderloin”
  

I believe that addressing the mental health crisis in the homeless population is crucial in solving Tenderloin’s homeless problem. According to the PIT Count conducted in January 2022, many of the homeless residents face mental health issues that make it difficult for them to exit homelessness, with 52% of those surveyed reporting substance abuse, 38% reporting symptoms of PTSD, and 36% reporting psychiatric or emotional conditions (ASR, 2022). In addition, only 15% reported having access to Medi-Cal, Medicare, or Covered California, with 12% utilizing community-based health services. This implies that the majority of the homeless population has little to no access to mental healthcare. A goal state in addressing this issue would be to ensure that the homeless residents of the Tenderloin have adequate access to mental health and behavioral health services. One measure of success would be the responses to the PIT Count questions regarding the usage of government assistance and community-based services. The percentage of homeless residents currently accessing government-supported health insurance and community-based health services were both around 20% in 2017. Therefore, an appropriate initial goal would be to return these statistics to their former levels. This problem concerns the entire Tenderloin community regardless of housing circumstances. However, solving it may be difficult as 56% of the city’s homeless residents stated “Don’t want government assistance” as their reason for not receiving government assistance. 






Word Count: 498 words


________________


References
Applied Survey Research (ASR). (2022). Point-in-Time and Housing Inventory Counts. SF HSH. https://hsh.sfgov.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/2022-PIT-Count-Report-San-Francisco-Updated-8.19.22.pdf 
City and County of San Francisco Controller’s Office. (2023). City Survey 2023 Summary report of key findings from resident survey. SF.GOV. https://sf.gov/sites/default/files/2023-04/City%20Survey%202023%20Summary%20Report.pdf 
San Francisco Redistricting Task Force. (2022). 2021–2022 San Francisco Redistricting Task Force Final Report. Department of Homelessness and Supportive Housing. https://sf.gov/sites/default/files/2022-05/2021-2022%20San%20Francisco%20Redistricting%20Task%20Force%20-%20Final%20Report.pdf

These statistics are better suited to strengthen my point than the number of homeless residents in the district, as it takes into account the ratio of the homeless to housed populations. 
[2] #rightproblem: I characterized the initial state, goal state, scale, and obstacles for both the broader problem of homelessness in Tenderloin and the subproblem I chose, the mental health crisis in the homeless population of Tenderloin. In both applications, I defined the initial state with quantifiable and specific figures, identified existing trends in the problems, and defined a tractable goal state based on the initial problems. I also analysed the scale at which solutions sould be proposed, and potential obstacles to the solutions.
[3] #breakitdown: I broke the problem down with four iterations; first by the portion of the Tenderloin community in which the problem arises, second by major factors that contribute to quality of life, third by internal and external factors in healthcare problems, and finally by how the complication affects the individual. I stopped the iteration at the issue of connecting homeless residents to mental health resources, as it had means of assessing the solution’s degree of success.

text_raw:
﻿Statistical Inference
FA50


Introduction        1
Dataset        2
Analysis        2
Hypotheses        2
Summary Statistics
Conditions for Inference        2
Difference of Means Test        4
Confidence Intervals        4
Results and Conclusions        5
References        5
Reflection        5
Appendix        5






Introduction


This report explores the engagement of students in Cornerstone classes based on metrics randomly sampled from all Cornerstone classes for the 2021-2022 academic year. The dataset has a total of 240 data points consisting of 30 class sessions from each Cornerstone for each semester. The metrics are pulled directly from Forum and can be found here. The first five rows of the dataset are displayed in Appendix A. The dataset was not used in the Describing Data assignment.


The exploration of this topic is guided by the question: Do students’ performance in class differ significantly in classes during daylight hours and classes outside of daylight hours? This paper performs a difference of means test, computes effect size, and constructs confidence intervals to determine if there is sufficient evidence to suggest that students are more engaged in classes during daylight hours, possibly due to the effect of sunlight on the optical system, circadian rhythm, and perceptual systems, as suggested by Bouberki (2016). The results of this analysis will offer insight into the relationship between students’ engagement in classes and the presence of sunlight, which can potentially be used for scheduling classes to encourage engagement and facilitate active learning.  


Analysis


Hypotheses


In order to address the research question we perform a difference of means significance test, construct confidence intervals, and measure effect size. As we are analyzing student engagement, we will take class section, specifically class start time included in the section name, as our independent variable. We will take average hand raises per student as the dependent variable, as they are an indicator of active engagement in classes. Class start time is a qualitative ordinal variable, as it represents categories that classes fall into based on when the class starts and the options have a distinct chronological order. Average student hand raises is a continuous quantitative variable because it can take any given positive value on the number line and there are infinitely many possible values between any two values.[1] 


We will define classes “during daylight hours” and “outside of daylight hours” as “Daytime classes” (classes with all of class time between sunrise and sunset) and “Nighttime classes” (classes with some portion of class time before sunrise or after sunset). In San Francisco, where most first-year students live, the latest sunrise and earliest sundown are at 7:38 am and 4:50 pm. Therefore, the subgroups “classes during daylight hours” and “classes after sundown” are divided into “classes starting after or at 7:38 am and at and before 3:20 pm” and “classes starting before 7:38 am or after 3:20 pm.” Because all classes in the sample start on the hour, the conditions for these subgroups can be reframed as “classes starting after 7:00 am and at or before 3:00 pm” and “classes starting at or before 7:00 am or after 3:00 pm.”


The significance level is set to the default ( ) because neither Type I nor Type II errors have significant adverse effects. Let us clearly define our hypotheses before proceeding:
* : There is no difference in average student hand raises between daytime classes and nighttime classes ()
* : There is a difference in average student hand raises between daytime classes and nighttime classes ()


Note that although we predict that students are more engaged in classes during daylight hours, the test is 2-tailed because we do not have sufficient reason to anticipate a difference in only one direction. The following sections describe how the hypothesis test was executed.


Summary Statistics 


The dataset was read into a local Python environment using the pandas package for statistical analysis. The first step to test the hypothesis is to examine the descriptive statistics for the subgroups defined above. 


The relevant summary statistics for each subgroup are shown in Table 1, and the code to obtain them is included in Appendix B. The mode is omitted as almost all entries in both subgroups are unique and it is not informative. Based on the descriptive statistics in Table 1 and the histograms for each group shown in Figures 1 and 2, it can be inferred that the two subgroups are relatively similar in terms of spread. This is evident from the similar values for range, standard deviation, and skewness and the similar shape pf the histograms. However, the average student hand raises in nighttime classes are slightly higher, as seen from the higher mean and median.[2][3]


Table 1: Summary statistics for average student hand raises per class for the sample groups daytime classes (classes starting after 7:00 am and at or before 3:00 pm) and nighttime classes (classes starting at or before 7:00 am and after 3:00 pm). 
	



	Daytime classes (day_classes)
	Nighttime classes (night_classes)
	Count
	5
	
	Mean
	
	
	Median
	
	
	Range
	
	
	Standard Deviation
	
	
	Skewness
	
	
	

  
Figure 1: Histogram representing distribution of average student hand raises in daytime classes (classes starting after 7:00 am and at or before 3:00 pm)
	  
Figure 2: Histogram representing distribution of average student hand raises in nighttime classes (classes starting at or before 7:00 am and after 3:00 pm)
	



Conditions for Inference 


Because the population standard deviation is unknown and we are estimating the standard error using the sample standard deviation, we will use the t-distribution for inferences. To see if this choice is appropriate we must evaluate the sample data on the following conditions:
   * Random sampling - The data points in the dataset are randomly sampled from metrics provided by Forum.
   * Independent observations - As there are 49 sections in the sample as seen in Appendix C and each section has 25 sessions each, there were at least 1225 class sessions in the 2021-2022 academic year. Therefore, the dataset will be less than 10% of the population if the population spans at least two academic years. Because the population we are making inferences about is all past and future Cornerstone classes, the sample size will be 5-10% of the population.
   * Normality of distribution or large sample size - The size of both subgroups is sufficiently large (,). However, the sample data is strongly skewed to the right (, )


Assuming that the sample data meets these conditions allows for the application of the central limit theorem, which ensures the normality of the sampling distribution. This forms the foundation of the t-test as it relies on the approximate normality of the sampling distribution. However, the strong positive skewness in the samples causes an underestimation of the standard error, which in turn causes an overestimation of the p-vale.[4]


Difference of Means Test


We perform a difference of means test to assess statistical significance. To do so, we first compute the standard error for the difference in means using the formula  We then use this value to calculate the t-score as . For the degrees of freedom used in computing the p-value, we use a conservative estimate of. The calculations in Python can be found in Appendix C. 


The T-score of 0.978 means that the difference in means between the two subgroups is 0.978 standard errors. The two-tailed p-value obtained from this shows a 0.333 probability of obtaining a difference of means as extreme as the observed value under the assumption that the null hypothesis is true. In other words, it shows a 0.333 probability of incorrectly rejecting the null.[5] As it is greater than our significance level of 0.05, we fail to reject the null hypothesis that there is no difference in average student hand raises between daytime classes and nighttime classes. It should also be noted that while the positive skewness of the samples causes an overestimation of the p-value, the obtained value is greater than the significance level by an order of magnitude. From this, it can be concluded that the overestimation is not likely to have impacted the failure to reject the null.[6]


Effect Size


We use Cohen’s d as a measure of effect size to assess practical significance. This is given by the formula , where . The calculations in Python can be found in Appendix C. 


The resulting Cohen’s d is 0.164 (<0.20) which is interpreted as a small effect size. Thus, from the p-value and effect size, it can be concluded that there is no difference in the average number of student hand raises in daytime and nighttime classes.


Confidence Intervals
We can conduct further analysis of the data by constructing confidence intervals for the mean of average student hand raises in each subgroup. We set the confidence level to the default 95% to correspond with the previously used significance level of 0.05 for the same reasons we set the significance level. The conditions for constructing confidence intervals based on the t-distribution are the same as those for performing a t-test. 


The confidence interval for each subgroup can be obtained from the formula , where . The calculations in Python can be found in Appendix C. The t-score corresponding to a 95% confidence level is 1.972. The resulting 95% confidence intervals, rounded to 3 decimal places, are:
      * Daytime classes: [3.834, 4.467]
      * Nighttime classes: [3.85, 5.19]


For each of these intervals, we can be 95% confident that the true population mean for each group falls into the respective range stated in the intervals. In other words, if we repeat the sampling process and compute confidence intervals for each sample, 95% of the intervals will capture the true population mean. Because the intervals overlap, we can infer that there is no difference in average student hand raises between the two subgroups. This was highly expected, as the difference of means test yielded results that favor the null hypothesis.[7]  


Results and Conclusions


This report assessed the levels of student engagement in Cornerstone classes measured by average hand raises per student based on a random sample of classes taught in the 2021-2022 academic year. The tests for statistical and practical significance (p=0.333, d=0.164) and overlapping confidence intervals for each subgroup ([3.83, 4.47], [3.85, 5.19]) provided evidence that there is no significant difference in student engagement between classes during daylight hours and classes outside of daylight hours.


This conclusion is inductive because this inference uses an analysis of a sample of 240 Cornerstone classes taken by M25s in the academic year 2021-2022 to make generalizations about all Cornerstone classes, including those taught in academic years before and after 2021-2022. It draws the conclusion that students do not have a different average number of hand raises for Cornerstone classes during daylight hours and classes outside of daylight hours. It is a strong enumerative inductive argument as it has a large sample size of 240, is based on a difference of means test with relatively narrow confidence intervals, and fails to reject the null hypothesis based on an appropriate significance level. However, it should be noted that although the sample size is sufficiently large, this does not fully compensate for the fact that is strongly skewed. Therefore, the induction is not very reliable even though the data is randomly and independently sampled. The reliability of the inductive reasoning can be increased by using a larger sample size to further mitigate the effects of skewness.[8]


References


Minerva University. (n.d.). Cornerstone Metrics [Data set]. https://drive.google.com/file/d/1d0ZbjnRfLotZQ61jIxG98s7tsRLzIKvE/view?usp=sharing
Python Software Foundation. (n.d.). Datetime - basic date and time types. Python documentation. https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html 
The SciPy community. (n.d.). Scipy.stats.skew. scipy.stats.skew - SciPy v1.11.4 Manual. https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.stats.skew.html 


Shishegar, N., & Boubekri, M. (2016). Natural light and productivity: Analyzing the impacts of daylighting on students’ and workers’ health and Alertness. International Journal of Advances in Chemical Engineering and Biological Sciences, 3(1). https://doi.org/10.15242/ijacebs.ae0416104  


Reflection


Testing


Besides manually computing the p-value using Python, I used a library package function, scipy.stats.ttest_ind_from_stats() to ensure that my calculations are correct. The results are slightly different because I used an absolute value for the t-score and the library function uses a different formula for calculating degrees of freedom. However, the results are aligned closely enough for me to infer that my results are accurate.


Using Feedback


I incorporated feedback I received on the Describing Data assignment to improve my application of  #descriptivestats. I was careful to use the correct formula for sample standard deviation with the denominator n-1. I also computed a measure of skewness using the third standardized moment formula, defined as (insert latex). To do this I searched how to compute skewness using the scipy package, and in the process learned the formal definition of skewness and how to interpret g. The documentation I used is listed in References.


Acknowledgments


I used Grammarly.com to proofread the final draft of this report. Consulted external resources are listed in References. I relied on official documentation for Python and library packages in my code. I also acknowledge the valuable feedback from my roommate, Lotte, my close friend Raphael from M24, and my FA50 TAs who grade classes. 


Appendix[9][10]


The full .py file and the data can be accessed in the zipped folder submitted as a secondary file.




Appendix A: Import, Analyze, and Visualize Data

Appendix B: Examine the Subgroups
# descriptive statistics for each subgroup

# function to extract time from section name
def check_time(section):
  # extract time information
  time = str(section).split('@')[1].split()[0]
  # convert string to datetime object

  # extract hour in 24-hour format

  # convert to integer
  result_integer = int(hour_24_format)
  return result_integer


# add new column with class start time to dataframe

# divide data into subgroups

night_classes = df[(df['section_time'] <= 7) | (df['section_time'] > 15)]

Appendix C: Difference of Means Test 
# count number of unique sections to assess independent sampling condition
num_sections = len(df.groupby(['course_id', 'section']).size().reset_index().rename(columns={0: ''}))
print(f"Number of sections in sample: {num_sections}")

# name variables for calculations
n1 = stats_df['Daytime Classes'][0]
n2 = stats_df['Nighttime Classes'][0]
x1 = stats_df['Daytime Classes'][1]
x2 = stats_df['Nighttime Classes'][1]
s1 = stats_df['Daytime Classes'][4]
s2 = stats_df['Nighttime Classes'][4]

# calculate standard error for difference of means, t-score, and p-value for difference of means test
# code adapted from FA50 session 23
# calculate standard error for difference of means test and t-score
SEdiff = np.sqrt(s1 ** 2 / n1 + s2 ** 2 / n2)
Tscore = np.abs((x1 - x2)) / SEdiff
df = min(n1, n2) - 1
# calculate p-value
pvalue = 2 * stats.t.cdf(-Tscore, df)
print(f"\nT-score: {Tscore:.3f} \np-value: {pvalue:.3f} \n")

# check results against library function
scipy_tscore, scipy_pvalue = stats.ttest_ind_from_stats(x1, s1, n1, x2, s2, n2)
print(f"T-score calculated with scipy: {scipy_tscore:.3f} \np-value calculated with scipy: {scipy_pvalue:.3f}")

# calculate pooled standard deviation and Cohen's d
SDpooled = np.sqrt((s1 ** 2 * (n1 - 1) + s2 ** 2 * (n2 - 1)) / (n1 + n2 - 2))
Cohensd = (x2 - x1) / SDpooled
print(f"Cohen's d: {Cohensd:.3f} \n")
	  



Appendix D: Confidence Intervals 
# calculate critical t-score and standard error and confidence intervals for each subgroup
t = stats.t.ppf(0.975, n1-1)
print(f"Threshold t value for 95% confidence = {t:.3f}")
SE1 = s1/n1**0.5

SE2 = s2/n2**0.5
print(f"Confidence interval for nighttime classes: [{x2-t*SE2:.3f}, {x2+t*SE2:.3f}]")
